# Hierarchical Manifold-Constrained Normalizing Flows for High-Fidelity 3D Point Cloud Generation

**Authors:**  
*Julian V. Thorne, Elena R. Sterling, and Marcus P. Halloway*  
*Department of Computer Science, Institute for Advanced Geometric Learning, Zurich*

### Abstract
Generative modeling of 3D point clouds remains a fundamental challenge in computer vision due to the permutation invariance of unordered sets and the complexity of underlying geometric manifolds. Existing methods, such as Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) and Variational Autoencoders (VAEs), often suffer from mode collapse or blurry point distributions. In this work, we propose **PointFlow-HMC**, a novel framework that integrates hierarchical normalizing flows with manifold-constrained latent spaces to model the probability density of 3D point clouds. By decomposing the point generation process into a global geometric structure and local surface detail refinement, our method ensures structural integrity while capturing high-frequency geometric features. We introduce a bi-directional projection mechanism that maps point distributions onto a learned latent manifold, effectively regularizing the training objective. Extensive experiments on the ShapeNet and ModelNet40 datasets demonstrate that PointFlow-HMC significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art generative models in terms of Chamfer Distance and Earth Mover’s Distance, achieving a 12% improvement in structural fidelity while maintaining computational efficiency.

### Keywords
Point Clouds, Normalizing Flows, Generative Modeling, Geometric Deep Learning, Manifold Learning

---

### Introduction
The proliferation of 3D sensing technologies, including LiDAR and depth-augmented RGB cameras, has necessitated robust algorithms for the generation and synthesis of point cloud data. Unlike images, which possess a rigid grid structure, point clouds are unordered sets of points in $\mathbb{R}^3$, complicating the application of traditional convolution-based generative models [1]. While recent advancements in deep generative models have made strides, the inherent sparsity and irregular sampling of 3D data remain persistent obstacles for high-fidelity reconstruction [2].

Normalizing flows (NFs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for density estimation by learning a diffeomorphism between a simple base distribution and a complex target distribution through a sequence of invertible transformations [3]. However, direct application of NFs to point clouds often results in "point dispersion," where the model fails to capture the sharp boundaries of 3D objects. This occurs because the standard flow formulation does not explicitly enforce the geometric priors inherent in physical objects.

In this paper, we propose PointFlow-HMC, a framework that addresses these limitations by introducing hierarchical manifold constraints. Our approach operates on two levels: first, a global structure latent space is learned to capture the coarse geometry; second, a local flow layer iteratively refines the point density to satisfy surface-level constraints. This dual-layer architecture not only stabilizes training but also enables the generation of high-resolution point clouds that adhere to manifold assumptions. We provide empirical evidence that our method effectively balances structural coherence with local geometric detail.

---

### Related Work
Generative modeling for point clouds has evolved from early attempts using PointGAN [4], which utilized adversarial training to map noise vectors to point coordinates. While successful in generating basic shapes, GAN-based approaches are notoriously difficult to train and prone to mode collapse. Subsequently, VAE-based models such as PointNetAE [5] introduced latent space representations, yet these models frequently produce over-smoothed surfaces due to the use of variational inference objectives that prioritize mean squared error.

More recently, normalizing flows have been explored in the context of 3D data. The original PointFlow [6] architecture represented point clouds as a distribution of points conditioned on a global shape latent variable. However, this approach treats points as conditionally independent, neglecting the spatial correlations between adjacent points. Other related works, such as Graph-based Flows [7] and Diffusion-based point generation [8], have attempted to model spatial dependencies. Nevertheless, these methods often incur prohibitive computational costs as the number of points increases, scaling poorly to high-density datasets. Our approach differentiates itself by incorporating hierarchical constraints that reduce the effective dimensionality of the flow, thereby improving both performance and efficiency.

---

### Background and Preliminaries
A point cloud is defined as a set $\mathcal{P} = \{x_i\}_{i=1}^N$, where each $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^3$. The objective of generative modeling is to learn the underlying density $p(\mathcal{P})$. Normalizing flows define a sequence of invertible mappings $f = f_k \circ f_{k-1} \circ \dots \circ f_1$. Given a latent variable $z \sim p_z(z)$, the generated point cloud is $x = f(z)$. The log-likelihood of the data is given by the change of variables formula:
\begin{equation}
\log p(x) = \log p_z(f^{-1}(x)) + \sum_{k=1}^K \log \left| \det \left( \frac{\partial f_k}{\partial z_{k-1}} \right) \right|
\end{equation}
where $z_k = f_k(z_{k-1})$. In our context, we aim to augment this density estimation with a manifold projection operator $\Pi_{\mathcal{M}}$, which maps generated points to a learned 3D manifold $\mathcal{M}$, ensuring that $\Pi_{\mathcal{M}}(x) \approx x$. This ensures the generated points remain on the surface of the underlying geometry.

---

### Methodology
PointFlow-HMC employs a hierarchical architecture consisting of a Structural Encoder and a Refinement Flow.

#### Structural Encoder
We represent the point cloud as a hierarchical structure. An input $\mathcal{P}$ is passed through a PointNet++ backbone [9] to extract a global shape descriptor $z_g$. This descriptor acts as the conditioning variable for the flow layers.

#### Refinement Flow
To capture local geometric details, we implement a flow network based on RealNVP [10] blocks, augmented with a manifold projection layer. The transformation is defined as:
\begin{equation}
y_i = \phi(x_i; z_g) + \lambda \cdot \nabla_x \Psi(x_i)
\end{equation}
where $\Psi$ is a learned distance field function representing the surface manifold, and $\lambda$ is a learnable scaling parameter. This ensures that the generated point density is concentrated near the surface.

#### Algorithm 1: PointFlow-HMC Training
```python
Input: Point cloud dataset D, Flow layers K, Manifold objective weight lambda
Output: Optimized parameters Theta
1: Initialize network parameters Theta
2: For each batch P in D:
3:    Compute global latent z_g = Encoder(P)
4:    Compute local flow z_k = Flow(P, z_g)
5:    Compute manifold projection error L_m = ||dist(P, M)||
6:    Compute NLL loss L_nll = -log(p(P))
7:    Update Theta via gradient descent on (L_nll + alpha * L_m)
8: Return Theta
```

---

### Theoretical Analysis
The complexity of our method is dominated by the flow transformations. Given $N$ points and $K$ layers, the computational complexity is $O(N \cdot K)$, which is linear with respect to the number of points. This provides a significant advantage over transformer-based generative models which scale quadratically $O(N^2)$. 

We hypothesize that the manifold projection layer acts as a regularizer, restricting the flow's exploration to the 3D surface manifold $\mathcal{M}$. Let $J$ be the Jacobian of the transformation $f$. Our claim is that by constraining $\text{tr}(J^T J)$ via $\Psi$, we mitigate the gradient explosion typically associated with deep flows in high-dimensional spaces.

---

### Experiments

#### Datasets and Setup
We evaluate our model on the ShapeNet [11] dataset, specifically the "Airplane" and "Chair" subsets, containing approximately 10,000 models. We follow standard evaluation protocols using Chamfer Distance (CD) and Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) [12].

#### Baselines
We compare against: (1) PointGAN [4], (2) r-GAN [13], (3) AtlasNet [14], (4) PointFlow [6], and (5) PC-Diffusion [8].

#### Results
Table 1 shows the performance comparison across categories.

| Model | CD (Airplane) | CD (Chair) | EMD (Airplane) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| PointGAN | 0.024 | 0.038 | 0.15 |
| r-GAN | 0.021 | 0.035 | 0.14 |
| AtlasNet | 0.018 | 0.031 | 0.12 |
| PointFlow | 0.015 | 0.028 | 0.10 |
| **PointFlow-HMC** | **0.012** | **0.024** | **0.08** |

---

### Ablation Study
To evaluate the impact of the manifold constraint and the hierarchical structure, we performed an ablation study (Table 2).

| Variant | CD | Improvement |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Full Model | 0.024 | - |
| w/o Manifold Projection | 0.029 | +20% |
| w/o Hierarchical Structure | 0.033 | +37% |

The results indicate that the manifold projection is critical for capturing surface-level detail, whereas the hierarchy provides the structural stability.

---

### Discussion
The empirical performance of PointFlow-HMC confirms the efficacy of integrating manifold constraints into normalizing flows. By explicitly modeling the distance field of the target objects, the model avoids the "leaky" point distributions characteristic of standard NFs. Furthermore, the hierarchical decomposition allows the model to handle diverse object topologies effectively, as the global encoder captures the coarse shape while the flows refine local density. 

We observe that the choice of the manifold projection parameter $\lambda$ is non-trivial. If $\lambda$ is too high, the model experiences gradient instability; if too low, the manifold constraint is insufficient. Our adaptive scheduler for $\lambda$ proved essential for convergence. These findings suggest that the integration of geometric priors—rather than purely statistical objectives—is the next frontier in generative 3D modeling.

The computational efficiency of our method is also noteworthy. By using linear-time flow blocks, we achieve training speeds approximately 1.5x faster than diffusion-based models on identical hardware. This makes our framework suitable for real-time 3D synthesis applications, such as VR/AR content generation.

---

### Limitations and Future Work
Despite its strengths, PointFlow-HMC has limitations. First, the model assumes a fixed number of points $N$. Future work will focus on extending the architecture to handle variable-density point clouds using adaptive sampling techniques. Second, the reliance on the encoder architecture for the hierarchical conditioning means that the quality of the generated point cloud is bounded by the encoder’s ability to compress complex geometries.

Third, while we addressed the "leaking" issue, modeling thin-walled structures (e.g., chair legs or airplane wings) remains challenging. Future research will explore multi-scale flow architectures, where different scales of the flow address different frequency bands of the geometric data, potentially allowing for the generation of finer geometric features.

Finally, we intend to investigate the generalization of this framework to non-rigid deformations, which would be essential for animating generated 3D objects. Integrating temporal consistency through latent space trajectories could lead to a robust system for dynamic 3D generation.

---

### Conclusion
We have presented PointFlow-HMC, a hierarchical normalizing flow model designed for 3D point cloud generation. By introducing a manifold-constrained objective, we have successfully addressed the inherent difficulties of generating unordered geometric data. Our experiments validate that the proposed approach improves structural fidelity and density estimation compared to state-of-the-art baselines. We believe this research provides a foundation for more complex and geometrically grounded generative models in the field of 3D computer vision.

---

# Cross-Modal Manifold Alignment for Low-Resource Automatic Speech Recognition

**Authors:**  
Dr. Elena R. Vance¹, Dr. Marcus T. Thorne², Dr. S. K. Gupta¹  
¹Department of Computer Science, Institute of Advanced Technology  
²Speech Intelligence Lab, Neural Systems Research Group  

---

### Abstract
Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) performance is significantly hindered in low-resource settings due to the scarcity of transcribed audio data. While self-supervised learning (SSL) frameworks have demonstrated success in leveraging unlabeled data, current methodologies often suffer from feature misalignment when transitioning from high-resource pre-training to low-resource fine-tuning. We propose the Cross-Modal Manifold Alignment (CMMA) framework, a novel self-supervised approach that enforces structural consistency between speech and textual latent manifolds using a Wasserstein-based contrastive loss. By regularizing the feature space during the pre-training phase, CMMA improves the model’s robustness to phonetic variability and noise in under-represented linguistic domains. Our experimental results on the Common Voice and BABEL datasets demonstrate that CMMA achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming established SSL baselines by an average of 12.4% Word Error Rate (WER) reduction in low-resource regimes. We further provide theoretical insights into the convergence properties of the manifold alignment mechanism.

**Keywords:** Self-Supervised Learning, Speech Recognition, Manifold Alignment, Low-Resource Learning, Contrastive Representation Learning.

---

### 1. Introduction
The advent of deep learning has revolutionized Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR), with models now achieving near-human parity on resource-rich languages such as English. However, this success is largely predicated on the availability of thousands of hours of human-transcribed audio [1]. For the majority of the world’s languages, which remain "low-resource," the lack of paired audio-text data poses a fundamental bottleneck [2]. Recent advancements in self-supervised learning (SSL), specifically masked acoustic modeling and contrastive predictive coding, have enabled models to learn meaningful representations from massive quantities of raw, unlabeled speech [3, 4].

Despite these successes, existing SSL frameworks often operate solely on acoustic features, failing to capture the underlying phonetic manifold structure that is shared across modalities [5, 6]. When these models are fine-tuned on limited labeled data, the inherent feature space often experiences significant drift, leading to catastrophic forgetting of pre-trained acoustic priors [7]. Furthermore, the lack of explicit alignment between the acoustic embedding space and the linguistic space limits the model’s ability to generalize to unseen speakers or acoustic environments [8].

To address these challenges, we introduce the Cross-Modal Manifold Alignment (CMMA) framework. Unlike prior works that rely exclusively on temporal masking or autoregressive predictive coding, CMMA incorporates a cross-modal manifold regularization term that minimizes the Wasserstein distance between the distributions of acoustic segments and their corresponding phonetic units in a shared latent space [9, 10]. Our contributions are threefold:
1. We propose a novel contrastive alignment loss that integrates manifold regularization into the SSL objective.
2. We present a theoretical analysis showing that our alignment objective effectively reduces the generalization error bound in low-resource downstream tasks.
3. We conduct extensive empirical evaluations across diverse low-resource datasets, demonstrating superior performance against state-of-the-art baselines.

---

### 2. Related Work
The landscape of ASR has shifted from supervised end-to-end models [11, 12] to SSL-based pre-training. Frameworks such as wav2vec 2.0 [13] and HuBERT [14] have established the paradigm of pre-training on raw audio, utilizing latent representations derived from predictive coding. These models effectively utilize unlabeled data but often require fine-tuning regimes that are highly sensitive to the distribution of the target language [15, 16].

Recent literature on cross-modal learning, particularly in vision-language models like CLIP [17], has demonstrated the benefits of contrastive learning. Adapting these techniques to speech, researchers have attempted to align audio with character embeddings [18, 19]. However, these approaches often assume high-quality alignments, which are seldom available in low-resource scenarios [20].

Manifold learning techniques, such as Laplacian Eigenmaps and t-SNE, have historically been used for dimensionality reduction [21]. In deep learning, manifold regularization has been applied to semi-supervised classification [22]. Our approach builds upon these foundations by explicitly enforcing a geometric correspondence between acoustic and linguistic manifolds, addressing the alignment issue in ways that contrastive learning alone cannot achieve [23, 24, 25].

---

### 3. Background / Preliminaries
Let $\mathcal{X}$ be the space of raw acoustic signals and $\mathcal{Y}$ be the space of corresponding phoneme sequences. We consider a latent embedding space $\mathcal{Z} \subset \mathbb{R}^d$. Given an input $x \in \mathcal{X}$, an encoder $f_\theta: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Z}$ maps the input into a latent representation $z = f_\theta(x)$.

The objective of self-supervised speech recognition is to minimize a contrastive loss $\mathcal{L}_{SSL}$ defined over unlabeled speech:
\begin{equation}
\mathcal{L}_{SSL} = -\log \frac{\exp(\text{sim}(z_i, z_j)/\tau)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp(\text{sim}(z_i, z_k)/\tau)}
\end{equation}
where $\text{sim}(\cdot, \cdot)$ is the cosine similarity and $\tau$ is the temperature hyperparameter. In low-resource settings, we are limited to a small dataset $\mathcal{D}_L = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^N$, where $N \ll 10^3$.

---

### 4. Methodology / Proposed Approach
CMMA introduces a dual-branch architecture. The encoder $f_\theta$ processes acoustic input $x$, while a light-weight embedding module $g_\phi$ processes phonetic tokens $y$. We define the Manifold Alignment Loss $\mathcal{L}_{MA}$ using the 2-Wasserstein distance:
\begin{equation}
\mathcal{L}_{MA} = W_2^2(\mathbb{P}_z, \mathbb{P}_y) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Gamma(\mathbb{P}_z, \mathbb{P}_y)} \int_{\mathcal{Z} \times \mathcal{Z}} \|z - y\|^2 d\gamma(z, y)
\end{equation}
The total training objective becomes $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{SSL} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{MA}$, where $\lambda$ is a weighting coefficient.

#### 4.1 Algorithmic Procedure
The algorithm performs iterative updates to $f_\theta$ and $g_\phi$.

**Algorithm 1: CMMA Training**
1. Initialize encoders $f_\theta, g_\phi$ with random weights.
2. For each mini-batch $(X, Y)$:
   - Compute latent representations $Z = f_\theta(X)$ and $E = g_\phi(Y)$.
   - Compute $\mathcal{L}_{SSL}$ via negative sampling.
   - Compute $\mathcal{L}_{MA}$ using the Sinkhorn iteration for $W_2$ estimation.
   - Update $\theta, \phi$ using gradient descent: $\nabla_{\theta, \phi} (\mathcal{L}_{SSL} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{MA})$.
3. Return converged parameters $\theta$.

Computational complexity is dominated by the Sinkhorn operator, $O(K \cdot N^2)$, where $K$ is the number of iterations and $N$ is batch size.

---

### 5. Theoretical Analysis
We claim that minimizing $\mathcal{L}_{MA}$ reduces the generalization error. Let $\hat{R}(f)$ be the empirical risk and $R(f)$ the true risk. By the VC-dimension theory applied to manifold embeddings, the difference $|R(f) - \hat{R}(f)|$ is bounded by the alignment error between the empirical distributions of latent features.

**Theorem 1.** *Given an alignment margin $\epsilon$, the generalization error of the CMMA encoder $f_\theta$ satisfies:*
\begin{equation}
\mathbb{E}[R(f_\theta)] \leq \hat{R}(f_\theta) + C \sqrt{\frac{d_{MA}}{N}}
\end{equation}
where $d_{MA}$ represents the Wasserstein-based manifold discrepancy, and $C$ is a constant related to the Lipschitz continuity of the encoder. This ensures that even with limited labeled data, the model maintains a lower bound on performance compared to standard SSL models.

---

### 6. Experiments
#### 6.1 Datasets
We evaluate on the Common Voice (Bulgarian, Lithuanian, and Turkish subsets) and the BABEL dataset (Pashto and Swahili subsets). These datasets range from 10 to 50 hours of audio.

#### 6.2 Baselines
We compare CMMA against: (1) Supervised Wav2Vec, (2) HuBERT, (3) XLSR-53, (4) CPC, and (5) Discrete-SSL.

**Table 1: WER Comparison on Low-Resource Datasets**
| Method | Bulgarian | Turkish | Pashto | Swahili |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Supervised | 28.4 | 31.2 | 45.6 | 38.9 |
| HuBERT | 22.1 | 25.4 | 38.2 | 32.1 |
| XLSR-53 | 21.8 | 24.8 | 37.9 | 31.5 |
| **CMMA (Ours)** | **18.9** | **21.5** | **33.1** | **28.4** |

---

### 7. Ablation Study
We analyze the impact of $\lambda$ and the necessity of the manifold loss.

**Table 2: Ablation of Components**
| Component | WER (Avg) | Improvement |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Baseline (SSL) | 32.4 | - |
| Baseline + $\mathcal{L}_{MA}$ | 28.9 | 3.5% |
| Baseline + $\mathcal{L}_{MA}$ + Weighting | 27.5 | 4.9% |

---

### 8. Discussion / Insights
The experimental results underscore the importance of geometric alignment in low-resource speech recognition. While standard SSL methods excel at learning spectral features, they often struggle with the semantic granularity required for low-resource languages. CMMA effectively bridges this gap by enforcing structural consistency.

We observe that as the size of the pre-training data increases, the marginal benefit of $\mathcal{L}_{MA}$ diminishes slightly. This is expected, as standard SSL objectives implicitly capture the manifold structure when the volume of data is sufficiently high. However, in the 10-50 hour regime, CMMA remains significantly more robust.

The computational trade-off is manageable. Although the Sinkhorn iteration adds a constant factor to training time, the convergence rate is accelerated, requiring approximately 15% fewer epochs compared to baseline HuBERT to reach parity.

---

### 9. Limitations & Future Work
While effective, CMMA assumes that phonetic distributions are relatively well-behaved. In languages with complex tonal structures or extreme dialectal variations, the Wasserstein distance estimation may become unstable.

Future work will investigate the use of variational manifold alignment to account for stochastic latent structures. Additionally, extending CMMA to multilingual multi-task settings remains a promising avenue for improving cross-lingual transferability.

Finally, we plan to address the reliance on token-level alignments during the initial stages by implementing a self-correcting alignment phase that dynamically updates the target phonetic manifold as training progresses.

---

### 10. Conclusion
This paper presented CMMA, an approach for low-resource ASR using cross-modal manifold alignment. By minimizing the Wasserstein distance between acoustic and phonetic manifolds, our method improves representation quality in scenarios where labeled data is scarce. Theoretical results confirm a tighter generalization bound, and experimental evaluations substantiate the method’s efficacy across multiple languages.

---

### 11. References
[1] A. Baevski, et al., "wav2vec 2.0: A framework for self-supervised learning of speech representations," in Proc. NeurIPS, 2020, pp. 12449-12460.  
[2] K. Cho, et al., "Learning phrase representations using RNN encoder-decoder for statistical machine translation," in Proc. EMNLP, 2014, pp. 1724-1734.  
[3] W. N. Hsu, et al., "HuBERT: Self-supervised speech representation learning by masked prediction of hidden units," in Proc. IEEE TASLP, 2021, pp. 1599-1610.  
[4] A. van den Oord, et al., "Representation learning with contrastive predictive coding," in Proc. NeurIPS, 2018, pp. 9191-9201.  
[5] S. Schneider, et al., "wav2vec: Unsupervised pre-training for speech recognition," in Proc. Interspeech, 2019, pp. 3465-3469.  
[6] T. T. Nguyen, et al., "Deep manifold learning for robust speech processing," in Proc. ICASSP, 2022, pp. 450-455.  
[7] J. R. Smith, "Catastrophic forgetting in low-resource ASR," in Proc. ACL, 2021, pp. 88-95.  
[8] M. P. Varma, et al., "Acoustic-phonetic feature alignment in deep networks," in Proc. ICLR, 2023, pp. 120-135.  
[9] C. Villani, *Optimal Transport: Old and New*, Springer, 2009.  
[10] M. Cuturi, "Sinkhorn distances: Lightspeed computation of optimal transport," in Proc. NeurIPS, 2013, pp. 2292-2300.  
[11] W. Chan, et al., "Listen, attend and spell: A neural network for fast encoder-decoder speech recognition," in Proc. ICASSP, 2016, pp. 4960-4964.  
[12] D. Amodei, et al., "Deep speech 2: End-to-end speech recognition in English and Mandarin," in Proc. ICML, 2016, pp. 173-182.  
[13] A. Conneau, et al., "Unsupervised cross-lingual representation learning for speech recognition," in Proc. Interspeech, 2021, pp. 2426-2430.  
[14] B. Liu, et al., "Robust speech recognition via contrastive learning," in Proc. SLT, 2022, pp. 101-108.

[18] Y. Z. Wang, et al., "Contrastive cross-modal speech alignment," in Proc. ACL, 2022, pp. 4450-4460.

[24] P. J. Tan, "Geometric constraints in neural network latent spaces," in Proc. ICLR, 2022, pp. 440-455.

# Adaptive Manifold Alignment: A Topological Approach to Cross-Modal Contrastive Learning in Vision-Language Models

**Dr. Elena R. Vance**, *Department of Computer Science, Stanford University*  
**Dr. Julian K. Thorne**, *Institute for Artificial Intelligence, ETH Zurich*  
**Dr. Sarah M. Chen**, *Department of Robotics and AI, University of Toronto*

### Abstract
Current Vision-Language Pretraining (VLP) paradigms rely heavily on global contrastive objectives, such as CLIP’s InfoNCE loss, which prioritize alignment in static Euclidean spaces. However, these methods often fail to capture the underlying geometric nuances of high-dimensional feature manifolds, leading to sub-optimal cross-modal alignment in complex, multi-object scenes. We introduce **Topological Manifold Contrastive Alignment (TMCA)**, a novel framework that incorporates learnable topological priors into the contrastive training loop. By augmenting the standard projection head with a persistent homology-guided attention mechanism, TMCA forces the model to learn alignment at both the global instance level and the local structural level. Extensive experiments on MS-COCO, Flickr30k, and VQAv2 demonstrate that TMCA achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming CLIP and ALIGN while maintaining lower computational overhead during inference. We provide theoretical justifications for the convergence of our topological loss term and detail its impact on mitigating alignment saturation.

### Keywords
Contrastive Learning, Vision-Language Pretraining, Persistent Homology, Manifold Alignment, Multi-Modal Representation Learning.

---

### Introduction
The emergence of contrastive learning has revolutionized vision-language pretraining (VLP), enabling models like CLIP [1] and ALIGN [2] to achieve zero-shot transfer capabilities across diverse downstream tasks. By maximizing the mutual information between image and text embeddings in a shared latent space, these architectures demonstrate significant robustness to distributional shifts [3, 4]. Despite these advancements, standard contrastive objectives operate under the implicit assumption that the data manifolds are globally isotropic, failing to account for the intrinsic local connectivity and topological constraints inherent in complex multi-modal data [5, 6, 7].

Existing methodologies, while scalable, often suffer from "alignment collapse," where the model maps distinct semantic concepts to proximate coordinates in the latent space due to a lack of structural awareness [8, 9]. As research shifts toward finer-grained vision-language alignment, the necessity of capturing local manifold structure becomes paramount [10, 11]. Recent studies have highlighted that vision models capture hierarchical visual features, whereas language models rely on recursive syntactic structures; forcing these into a flat Euclidean alignment space inevitably introduces geometric distortion [12, 13, 14].

In this paper, we propose the Topological Manifold Contrastive Alignment (TMCA) framework. TMCA transitions from global contrastive learning to a hybrid approach that integrates persistent homology—a tool from Topological Data Analysis (TDA)—directly into the contrastive objective [15, 16]. By constructing dynamic simplicial complexes in the latent space, TMCA enforces constraints that preserve the topological signature of objects across modalities [17, 18, 19]. We demonstrate that by integrating this topological prior, we can regularize the alignment process, preventing the collapse of semantic clusters and enhancing the model's capacity for fine-grained reasoning [20, 21, 22].

---

### Related Work
The foundation of modern VLP is built upon contrastive losses, specifically the InfoNCE objective [23, 24]. The CLIP architecture [1] established a baseline by utilizing large-scale image-text pairs, while subsequent improvements such as FILIP [25] and DeCLIP [26] focused on token-level alignment to refine local interactions. However, these models largely ignore the topological structure of the latent space, focusing primarily on minimizing cosine distance between embeddings [27, 28].

TDA has been recently explored in deep learning for representation stabilization. Methods such as persistent homology layers [29] have been successfully applied to image classification, where they assist in capturing global shapes, invariant to deformations [30, 31]. While traditional TDA is computationally expensive, recent approximations for simplicial complexes make it feasible for high-dimensional mini-batches [32, 33]. Our work deviates from previous applications by utilizing these tools specifically as a regularization term for multi-modal alignment, rather than a standalone feature extractor [34, 35].

---

### Background and Preliminaries
Let $\mathcal{V}$ be the space of visual embeddings and $\mathcal{L}$ be the space of linguistic embeddings. We define the encoders $E_v: \mathcal{I} \to \mathbb{R}^d$ and $E_l: \mathcal{T} \to \mathbb{R}^d$. The standard contrastive objective (InfoNCE) is defined as:

$$\mathcal{L}_{InfoNCE} = -\log \frac{\exp(\text{sim}(z_v, z_l) / \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^N \exp(\text{sim}(z_v, z_{l,j}) / \tau)}$$

where $z_v$ and $z_l$ are the normalized outputs of $E_v$ and $E_l$, and $\tau$ is the temperature hyperparameter [1, 23]. This objective assumes a uniform distribution of classes, which is rarely met in open-vocabulary datasets.

To improve this, we utilize persistent homology to extract the barcode representation of the latent space $S$. Let $K$ be a filtration constructed over the embedding batch; the persistence barcode $\mathcal{B}(K)$ captures the birth and death of topological features across the latent manifold [15]. We define the topological alignment as the Wasserstein distance between the persistence diagrams of the visual and language modalities.

---

### Methodology
TMCA introduces an adaptive projection head that outputs both a standard embedding $z$ and a topological descriptor $p$. The total loss function is defined as:

$$\mathcal{L}_{Total} = \mathcal{L}_{InfoNCE} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{Topo}(z_v, z_l)$$

where $\lambda$ balances the trade-off between standard global alignment and structural manifold fidelity.

**Algorithm 1: Topological Manifold Contrastive Alignment**
1.  **Input:** Image $I$, Text $T$, batch size $N$, hyperparams $\lambda, \tau$.
2.  **Encode:** $z_v = E_v(I), z_l = E_l(T)$.
3.  **Filtration:** Construct Vietoris-Rips complexes $\mathcal{K}_v, \mathcal{K}_l$ using $z_v, z_l$.
4.  **Persistent Homology:** Compute persistence diagrams $D_v, D_l = PH(\mathcal{K}_v, \mathcal{K}_l)$.
5.  **Compute TopoLoss:** $\mathcal{L}_{Topo} = W_p(D_v, D_l)$, where $W_p$ is the p-Wasserstein distance.
6.  **Update:** Backpropagate $\mathcal{L}_{Total}$ to encoders $E_v$ and $E_l$.

The computational complexity is $O(N^3)$ for persistent homology, but by restricting the filtration to the $k$-nearest neighbors, we achieve a practical complexity of $O(N \log N)$ per iteration, allowing for seamless integration into existing VLP pipelines.

---

### Theoretical Analysis
We claim that TMCA induces a smoother alignment mapping. Let $M_v$ and $M_l$ be the underlying manifolds for visual and text data. By minimizing $W_p(D_v, D_l)$, we minimize the bottleneck distance between the barcodes, ensuring that the homotopy types of the manifolds are preserved.

*Theorem 1:* If $E_v$ and $E_l$ map to a space where the persistence diagrams are $\epsilon$-close in Wasserstein distance, then the homeomorphism between $M_v$ and $M_l$ is maintained up to a deformation factor $\delta(\epsilon)$.

*Justification:* Since the persistence diagrams are robust to noise and perturbations in the input points, the topological objective acts as a manifold-level regularizer, preventing the "stretching" of semantic clusters that occurs in standard InfoNCE.

---

### Experiments

**Datasets:** We utilize the COCO-Captions (113k), Flickr30k (31k), and VQAv2 datasets.
**Baselines:** CLIP [1], ALIGN [2], BLIP [36], SimVLM [37], and Florence [38].

**Table 1: Zero-shot retrieval performance (Recall@1)**
| Model | COCO-Retrieval | Flickr30k | VQAv2 Acc |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CLIP | 68.2 | 81.4 | 62.1 |
| ALIGN | 69.5 | 82.1 | 63.4 |
| BLIP | 72.8 | 85.3 | 68.7 |
| SimVLM | 73.1 | 86.0 | 69.2 |
| TMCA (Ours) | **75.4** | **87.5** | **71.3** |

---

### Ablation Study
We analyze the contribution of the persistent homology module ($\mathcal{L}_{Topo}$) versus the standard alignment loss.

**Table 2: Ablation results**
| Component | Recall@1 (COCO) | Delta |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Base CLIP Loss | 68.2 | - |
| CLIP + Topo ($PH_0$) | 71.5 | +3.3 |
| CLIP + Topo ($PH_0 + PH_1$) | 75.4 | +7.2 |

The results indicate that capturing $H_1$ (loops/cycles) provides a significant performance boost over merely considering $H_0$ (connected components).

---

### Discussion
The observed improvements demonstrate that standard Euclidean alignment is insufficient for high-dimensional VLP. By forcing the latent space to respect the topological connectivity of visual objects, TMCA allows the model to better generalize across modalities. The ablation study suggests that while connected components help cluster similar objects, it is the higher-order structural information (cycles) that assists in complex scene understanding and multi-object grounding.

One intriguing insight is the efficiency-accuracy trade-off. While our topological module adds an overhead of approximately 12% in training time, the convergence speed of the overall model increases by 15% because the topological prior restricts the search space of the gradient descent, avoiding poor local minima that are prevalent in purely contrastive landscapes.

Finally, we note that the stability of the topological loss is highly sensitive to the filtration parameter $\epsilon$. Proper tuning of this parameter is essential for real-world application, though we find that a dynamic scaling factor based on the batch variance provides sufficient robustness for most datasets.

---

### Limitations and Future Work
While TMCA offers significant improvements, it remains constrained by the computational cost of persistent homology calculations for very large batch sizes. Future work will investigate hardware-accelerated persistence computation using GPU kernels to further reduce training latency. Additionally, we acknowledge that our current implementation assumes a uniform weighting of homology dimensions; developing an adaptive weighting scheme that focuses on specific topological features based on the downstream task could further optimize performance.

Moreover, the current framework is designed for static images and text. Extending TMCA to handle video-language data will require the incorporation of temporal persistent homology, which involves computing barcodes over 3D spacetime volumes. This remains a non-trivial challenge due to the exponential growth of simplicial complexes in higher dimensions.

Finally, while we have tested TMCA on standard benchmarks, its efficacy on out-of-distribution, adversarial, or low-resource linguistic datasets remains to be thoroughly quantified. We anticipate that the structural regularization provided by TMCA will offer increased robustness against noise, a hypothesis we intend to test in future research.

---

### Conclusion
We have presented Topological Manifold Contrastive Alignment (TMCA), a robust framework for integrating topological priors into vision-language pretraining. By leveraging persistent homology to enforce structural alignment in latent manifolds, we have demonstrated that TMCA achieves superior performance over traditional global contrastive methods. Our empirical results underscore the importance of topological constraints in deep representation learning.

# Variational Conservative Policy Optimization: Mitigating Distributional Shift in Offline Reinforcement Learning

**Authors:**
Elena V. Sterling, Department of Computer Science, Stanford University; Marcus J. Thorne, AI Research Division, DeepMind; Sarah A. Chen, Department of Robotics, MIT.

---

### 1. Introduction
Offline Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a paradigm-shifting approach in sequential decision-making, promising the ability to learn robust control policies from static datasets without environment interaction [1]. This capability is critical in high-stakes domains such as autonomous driving, healthcare, and industrial robotics, where exploration-based online learning is often infeasible or prohibitively expensive [2]. However, the foundational challenge in offline RL remains the severe distributional shift between the behavior policy—which generated the dataset—and the learned policy [3].

When a policy deviates from the data-generating distribution, the Q-function estimates tend to over-extrapolate on out-of-distribution (OOD) actions [4]. Traditional approaches attempt to mitigate this through explicit pessimism, typically by regularizing the policy toward the behavior policy or penalizing Q-value estimates [5]. Yet, these methods often struggle with sub-optimal demonstrations or suffer from excessive conservatism, which leads to performance degradation in complex task spaces [6].

In this paper, we introduce Variational Conservative Policy Optimization (VCPO), a novel framework that reformulates offline RL as a constrained variational inference problem. By integrating a latent-space uncertainty model directly into the policy optimization objective, we provide a mathematically rigorous mechanism to dynamically modulate conservatism. Our approach avoids the pitfalls of fixed-penalty methods by adaptively balancing policy improvement with the epistemic uncertainty of the underlying transition dynamics. Through extensive experimentation, we demonstrate that VCPO achieves state-of-the-art performance on the D4RL benchmark suite, exhibiting superior robustness to noise and multi-modal behavior patterns compared to leading baseline architectures [7, 8].

---

### 2. Related Work
The field of offline RL has bifurcated into several distinct methodologies, primarily categorized by how they handle the distributional shift problem. The first category involves policy constraints that restrict the learned policy to remain close to the behavior policy. Fujimoto et al. [9] introduced Batch-Constrained Q-learning (BCQ), which utilizes a generative model to restrict action selection. Subsequently, Kumar et al. [10] proposed Conservative Q-Learning (CQL), which adds a penalty to the Bellman operator to ensure lower-bound estimates of the Q-function.

A second category focuses on importance sampling and reweighting. Off-policy evaluation (OPE) methods, such as those proposed by Thomas et al. [11] and Liu et al. [12], have sought to correct for the discrepancy in state-action visitations. However, these techniques often suffer from high variance, particularly in long-horizon tasks, leading to unstable policy optimization.

Model-based offline RL constitutes a third, rapidly evolving branch. By learning a dynamics model of the environment, these methods allow for synthetic experience generation [13]. Kidambi et al. [14] introduced MOReL, which penalizes the reward function based on model uncertainty. Similarly, Yu et al. [15] proposed MOPO, which augments the environment with an uncertainty-aware reward penalty. While effective, these methods are often sensitive to model hallucinations [16].

Our proposed VCPO framework bridges the gap between model-free and model-based paradigms. By employing a variational approach, we treat the policy as a distribution conditioned on latent features, allowing us to quantify and penalize epistemic uncertainty without the heavy computational burden of explicit ensemble-based model learning [17, 18]. Our work builds upon the theoretical foundations of variational inference established by Blei et al. [19] and extends them to the conservative estimation of Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) as defined by Sutton and Barto [20].

---

### 3. Background / Preliminaries
We consider a standard MDP defined by the tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma, \rho_0)$, where $\mathcal{S}$ is the state space, $\mathcal{A}$ is the action space, $\mathcal{P}(s'|s,a)$ is the transition dynamics, $\mathcal{R}(s,a)$ is the reward function, $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is the discount factor, and $\rho_0$ is the initial state distribution. In the offline setting, we are provided with a fixed dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(s_i, a_i, r_i, s'_i)\}_{i=1}^N$, sampled from a behavior policy $\pi_\beta$.

The objective is to find an optimal policy $\pi^*$ that maximizes the expected return $\mathbb{E}_\pi[\sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t r_t]$. The fundamental issue, as identified by Levine et al. [21], is the discrepancy in the Bellman backup:
$$ Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathcal{R}(s,a) + \gamma \mathbb{E}_{s' \sim \mathcal{P}, a' \sim \pi} [Q^\pi(s',a')] $$
When $\pi$ is not the behavior policy $\pi_\beta$, the expectation over $a'$ leads to OOD queries on the Q-function. The standard remedy is the enforcement of a divergence constraint $\mathcal{D}_{KL}(\pi || \pi_\beta) \leq \epsilon$.

---

### 4. Methodology / Proposed Approach
VCPO operates by learning a joint representation of the transition dynamics and the value function through a variational bottleneck. We introduce a latent variable $z \sim q_\phi(z|s,a)$ that captures the stochasticity of the environment.

The objective function for the critic is defined as:
$$ J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(s,a,s') \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \frac{1}{2} (Q_\theta(s,a) - \hat{\mathcal{B}}^\pi Q_{\bar{\theta}}(s,a))^2 + \lambda \cdot \mathcal{U}(s,a) \right] $$
where $\hat{\mathcal{B}}^\pi$ is the conservative Bellman operator and $\mathcal{U}(s,a)$ is the epistemic uncertainty term derived from the variational posterior. We optimize $\mathcal{U}$ to ensure that actions with high reconstruction error receive higher penalties, preventing the policy from preferring regions of the state-action space where the data is sparse.

The policy $\pi_\psi$ is updated via:
$$ \nabla_\psi J(\psi) = \mathbb{E}_{s \sim \mathcal{D}} [ \nabla_\psi \log \pi_\psi(a|s) (Q_\theta(s,a) - \alpha \log \frac{\pi_\psi(a|s)}{\pi_\beta(a|s)}) ] $$
where $\alpha$ is a learned temperature parameter.

#### 4.1 Pseudocode
```python
# VCPO Algorithm
Initialize Q-networks Q_1, Q_2, policy pi_psi, and latent encoder q_phi
For iteration = 1 to M:
    Batch = sample_from_dataset(D)
    # Estimate Uncertainty
    z = q_phi.sample(Batch.s, Batch.a)
    U = compute_epistemic_uncertainty(z, Batch.s, Batch.a)
    # Update Critic
    target = R + gamma * min(Q_1_targ, Q_2_targ) - U
    Q_loss = MSE(Q(s,a), target)
    # Update Policy
    pi_loss = -E[Q(s,a) - alpha * log(pi/pi_beta)]
    Update parameters using Adam optimizer
```

---

### 5. Theoretical Analysis
We provide a brief justification for the convergence properties of VCPO. Let $\epsilon_{shift}$ denote the maximum distributional shift between $\pi$ and $\pi_\beta$. We claim that under the VCPO objective, the learned policy $\hat{\pi}$ satisfies:
$$ V^{\hat{\pi}}(s) \geq V^\pi(s) - \frac{2\gamma \epsilon_{shift}}{(1-\gamma)^2} \mathcal{C}(\mathcal{D}) $$
where $\mathcal{C}(\mathcal{D})$ represents the coverage of the dataset [22]. The proof follows from the application of the contraction mapping theorem on the conservative Bellman operator $\hat{\mathcal{B}}^\pi$. By penalizing the Q-value with an uncertainty term $U \propto || \nabla_a Q(s,a) ||_2$, we ensure that the optimization trajectory stays within the convex hull of the support of $\pi_\beta$.

The computational complexity of each training step is $O(N \cdot |\mathcal{A}| \cdot d)$, where $d$ is the latent dimension of the variational encoder. This compares favorably to ensemble-based methods, which typically scale at $O(N \cdot K)$, where $K$ is the number of ensemble members.

---

### 6. Experiments
We evaluate VCPO on the D4RL MuJoCo locomotion tasks, specifically the "Medium-Replay" and "Medium-Expert" datasets [7].

#### 6.1 Baselines
We compare VCPO against five prominent baselines:
1. BCQ [9] (Batch-Constrained Q-learning)
2. CQL [10] (Conservative Q-Learning)
3. BRAC [23] (Behavior Regularized Actor-Critic)
4. IQL [24] (Implicit Q-Learning)
5. MOPO [15] (Model-based Offline Policy Optimization)

#### 6.2 Results
Table 1 shows the normalized scores on the locomotion tasks.

| Dataset | BCQ | CQL | BRAC | IQL | MOPO | VCPO (Ours) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| HalfCheetah-MR | 42.1 | 44.0 | 41.9 | 47.4 | 45.2 | **49.8** |
| Hopper-MR | 54.5 | 58.2 | 52.1 | 66.2 | 59.8 | **68.4** |
| Walker2d-MR | 33.1 | 48.6 | 41.2 | 78.3 | 44.5 | **79.2** |
| Ant-MR | 38.2 | 41.0 | 39.5 | 42.1 | 43.0 | **45.6** |

Table 2 presents results on the "Medium-Expert" datasets, indicating strong performance in multi-modal trajectory scenarios.

---

### 7. Ablation Study
To understand the contribution of the variational bottleneck and the uncertainty penalty, we conducted a component ablation.

| Variant | Score (Avg) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Full VCPO | 65.8 |
| w/o Latent Encoder | 58.2 |
| w/o Uncertainty Penalty | 51.4 |
| w/o Conservative Bellman | 42.9 |

The results indicate that the uncertainty penalty is the most critical component, accounting for approximately 14% of the performance gains.

---

### 8. Discussion / Insights
The experimental results underscore the effectiveness of dynamic conservatism. Unlike CQL, which employs a static penalty over the entire state-action space, VCPO’s variational approach allows the agent to distinguish between "unknown" regions due to lack of data versus "known" sub-optimal regions. This granular control is essential for complex tasks like *Walker2d*, where simple regularization often leads to overly cautious, stiff policies.

Furthermore, our observations suggest that the latent space representation acts as a regularizer, preventing the policy from overfitting to the noise inherent in the behavior policy demonstrations. This is particularly evident in the *Medium-Replay* datasets, where high-variance, low-quality transitions are prevalent.

Finally, we observe that the computational overhead of the variational encoder is negligible compared to the benefits of faster convergence. By avoiding the need for large ensemble networks common in model-based RL, VCPO remains memory-efficient, making it suitable for deployment in resource-constrained environments.

---

### 9. Limitations & Future Work
Despite the promising results, VCPO exhibits limitations. First, the performance is highly dependent on the quality of the latent representation. In environments with highly non-stationary transitions, the encoder may fail to capture the underlying dynamics, leading to inaccurate uncertainty estimates. Future work will investigate the use of contrastive learning objectives to improve latent space robustness [25].

Second, the current formulation assumes a continuous action space. Extending VCPO to discrete action spaces or hybrid action spaces remains a non-trivial challenge, as the variational inference of latent variables is significantly different in discrete settings. Integrating Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization is a primary avenue for future research.

Third, while VCPO addresses distributional shift, it does not explicitly handle long-horizon task credit assignment issues. Incorporating hierarchical RL architectures or temporal-difference learning with eligibility traces may provide further improvements in sparse-reward environments.

---

### 10. Conclusion
We have presented Variational Conservative Policy Optimization (VCPO), a framework for offline RL that leverages variational inference to mitigate the distributional shift between behavior and learned policies. By incorporating an epistemic uncertainty-aware penalty into the Bellman operator, VCPO enables safer and more efficient policy optimization. Our empirical results validate that VCPO outperforms current state-of-the-art methods across multiple benchmarks, establishing a new foundation for robust offline reinforcement learning.

---

### 11. References
[1] S. Levine, A. Kumar, G. Tucker, and J. Fu, "Offline reinforcement learning: Tutorial, review, and perspectives on open problems," *arXiv preprint arXiv:2005.01643*, 2020.
[2] A. M. Ahmed and M. J. Collins, "Challenges in real-world deployment of reinforcement learning," in *Proc. Int. Conf. on Machine Learning (ICML)*, 2021, pp. 120-135.
[3] D. K. R. R. B. P. A. S. L. A. K. A. G. T. J. F., "Stabilizing offline reinforcement learning," in *Proc. NeurIPS*, 2020, pp. 201-215.

# Causal-Gradient Refinement: Improving Interpretability in Medical Imaging via Structural Causal Models

**Authors:** Elena V. Rostova, Marcus T. Thorne, and Julian D. Halloway  
**Affiliations:** Department of Computer Science, Institute for Advanced Medical AI, Zurich; Department of Radiology, St. Jude’s Research Hospital, Boston.

---

### Abstract
Deep learning models have achieved diagnostic performance comparable to clinical experts in various medical imaging domains. However, the "black-box" nature of these architectures hinders their integration into clinical workflows. Traditional saliency methods, such as Integrated Gradients or Grad-CAM, often produce noisy attributions that fail to differentiate between spurious correlations and causal diagnostic features. We propose **Causal-Gradient Refinement (CGR)**, a novel framework that integrates Structural Causal Models (SCMs) with gradient-based saliency maps. By enforcing a causal intervention constraint on the feature manifold, CGR suppresses non-causal gradients that contribute to model predictions without clinical significance. We demonstrate that CGR significantly improves localization accuracy on the ChestX-ray14 dataset and provides higher diagnostic fidelity as evaluated by board-certified radiologists. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in both faithfulness and stability, offering a robust mechanism for verifiable medical AI.

**Keywords:** Explainable AI, Causal Inference, Medical Imaging, Saliency Maps, Deep Learning.

---

### Introduction
The deployment of Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) in medical diagnostics is increasingly constrained by the requirement for model interpretability [1]. While saliency methods offer a visual window into model decision-making, they are frequently criticized for their lack of robustness and susceptibility to input perturbations [2]. Standard gradient-based approaches, such as Vanilla Gradients, often highlight background noise or image artifacts that do not align with pathological evidence [3].

The fundamental issue is that current attribution methods measure correlation rather than causation. In medical imaging, a model might correctly identify a pathology while relying on non-causal features—such as hospital-specific tags or patient positioning—that happen to correlate with the label in the training set [4]. This phenomenon, known as shortcut learning, poses a severe risk in clinical settings where diagnostic decisions must be grounded in physiological reality [5].

To address this, we introduce the **Causal-Gradient Refinement (CGR)** framework. CGR leverages the structural dependencies within medical images to filter out gradients that do not represent causal paths to the target variable. Unlike prior approaches that treat all high-gradient pixels as equally important, CGR applies a causal mask derived from a latent SCM, ensuring that only features with a direct causal influence on the diagnostic outcome are visualized. We provide theoretical proof that CGR satisfies the axioms of causal faithfulness and demonstrate its efficacy across multiple clinical benchmarks.

---

### Related Work
Interpretability in deep learning typically manifests through post-hoc saliency methods. Early approaches like Saliency Maps [6] and Grad-CAM [7] localized diagnostic features by backpropagating gradients to the input. However, recent literature indicates these methods can be independent of the model’s weights, raising concerns regarding their reliability [8].

Causal inference has recently emerged as a rigorous framework for improving AI robustness. Researchers have explored causal interventions in latent spaces [9], yet these methods are often computationally prohibitive for high-resolution medical imaging. Our work builds upon the foundation of Integrated Gradients (IG) [10], which satisfies the desirable property of completeness, but extends it by introducing a causal intervention layer that explicitly models the relationship between anatomical regions.

Previous studies in medical AI have attempted to incorporate domain knowledge via attention mechanisms [11] or anatomical constraints [12]. While effective, these models often require pixel-level segmentation masks during training. In contrast, CGR is an unsupervised interpretability technique that does not require additional ground-truth segmentation data, making it uniquely suited for clinical datasets where pixel-level annotations are scarce [13].

---

### Background and Preliminaries
Let $X \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ be an input medical image and $f_\theta: X \to Y$ be a deep neural network predicting diagnostic label $Y$. Standard saliency methods define the attribution $A$ as:
$$A(x) = \nabla_x f_\theta(x) \odot x$$
where $\odot$ denotes the element-wise product. We assume a Structural Causal Model (SCM) defined by the tuple $\mathcal{M} = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E}, \mathcal{P})$, where $\mathcal{V}$ are variables (latent features), $\mathcal{E}$ are exogenous noise terms, and $\mathcal{P}$ is the set of causal mechanisms [14].

We define an intervention $do(V = v)$ as the process of fixing variable $V$ to value $v$ while keeping other causal mechanisms intact [15]. In our context, we seek an attribution that reflects the causal effect of image region $r$ on the prediction $f_\theta(x)$:
$$\tau(r) = \mathbb{E}[f_\theta(x) | do(X_r = x_r)] - \mathbb{E}[f_\theta(x) | do(X_r = x'_r)]$$
where $x'_r$ is a counterfactual reference value representing the absence of pathology.

---

### Methodology: Causal-Gradient Refinement (CGR)
The CGR framework operates in three stages: latent manifold projection, causal intervention, and gradient filtering.

**1. Latent Manifold Projection:** We pass the input $X$ through a pre-trained feature extractor $E_\phi$ to obtain a latent representation $Z = E_\phi(X)$. We assume $Z$ follows a causal structure consistent with anatomical regions.

**2. Causal Intervention Layer:** We introduce an intervention function $\mathcal{I}$ that perturbs the latent variables $Z$ based on a pre-defined causal graph $\mathcal{G}$. This graph is constructed using anatomical priors (e.g., lungs influence heart size, but not vice-versa).
$$\tilde{Z} = \mathcal{I}(Z, \mathcal{G}) = \text{mask}(Z) \odot (Z - \mu_Z) + \mu_Z$$

**3. Gradient Filtering:** The final attribution $A_{CGR}$ is computed by backpropagating the prediction through the intervention layer:
$$A_{CGR} = \left\| \frac{\partial f_\theta(\mathcal{I}(E_\phi(X)))}{\partial X} \right\|_2$$

**Algorithm 1: Causal-Gradient Refinement**
1. **Input:** Image $X$, model $f_\theta$, causal graph $\mathcal{G}$.
2. **Initialize:** Define baseline $X_0$ (black image/blur).
3. **Compute:** $Z = E_\phi(X)$.
4. **Intervene:** For each region $r \in \mathcal{G}$, compute $\mathcal{I}(Z_r)$.
5. **Backpropagate:** Calculate $\nabla_X f_\theta(\mathcal{I}(Z))$.
6. **Output:** $A_{CGR}$ (Causal Saliency Map).

Complexity Analysis: The computational complexity of CGR is $O(K \cdot N)$, where $K$ is the number of causal nodes and $N$ is the number of pixels. This is linear relative to the number of regions, allowing for real-time inference on modern GPUs [16].

---

### Theoretical Analysis
**Theorem 1 (Faithfulness):** Given a causal model $\mathcal{M}$, the attribution $A_{CGR}$ is faithful to the structural causal dependencies if the intervention set satisfies the back-door criterion [17].
*Proof Sketch:* By the definition of the $do$-operator, $A_{CGR}$ isolates the variation in $f_\theta$ caused by $X_r$, effectively blocking spurious paths from non-causal confounders [18].

**Theorem 2 (Sparsity):** The gradient filtering operation in CGR induces sparsity in the attribution space, reducing the variance of the saliency map relative to standard Integrated Gradients [19].
*Justification:* The intervention layer acts as a Laplacian prior on the gradients, forcing the attribution to align with the causal graph $\mathcal{G}$ rather than high-frequency noise [20].

---

### Experiments

#### Datasets
We evaluate CGR on the **ChestX-ray14** dataset [21], containing 112,120 frontal-view X-ray images, and the **MURA** (Musculoskeletal Radiographs) dataset [22].

#### Experimental Setup
We compare CGR against five baseline methods: Vanilla Gradients (VG), Integrated Gradients (IG) [10], Grad-CAM [7], SmoothGrad [23], and LIME [24]. All models are implemented in PyTorch using an ImageNet-pretrained ResNet-50 backbone. Hyperparameters: learning rate $1e-4$, weight decay $1e-5$, and a causal intervention strength $\lambda = 0.5$.

#### Evaluation Metrics
We use **Faithfulness Correlation (FC)** and **Pointing Game Accuracy (PGA)**. PGA measures the percentage of salient peaks falling within the radiologist-annotated bounding boxes of pathology [25].

**Table 1: Performance Comparison on ChestX-ray14**
| Method | Faithfulness (FC) | Pointing Accuracy (PGA) | Stability (SSIM) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Vanilla Gradients | 0.42 | 58.2% | 0.31 |
| Integrated Gradients | 0.61 | 67.4% | 0.65 |
| Grad-CAM | 0.58 | 64.1% | 0.59 |
| SmoothGrad | 0.64 | 69.8% | 0.68 |
| LIME | 0.55 | 61.2% | 0.44 |
| **CGR (Ours)** | **0.78** | **76.5%** | **0.82** |

---

### Ablation Study
We analyze the contribution of the intervention layer and the causal graph structure.

**Table 2: Ablation Study Results**
| Configuration | Pointing Accuracy (PGA) | Mean Saliency Entropy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Full CGR | 76.5% | 1.22 |
| Without Causal Graph | 68.2% | 1.85 |
| Without Intervention | 62.4% | 2.10 |
| Random Graph | 59.1% | 2.45 |

The data confirms that the causal graph provides a significant uplift in localization precision, suggesting that anatomical constraints are essential for medical saliency.

---

### Discussion
The empirical results demonstrate that CGR effectively bridges the gap between deep learning predictions and clinical interpretability. By incorporating a causal graph, the model learns to ignore non-causal artifacts—such as text markers on X-ray plates—which often mislead standard saliency methods. This is reflected in the higher PGA scores, which confirm that CGR highlights the actual anatomical regions of interest (ROI) identified by expert radiologists.

The stability analysis (SSIM) reveals that CGR is significantly more robust to input noise than existing gradient-based methods. This is likely due to the intervention layer acting as a low-pass filter on the gradient manifold, suppressing the high-frequency artifacts that typically plague SmoothGrad and Vanilla Gradients. Consequently, the visualizations produced by CGR are more interpretable and less "jittery."

However, the reliance on a predefined causal graph $\mathcal{G}$ introduces a new dependency: the quality of the anatomical priors. While we utilized standard clinical knowledge, future work should explore automated causal discovery algorithms that can learn $\mathcal{G}$ directly from medical datasets. This would enable CGR to be applied to domains where causal structures are not yet fully mapped by medical literature.

---

### Limitations and Future Work
Despite its advantages, CGR has limitations. First, the computational cost of the intervention layer increases linearly with the number of anatomical regions defined in the causal graph. While currently feasible, scaling this to 3D volumetric data (e.g., MRI/CT) may require further optimizations such as sparse causal graph approximations.

Second, CGR assumes that the causal mechanisms are stationary. In clinical scenarios, diagnostic patterns can shift due to changing imaging protocols or demographic shifts (dataset shift). Future iterations should investigate dynamic causal graphs that evolve alongside the model’s weight updates.

Finally, while we demonstrate improved localization, we have not yet conducted a full clinical trial to assess how CGR affects clinician decision-making speed or accuracy. Future studies should focus on Human-in-the-Loop experiments to validate the practical utility of these saliency maps in real-world triage scenarios.

---

### Conclusion
We presented Causal-Gradient Refinement (CGR), a novel interpretability framework for medical imaging that integrates structural causal modeling with gradient-based attribution. By enforcing anatomical causal constraints, CGR suppresses non-causal noise, leading to more faithful and stable saliency maps. Our experiments on the ChestX-ray14 dataset demonstrate that CGR outperforms standard methods in both diagnostic localization and robustness. We believe that CGR provides a critical step toward the development of transparent and trustworthy medical AI systems.

---

# Latent Attention Traversal: A Differentiable Framework for Causal Interpretation in Text Classification

**Authors:** Elena V. Sterling [1], Marcus J. Thorne [2], and Sarah H. Jenkins [3]

**Affiliations:** 
[1] Department of Computer Science, Institute for Advanced Machine Learning, Zurich
[2] Laboratory for Neural Symbolic Systems, MIT
[3] Center for Explainable AI, Stanford University

---

### Abstract
Despite the empirical success of Transformer-based architectures in natural language processing (NLP), the interpretability of self-attention mechanisms remains a subject of intense debate. Standard attention weights often fail to provide faithful causal explanations due to the non-linear interaction of hidden states across layers. In this paper, we propose *Latent Attention Traversal* (LAT), a novel framework that treats the propagation of information through attention heads as a flow optimization problem. By enforcing a sparsity-constrained path integral over the attention graph, LAT identifies "information bottlenecks" that disproportionately influence final classification logits. We provide theoretical evidence that LAT satisfies the axioms of faithfulness and local sensitivity. Experimental results on the GLUE benchmark and custom sentiment analysis datasets demonstrate that LAT outperforms gradient-based attribution methods in both human-annotated alignment and robustness against adversarial perturbations.

**Keywords:** Explainable AI, Self-Attention, Causal Inference, Information Flow, NLP Interpretability.

---

### 1. Introduction
The ubiquity of large language models (LLMs) has necessitated a shift from predictive accuracy toward interpretability. While self-attention is touted as the cornerstone of Transformers, research suggests that raw attention scores are poor indicators of model decision-making [1]. This decoupling between attention weights and prediction performance stems from the fact that attention matrices are learned representations of token interactions, rather than explicit mappings of causal influence [2].

To address this, existing literature has moved toward post-hoc attribution methods. However, techniques like Integrated Gradients (IG) or LIME often suffer from high variance and computational overhead [3]. Furthermore, they frequently ignore the layered nature of Transformers, treating the network as a black-box function rather than a sequence of information-processing stages [4]. 

We introduce the Latent Attention Traversal (LAT) framework. LAT conceptualizes the Transformer as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of latent states. By applying an optimal transport objective to the attention matrices, we identify the most influential paths, or "traversal sequences," that dictate the final classification. Our contribution is threefold: (1) we formalize the attention flow problem as a constrained optimization; (2) we introduce an efficient algorithm for path extraction; and (3) we validate the framework against state-of-the-art baselines on high-stakes classification tasks.

---

### 2. Related Work
Interpretability in deep learning has historically been dominated by gradient-based saliency methods [5]. Early works sought to attribute importance to input pixels or tokens by calculating partial derivatives [6]. However, these methods are often sensitive to local noise and fail to capture global contextual dependencies [7].

Attention-based interpretability emerged with the advent of the Transformer architecture [8]. Bahdanau et al. [9] initially argued that attention weights could serve as soft alignments. Subsequent critiques by Jain and Wallace [10] challenged this, demonstrating that attention weights are frequently uncorrelated with gradient-based feature importance. Our work builds upon the "Attention Rollout" paradigm [11], extending it to a differentiable flow problem that incorporates cross-layer dependencies.

Recent advances in causal interpretability [12] have proposed interventions on latent variables. These methods, while robust, are often prohibitively slow [13]. LAT distinguishes itself by providing a closed-form approximation of these causal impacts, bridgeing the gap between efficient saliency mapping and rigorous causal analysis.

---

### 3. Background / Preliminaries
Let $X = \{x_1, \dots, x_n\}$ be an input sequence of tokens. A Transformer model $\mathcal{F}$ maps $X$ to a logit vector $y \in \mathbb{R}^K$ via a series of $L$ layers. Each layer $l$ contains $H$ attention heads. The attention matrix $A^{(l,h)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ denotes the interaction weights between tokens in layer $l$, head $h$.

The standard attention computation is defined as:
$$A^{(l,h)} = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) \quad (1)$$
where $Q$ and $K$ are the query and key projections. The state update is given by $V^{(l)} = \sum_{h=1}^H \text{Attn}(A^{(l,h)})W_O^{(l,h)}$. We define the *Total Attention Influence* (TAI) as the cumulative weight assigned to a token path $\pi = \{t_0, t_1, \dots, t_L\}$, where $t_l$ is the index of the token attended to at layer $l$.

---

### 4. Methodology / Proposed Approach
LAT operates by augmenting the attention matrices with a regularization term $\Omega(A)$ that penalizes dense connectivity, forcing the model to select a sparse set of high-influence paths. We define the flow objective as minimizing the divergence between the latent state transition and the primary logit direction.

Let $\mathbf{z}_l$ be the hidden representation at layer $l$. We define the traversal cost for a path $\pi$ as:
$$J(\pi) = \sum_{l=1}^L \| \mathbf{z}_l - \text{proj}(\mathbf{z}_{l-1}, A^{(l)}) \|_2^2 + \lambda \Omega(A^{(l)}) \quad (2)$$
where $\lambda$ is a hyperparameter balancing sparsity and reconstruction fidelity.

#### 4.1 The LAT Algorithm
The algorithm seeks to identify the path $\pi^*$ that maximizes the logit contribution $\nabla_{\mathbf{z}_L} y$. 

**Algorithm 1: Latent Attention Traversal**
1. **Input:** Input embeddings $X$, Model $\mathcal{F}$, Sparsity factor $\lambda$.
2. **Forward Pass:** Compute all $A^{(l,h)}$ and hidden states $H^{(l)}$.
3. **Backpropagation:** Compute importance scores $\alpha_{i,l} = \frac{\partial y}{\partial A_{i,l}}$.
4. **Path Optimization:** Solve the constrained flow:
   $$\min_{\pi} \sum_{l} \alpha_{l} \cdot A^{(l)}_{\pi(l), \pi(l+1)}$$
   subject to sparsity constraint $\sum_{l} \text{sparsity}(A^{(l)}) < \tau$.
5. **Output:** A ranked set of paths $\Pi^*$.

---

### 5. Theoretical Analysis
**Theorem 1 (Faithfulness):** *Given an optimal traversal path $\pi^*$, the gradient of the logit $y$ with respect to the input $x_i$ is linearly proportional to the cumulative flow along $\pi^*$.*

*Proof Sketch:* By the chain rule, $\frac{\partial y}{\partial x_i} = \sum_{\pi} \prod_{l=1}^L \frac{\partial \mathbf{z}_l}{\partial \mathbf{z}_{l-1}} \frac{\partial \mathbf{z}_{l-1}}{\partial x_i}$. Since LAT restricts the summation to sparse paths, the approximation error $||\nabla y - \sum_{\pi^*} \dots||$ is bounded by the residual variance of the pruned attention weights. Given the contractive property of the softmax operator, the error remains $O(1/L)$ as $L \to \infty$.

**Computational Complexity:** The path optimization step is formulated as a Viterbi-like search, resulting in $O(L \cdot H \cdot n^2)$ complexity, where $n$ is sequence length. This makes LAT suitable for deployment in large-scale inference pipelines.

---

### 6. Experiments
#### 6.1 Datasets
We evaluate LAT on the **SST-2 (Sentiment Analysis)** and **QNLI (Question-Answering)** datasets from the GLUE benchmark. We also employ the **ERASER** benchmark for human-annotated rationale evaluation [14].

#### 6.2 Baselines
We compare LAT against:
1. **Raw Attention (RA):** Unmodified softmax weights.
2. **Attention Rollout (AR):** Cumulative multiplication of matrices.
3. **Integrated Gradients (IG):** Path-integral gradient attribution.
4. **LIME:** Perturbation-based local linear explanations.
5. **Shapley Value Sampling (SVS):** Game-theoretic feature importance.

#### 6.3 Results
Results are measured by *Faithfulness Correlation* (FC) and *AUPRC* (Area Under Precision-Recall Curve) for rationale alignment.

**Table 1: Performance on SST-2 and QNLI Benchmarks**
| Method | SST-2 (FC) | QNLI (FC) | AUPRC (Alignment) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| RA | 0.52 | 0.48 | 0.41 |
| AR | 0.61 | 0.55 | 0.50 |
| IG | 0.74 | 0.68 | 0.62 |
| LIME | 0.69 | 0.64 | 0.58 |
| SVS | 0.76 | 0.71 | 0.65 |
| **LAT (Ours)** | **0.82** | **0.79** | **0.74** |

---

### 7. Ablation Study
We analyze the sensitivity of LAT to the sparsity parameter $\lambda$ and the number of layers $L$.

**Table 2: Ablation of LAT Components**
| Configuration | FC (SST-2) | Latency (ms) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| LAT (No Sparsity) | 0.71 | 45 |
| LAT (Fixed Path) | 0.75 | 12 |
| LAT (Full Framework) | 0.82 | 28 |

Observations indicate that while the full framework is slightly slower than a static path approximation, the gain in faithfulness is significant. Sparsity is essential for preventing "attention smearing" across irrelevant context.

---

### 8. Discussion / Insights
The experimental results confirm that LAT provides a more robust explanation of Transformer behavior than traditional saliency methods. By explicitly accounting for the cross-layer flow of information, LAT avoids the pitfalls of attributing importance to "noisy" layers that do not contribute to the final logit. The superiority of LAT on the ERASER benchmark highlights its alignment with human intuition regarding causal tokens.

Furthermore, we observed that LAT consistently identified linguistic markers (e.g., negation tokens in sentiment analysis) that other models missed. This suggests that the latent flow captured by our framework is better aligned with the semantic structure of the language, rather than just the statistical associations captured by raw attention.

The trade-off between latency and accuracy remains a critical consideration. While LAT is significantly faster than Shapley-based approximations, it introduces a 15-20% overhead compared to raw attention. Future iterations will explore weight quantization to further reduce the inference burden of the traversal optimization.

---

### 9. Limitations & Future Work
Despite its effectiveness, LAT has limitations. First, the current formulation assumes a linear composition of attention flows. While this provides a strong approximation, deep non-linearities in the MLP blocks of Transformers (GELU activations) may introduce higher-order interactions that our model currently approximates as noise.

Second, LAT is currently optimized for post-hoc explanation. Integrating the LAT objective directly into the model training phase as a regularization term could yield "intrinsically interpretable" models that maintain high performance without sacrificing transparency. 

Future work will investigate the scalability of LAT to models with billions of parameters (e.g., Llama-3, GPT-4). We hypothesize that the sparsity-constrained flow approach will prove even more critical in deeper architectures where attention diffusion is a major source of interpretability decay.

---

### 10. Conclusion
In this work, we presented Latent Attention Traversal (LAT), a novel framework for interpreting Transformer models. By modeling information propagation as a sparse flow problem, we achieved superior faithfulness in attribution compared to baseline methods. Our theoretical analysis and experimental validation demonstrate that LAT is a robust tool for XAI in NLP, bridging the gap between statistical performance and causal understanding.

---

### 11. References
[1] A. Vaswani et al., "Attention is all you need," in Proc. NeurIPS, Long Beach, 2017, pp. 5998-6008.
[2] J. Devlin et al., "BERT: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers," in Proc. NAACL, Minneapolis, 2019, pp. 4171-4186.
[3] M. Sundararajan et al., "Axiomatic attribution for deep networks," in Proc. ICML, Sydney, 2017, pp. 3319-3328.
[4] S. M. Lundberg and S. I. Lee, "A unified approach to interpreting model predictions," in Proc. NeurIPS, Long Beach, 2017, pp. 4765-4774.
[5] K. Simonyan et al., "Deep inside convolutional networks," in Proc. ICLR, Banff, 2014, pp. 1-12.
[6] M. D. Zeiler and R. Fergus, "Visualizing and understanding convolutional networks," in Proc. ECCV, Zurich, 2014, pp. 818-833.
[7] G. Hooker et al., "A benchmark for interpretability methods," in Proc. NeurIPS, Vancouver, 2019, pp. 7824-7834.
[8] B. Zhou et al., "Learning deep features for discriminative localization," in Proc. CVPR, Las Vegas, 2016, pp. 2921-2929.
[9] D. Bahdanau et al., "Neural machine translation by jointly learning to align and translate," in Proc. ICLR, San Diego, 2015, pp. 1-15.
[10] S. Jain and B. C. Wallace, "Attention is not explanation," in Proc. NAACL, Minneapolis, 2019, pp. 3543-3556.
[11] S. Abnar and W. Zuidema, "Quantifying attention flow in transformers," in Proc. ACL, Online, 2020, pp. 4190-4197.
[12] J. Pearl, "Causality: Models, reasoning, and inference," Cambridge University Press, 2009.
[13] T. Gao et al., "Causal intervention for transformer interpretability," in Proc. EMNLP, Punta Cana, 2021, pp. 1205-1215.
[14] J. DeYoung et al., "ERASER: A benchmark for rationalizing NLP models," in Proc. ACL, Florence, 2020, pp. 4443-4458.
[15] R. R. Selvaraju et al., "Grad-CAM: Visual explanations from deep networks," in Proc. ICCV, Venice, 2017, pp. 618-626.
[16] M. T. Ribeiro et al., "Why should I trust you? Explaining the predictions of any classifier," in Proc. KDD, San Francisco, 2016, pp. 1135-1144.
[17] Y. Liu et al., "RoBERTa: A robustly optimized BERT pretraining approach," arXiv preprint arXiv:1907.11692, 2019.
[18] C. Raffel et al., "Exploring the limits of transfer learning with a unified text-to-text transformer," JMLR, vol. 21, no. 140, pp. 1-67, 2020.
[19] H. Liu et al., "Attention masking for interpretability," in Proc. ACL, Bangkok, 2021, pp. 2234-2245.
[20] A. Karpathy, "The unreasonable effectiveness of recurrent neural networks," in Proc. CVPR, Boston, 2015, pp. 120-128.
[21] P. Veličković et al., "Graph attention networks," in Proc. ICLR, Vancouver, 2018, pp. 1-12.
[22] D. P. Kingma and J. Ba, "Adam: A method for stochastic optimization," in Proc. ICLR, San Diego, 2015, pp. 1-15.
