# Bio-HeteroGraph: A Multi-Relational Transformer Framework for Biomedical Relation Extraction

**Authors:**
Elena V. Sterling$^1$, Marcus Thorne$^2$, and Julian K. Halloway$^1$
$^1$Department of Computer Science, Institute for Advanced AI, Zurich
$^2$Biomedical Informatics Research Group, Stanford University

---

### Abstract

Biomedical Relation Extraction (BRE) remains a critical bottleneck in the curation of structured knowledge bases from unstructured clinical literature. Conventional approaches often fail to capture the complex, multi-modal dependencies inherent in heterogeneous biomedical graphs, where entities (genes, diseases, proteins) exhibit diverse interaction types. In this paper, we propose **Bio-HeteroGraph**, a novel Heterogeneous Graph Transformer (HGT) architecture specifically designed to model multi-relational biomedical knowledge. Our approach incorporates a relation-aware attention mechanism that dynamically weights the importance of neighbor nodes based on the edge semantic schema. We introduce a "Semantic Message-Passing" (SMP) protocol that preserves node-type specificity while enabling cross-domain feature propagation. Extensive experiments on the BioRED and PubMedBERT-curated datasets demonstrate that Bio-HeteroGraph achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming competitive baselines by an average of 3.2% F1-score. We provide a rigorous complexity analysis, demonstrating that our model maintains a linear time complexity $O(|V| + |E|)$ with respect to graph size, making it suitable for large-scale clinical corpora.

**Keywords:** Graph Neural Networks, Transformer Architectures, Biomedical Relation Extraction, Heterogeneous Graphs, Multi-Relational Learning.

---

### I. Introduction

The rapid proliferation of biomedical literature, exceeding millions of publications annually, presents a formidable challenge for manual knowledge curation. Biomedical Relation Extraction (BRE) serves as the foundational task for constructing Knowledge Graphs (KGs) that facilitate drug discovery, personalized medicine, and molecular pathway analysis [1]. Despite advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs), these models often struggle with the structural complexity of biomedical relationships, where the interaction between a protein and a disease is fundamentally distinct from the interaction between two chemical compounds [2].

Existing paradigms for BRE typically rely on either sequence-based models or homogeneous graph neural networks (GNNs). While sequence models capture local syntactic nuances, they frequently ignore the long-range relational dependencies defined in established biomedical ontologies [3]. Conversely, traditional GNNs suffer from "over-smoothing" and often collapse heterogeneous node types into a singular feature space, thereby obscuring the rich semantic distinctions inherent in multi-relational graphs [4].

To address these limitations, we introduce **Bio-HeteroGraph**, a framework that explicitly models the heterogeneity of biomedical graphs through a relation-aware attention mechanism. Unlike previous architectures, our method leverages a dual-stream Transformer that processes node features and relational schema embeddings in parallel, ensuring that the structural integrity of the biomedical graph is preserved during feature aggregation [5]. 

The contributions of this paper are three-fold:
1. We propose the Bio-HeteroGraph architecture, featuring an SMP protocol that modulates message passing based on relation-specific transformation matrices.
2. We derive a theoretical bound on the representational power of heterogeneous attention, demonstrating that our approach reduces the bias associated with node-type imbalance in sparse clinical datasets.
3. We conduct an empirical evaluation against five baseline models, showing significant gains in precision and recall across two benchmark datasets, providing insights into the efficiency-accuracy trade-offs inherent in large-scale graph transformers.

---

### II. Related Work

The landscape of BRE has evolved from rule-based systems to deep learning architectures. Early work focused on dependency parsing and kernel-based methods, which were limited by the manual effort required for feature engineering [6]. Recent trends have shifted toward Transformer-based models, such as PubMedBERT, which leverage large-scale pre-training on domain-specific corpora [7]. However, these models remain "graph-blind," often neglecting the explicit relational structure defined in ontologies like UMLS.

Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged as the standard for structured data. Relational GCNs (R-GCNs) introduced relation-specific weights to handle multi-relational edges [8]. Yet, R-GCNs are susceptible to vanishing gradients in deep architectures. Heterogeneous Graph Transformers (HGT) attempted to resolve this by utilizing node-type and edge-type specific attention, though they are computationally intensive [9]. Our work builds upon these foundations by introducing a lightweight SMP mechanism that mitigates the computational overhead while enhancing the semantic granularity of message aggregation.

Furthermore, recent studies have explored the integration of knowledge graphs into language models via Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) [10]. While effective, these methods often treat the KG as a static retrieval source rather than a dynamic computational graph. Bio-HeteroGraph differentiates itself by performing joint representation learning over the graph structure and textual metadata.

---

### III. Background and Preliminaries

We formally define a biomedical heterogeneous graph as $G = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E}, \mathcal{T}, \mathcal{R})$, where $\mathcal{V}$ is the set of nodes (e.g., genes, drugs), $\mathcal{E}$ is the set of edges (relations), and $\mathcal{T}$ and $\mathcal{R}$ are the sets of node and relation types, respectively. Each node $v \in \mathcal{V}$ is associated with a type $\tau(v) \in \mathcal{T}$ and an initial feature vector $h_v^{(0)}$.

The objective of BRE is to predict the label $y_{uv} \in \mathcal{Y}$ for a given pair of nodes $(u, v)$. In a heterogeneous graph, the message-passing function can be defined as:
$$m_{u \to v}^{(l)} = \text{AGG}(\{ \text{ATTN}(h_u^{(l-1)}, h_v^{(l-1)}, r_{uv}) \})$$
where $r_{uv} \in \mathcal{R}$ denotes the relation type between nodes $u$ and $v$. The core challenge is defining the function $\text{ATTN}$ such that it captures the heterogeneity without introducing excessive parameters.

---

### IV. Methodology

#### A. Architecture Overview
Bio-HeteroGraph operates by mapping heterogeneous inputs into a shared latent space through type-specific projection layers. Given a node $v$ of type $\tau$, we project its input features $x_v$ into a common dimension $d$:
$$h_v^{(0)} = W_{\tau(v)} x_v + b_{\tau(v)}$$
where $W_{\tau(v)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_{in}}$ is a learnable projection matrix. This initialization ensures that nodes of different types (e.g., a "Disease" vs. a "Gene") are aligned before entering the Transformer layers.

#### B. Semantic Message-Passing (SMP)
Our SMP protocol computes the attention score $\alpha_{uv}$ by considering the relation $r$ as a transformation query. For a target node $v$, the hidden state update is:
$$h_v^{(l)} = \sigma \left( \sum_{u \in \mathcal{N}(v)} \alpha_{uv}^{(l)} \cdot (W_{r}^{(l)} h_u^{(l-1)}) \right)$$
where $W_r^{(l)}$ is the relation-specific weight matrix. The attention coefficient $\alpha_{uv}$ is computed using a multi-head mechanism:
$$\alpha_{uv} = \text{softmax} \left( \frac{(Q_{\tau(v)} h_v)(K_{\tau(u)} h_u)^T}{\sqrt{d}} + \beta_{r} \right)$$
Here, $\beta_r$ is a learnable bias scalar assigned to the edge type $r$, which accounts for the varying frequency of relation types in the training set [11].

#### C. Algorithmic Complexity
The computational complexity of a single layer is $O(|V| \cdot d^2 + |E| \cdot d)$, where $|V|$ and $|E|$ represent the number of nodes and edges, respectively. By utilizing sparse matrix multiplication, we ensure the model scales linearly with the graph size, satisfying the requirements for large-scale bio-informatic datasets [12].

---

### V. Theoretical Analysis

**Claim 1.** *The SMP mechanism effectively reduces the over-smoothing phenomenon common in deep GNNs by enforcing relation-specific signal modulation.*

*Proof Sketch:* In standard GCNs, the Laplacian smoothing leads to the convergence of node features to a stationary distribution. In Bio-HeteroGraph, the relation-specific weight matrices $W_r$ serve as a projection constraint. Since $W_r$ is learned per edge-type, the gradient flow is restricted to the subspace defined by the relation, preventing the indiscriminate mixing of feature spaces across disparate node types [13].

**Claim 2.** *The inclusion of the bias scalar $\beta_r$ minimizes the variance of the attention distribution in sparse graphs.*

In biomedical datasets, long-tail relations (rare interactions) often suffer from high variance in attention scores. The bias term $\beta_r$ acts as a prior that stabilizes the softmax normalization, effectively increasing the signal-to-noise ratio for infrequent relations [14].

---

### VI. Experiments

#### A. Datasets
We evaluate our model on two primary datasets:
1. **BioRED:** A human-curated dataset containing 600 abstracts with gene-disease relations [15].
2. **PubMed-Large:** A synthetic graph constructed from 50,000 PubMed articles using NER tools, containing 200k entities and 800k edges [16].

#### B. Baselines
We compare Bio-HeteroGraph against:
1. **GCN:** Standard Graph Convolutional Network [17].
2. **R-GCN:** Relational Graph Convolutional Network [18].
3. **PubMedBERT:** Transformer-based sequence model [19].
4. **HGT:** Standard Heterogeneous Graph Transformer [20].
5. **KG-BERT:** Knowledge graph aware language model [21].

#### C. Evaluation Metrics
We report Macro-F1 and Precision-Recall Area Under the Curve (PR-AUC), which are robust against class imbalance [22].

---

### VII. Results and Ablation Study

**Table 1: Comparison of BRE Performance (Macro-F1 %)**

| Model | BioRED | PubMed-Large |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| GCN | 68.2 | 64.5 |
| R-GCN | 71.4 | 67.2 |
| PubMedBERT | 74.8 | 72.1 |
| HGT | 76.5 | 73.9 |
| **Bio-HeteroGraph** | **79.7** | **77.4** |

**Table 2: Ablation Study (BioRED Dataset)**

| Component | Macro-F1 | $\Delta$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Full Bio-HeteroGraph | 79.7 | - |
| w/o Relation Bias ($\beta_r$) | 77.1 | -2.6 |
| w/o Type-specific Projection | 75.4 | -4.3 |
| w/o SMP Protocol | 72.9 | -6.8 |

*Observation:* The ablation study indicates that the SMP protocol is the most significant contributor to performance, reinforcing the importance of relation-aware message passing in heterogeneous biomedical contexts [23].

---

### VIII. Discussion

The experimental results suggest that Bio-HeteroGraph successfully addresses the challenges of multi-relational modeling. The superior performance on the PubMed-Large dataset highlights the model's scalability; as the graph density increases, the SMP protocol maintains coherent signal propagation, whereas standard HGT models exhibit performance degradation due to parameter explosion [24].

A critical insight from our findings is the role of the relation bias $\beta_r$. In biomedical corpora, many relations (e.g., "inhibits") are highly frequent, while others (e.g., "regulates-transcription-of") are rare. The bias term effectively acts as a regularization mechanism, preventing the model from overfitting to the most frequent relations while preserving the structural nuances of rare, high-value biomedical interactions [25].

Furthermore, the integration of node-type projections allows Bio-HeteroGraph to handle "cold-start" entities effectively. By projecting different node types into a shared semantic space, the model learns a latent representation that captures the functional similarities between genes and proteins, even when their textual descriptions are sparse or noisy.

---

### IX. Limitations and Future Work

Despite the efficacy of Bio-HeteroGraph, several limitations remain. First, the model assumes a fixed graph structure. In dynamic scenarios where new biomedical literature is indexed in real-time, the graph must be re-processed. Future work will investigate incremental graph learning techniques that allow for the update of node representations without full-graph retraining.

Second, the current SMP protocol relies on predefined edge types. While this provides stability, it limits the model's ability to discover latent, implicit relations that are not explicitly defined in the input schema. We aim to integrate a latent relation discovery module that can infer implicit edges based on structural graph motifs.

Finally, the computational requirements for training on massive scale graphs (multi-million nodes) remain non-trivial. Although we achieve linear complexity, the constant factors in Transformer-based attention are significant. Future research will focus on model quantization and pruning to facilitate deployment on resource-constrained clinical infrastructure.

---

### X. Conclusion

In this paper, we introduced Bio-HeteroGraph, a novel architecture for Biomedical Relation Extraction. By combining relation-aware attention with a semantic message-passing protocol, we demonstrated that heterogeneous graph transformers can significantly outperform existing baselines in capturing the complexities of biomedical knowledge. Our theoretical and empirical analysis confirms that the model is both robust and scalable. We believe this framework provides a strong foundation for future developments in automated knowledge discovery in the life sciences.

---

### XI. References

[1] A. Smith, B. Jones, "Knowledge graphs in medicine," *J. Bio. Informatics*, 2021, pp. 10-25.

# Latent Causal Disentanglement via Variational Structural Intervention (VSI) for Robust Out-of-Distribution Generalization

**Authors:**  
Dr. Elena R. Vance¹, Dr. Marcus T. Thorne², Dr. Sarah J. Jenkins¹  
¹Department of Computer Science, Institute for Advanced AI, Zurich.  
²Department of Statistics, Stanford University, Palo Alto.

---

### Abstract
Out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization remains a fundamental bottleneck in deep learning, primarily due to the tendency of neural models to rely on spurious correlations rather than causal mechanisms. In this paper, we propose Variational Structural Intervention (VSI), a novel framework for causal representation learning. VSI operates by decomposing latent space representations into invariant causal features and environment-specific spurious features through a structural causal model (SCM) constrained by interventional objective functions. Unlike existing invariant risk minimization (IRM) approaches, VSI explicitly models the causal graph topology, allowing for the isolation of exogenous noise variables that typically degrade OOD performance. We demonstrate that our method achieves superior robust generalization across three benchmark datasets—Colored MNIST, DomainBed-VLCS, and TerraIncognita—outperforming current state-of-the-art baselines by an average of 4.2% in accuracy. We further provide a theoretical analysis of the identifiability of our latent causal factors under the assumption of independent causal mechanisms.

**Keywords:** Causal Inference, OOD Generalization, Representation Learning, Variational Inference, Structural Causal Models.

---

### 1. Introduction
Modern machine learning models frequently achieve human-level performance under the assumption that the training and test distributions are i.i.d. [1], [2]. However, in real-world deployment, this assumption is often violated, leading to catastrophic failure modes when models encounter distribution shifts [3], [4]. These failures are largely attributed to the reliance on "shortcut learning" or spurious correlations, where models exploit statistical patterns that are predictive in training but unstable under environmental shifts [5], [6].

To address this, recent research has pivoted toward causal representation learning, which aims to recover the underlying data-generating process rather than mere statistical associations [7], [8]. While approaches such as Invariant Risk Minimization (IRM) have attempted to learn features that perform optimally across multiple environments, they often lack the structural granularity required to distinguish between causal and non-causal latent variables [9], [10]. Furthermore, many existing frameworks require access to explicit intervention data, which is rarely available in high-dimensional observational settings [11], [12].

In this paper, we introduce Variational Structural Intervention (VSI), a novel framework that bridges the gap between variational autoencoders and structural causal models. Our core contribution is the integration of an interventional bottleneck that enforces structural constraints during the latent encoding process. By leveraging the principle of independent causal mechanisms (ICM), VSI promotes the emergence of representations that are robust to environmental perturbations [13], [14]. We demonstrate that by explicitly modeling the latent DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) structure, we can achieve significantly better generalization than purely discriminative or standard variational methods [15], [16].

---

### 2. Related Work
The literature on OOD generalization can be categorized into domain generalization, invariant learning, and causal discovery [17], [18]. Domain generalization seeks to learn a representation that is invariant across domains, often using adversarial training or distribution matching [19], [20]. However, these methods often fail to identify the true causal structure, leading to overly conservative representations that sacrifice accuracy for robustness [21], [22].

Invariant Risk Minimization (IRM) represents a seminal approach that proposes optimizing for a predictor that is simultaneously optimal across all domains [23], [24]. Despite its theoretical elegance, IRM suffers from optimization instability and high variance in small-sample regimes [25], [26]. Our work builds upon IRM but introduces a structural prior that mitigates the need for explicit domain labels during inference, effectively learning a causal disentanglement that is more stable.

Causal representation learning has recently gained traction, particularly with the development of identifiable VAEs [27], [28]. By incorporating auxiliary information or weak supervision, these methods attempt to recover the latent factors that satisfy the structural requirements of a causal graph [29], [30]. We extend this by proposing a unified framework where the latent space is partitioned into causal and anti-causal components, directly addressing the OOD challenge through structural intervention.

---

### 3. Background and Preliminaries
Let $\mathcal{X}$ denote the input space and $\mathcal{Y}$ the target space. We assume data is generated by an underlying Structural Causal Model (SCM) defined by a set of exogenous noise variables $\mathcal{U}$ and endogenous variables $\mathcal{Z} = \{z_1, \dots, z_k\}$. The causal mechanism is defined by functions $f_i$:
$z_i = f_i(pa(z_i), u_i)$ (1)
where $pa(z_i) \subseteq \mathcal{Z} \setminus \{z_i\}$ are the parents of $z_i$ in the DAG $\mathcal{G}$.

In OOD scenarios, we assume the existence of multiple environments $e \in \mathcal{E}$. Each environment modifies the distribution of exogenous noise $P(u | e)$, thereby inducing a shift in the observed data $P(x, y | e)$. Our objective is to learn a representation $h = \phi(x)$ that is invariant to the changes in $P(u | e)$ and relies only on the causal parents of the target variable $y$.

---

### 4. Methodology: Variational Structural Intervention (VSI)
The VSI framework consists of two main components: a causal encoder $\phi_\theta$ that maps inputs to latent factors, and a structural decoder $\psi_\phi$ that reconstructs the input while satisfying the DAG constraints.

#### 4.1. The Interventional Bottleneck
We define the latent space $\mathcal{Z}$ as a concatenation of causal features $z_c$ and spurious features $z_s$. We impose a structural constraint using an interventional loss, defined as the KL-divergence between the latent distributions across simulated interventions:
$\mathcal{L}_{int} = \mathbb{E}_{e \sim \mathcal{E}} [D_{KL}(q_\theta(z_c|x, e) || q_\theta(z_c|x, e'))]$ (2)
This encourages $z_c$ to remain invariant regardless of the environment-specific spurious variations captured in $z_s$.

#### 4.2. Algorithm
The training procedure follows the pseudocode described in Algorithm 1.

**Algorithm 1: Training VSI**
1. Initialize encoder $\phi$, decoder $\psi$, and causal graph $\mathcal{G}$.
2. For iteration $t = 1 \dots T$:
3.   Sample batch $(x, y, e)$ from training environments.
4.   Compute latent representations $z_c, z_s = \phi(x)$.
5.   Compute reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L}_{rec} = ||x - \psi(z_c, z_s)||^2$.
6.   Compute predictive loss $\mathcal{L}_{pred} = \mathcal{L}(y, \text{softmax}(W \cdot z_c))$.
7.   Compute interventional bottleneck loss $\mathcal{L}_{int}$ using Equation (2).
8.   Backpropagate total loss: $\mathcal{L}_{tot} = \mathcal{L}_{pred} + \lambda_1 \mathcal{L}_{rec} + \lambda_2 \mathcal{L}_{int}$.
9.   Update $\phi, \psi$ via Adam optimizer.

---

### 5. Theoretical Analysis
**Theorem 1 (Identifiability).** *Under the assumption of additive noise and non-Gaussianity of exogenous variables, the causal latent factors $z_c$ are identifiable up to a permutation and element-wise scaling if the structural intervention $\mathcal{L}_{int}$ is minimized to zero.*

*Proof Sketch:* The structural intervention forces the causal mechanism $f_c$ to be independent of the environmental noise $u_e$. Given the non-Gaussianity, the ICA-based disentanglement property ensures that the latent space can be recovered as the true causal variables [31].

**Complexity Analysis:** The computational complexity of VSI is dominated by the forward pass of the encoder and the structural graph updates. For a latent space of dimension $D$, the complexity is $O(N \cdot D^2)$, where $N$ is the batch size. This is comparable to standard VAE architectures, making it highly scalable.

---

### 6. Experiments
We evaluate VSI on three datasets:
1. **Colored MNIST:** A synthetic dataset where the color of the digit is correlated with the label but flips in test environments.
2. **DomainBed-VLCS:** A standard benchmark for domain generalization.
3. **TerraIncognita:** A real-world dataset involving camera trap images across different locations.

#### 6.1. Baselines
We compare VSI against: (1) ERM (Empirical Risk Minimization), (2) IRM [23], (3) V-REx [32], (4) GroupDRO [33], and (5) C-OOD [34].

#### 6.2. Results
Table 1 shows the OOD accuracy on the test domains.

| Method | Colored MNIST | VLCS | TerraIncognita |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ERM | 70.2% | 74.1% | 42.5% |
| IRM | 78.5% | 76.8% | 48.2% |
| V-REx | 79.1% | 77.2% | 49.1% |
| GroupDRO | 77.9% | 76.5% | 47.8% |
| C-OOD | 81.3% | 78.4% | 50.3% |
| **VSI (Ours)** | **85.4%** | **81.2%** | **53.7%** |

---

### 7. Ablation Study
We analyze the contribution of the interventional bottleneck ($\mathcal{L}_{int}$) and the structural DAG prior.

| Configuration | Accuracy (Avg) |
| :--- | :--- |
| VSI (Full) | 73.4% |
| VSI (w/o $\mathcal{L}_{int}$) | 68.2% |
| VSI (w/o DAG prior) | 65.5% |

The results indicate that both the interventional bottleneck and the structural prior are essential for capturing robust causal representations.

---

### 8. Discussion
The experimental results demonstrate that VSI consistently outperforms standard invariant learning baselines. The superior performance in the TerraIncognita dataset is particularly notable, as it suggests that VSI is capable of handling complex, high-dimensional environmental shifts where spurious correlations are deeply embedded in the feature space.

The theoretical identifiability provided by the structural intervention allows the model to ignore domain-specific noise that would otherwise lead to performance degradation. This confirms our hypothesis that explicit modeling of causal dependencies is a prerequisite for robust OOD performance.

We note that the performance gap between VSI and IRM increases as the number of environments decreases, suggesting that VSI is more sample-efficient in terms of required environmental diversity. This is a critical advantage in real-world scenarios where data collection is expensive and limited.

---

### 9. Limitations and Future Work
Despite the promising results, VSI has several limitations. First, it relies on the assumption of a static causal graph across all environments. In dynamic systems where the causal structure itself evolves, the current formulation may require extension to non-stationary structural models.

Second, the computational cost of calculating the interventional bottleneck grows with the number of latent dimensions. While currently manageable, future work will explore approximation techniques such as sparse causal graph learning to handle significantly larger feature sets.

Finally, the reliance on the additive noise assumption for identifiability is a strong constraint. Investigating non-linear, non-additive causal mechanisms remains an open area for future research, potentially involving kernel-based methods or transformer-based latent dynamics.

---

### 10. Conclusion
In this paper, we proposed Variational Structural Intervention (VSI), a framework for learning causal representations in the presence of distribution shifts. By integrating structural causal models with variational inference, we successfully disentangled causal features from spurious correlations. Our empirical results validate the robustness of VSI across various domains, providing a significant step forward in the quest for reliable and generalizable AI systems.

---

### 11. References
[1] L. Bottou, "Machine learning that matters," in *Proc. NeurIPS*, Vancouver, 2012, pp. 1-15.  
[2] V. Vapnik, *Statistical Learning Theory*, Wiley, 1998.  
[3] J. Quinonero-Candela et al., *Dataset Shift in Machine Learning*, MIT Press, 2009.  
[4] R. Geirhos et al., "Shortcut learning in deep neural networks," in *Nature Machine Intelligence*, vol. 2, 2020, pp. 665-673.  
[5] K. Muandet et al., "Domain generalization using invariant feature representation," in *Proc. ICML*, Atlanta, 2013, pp. 10-18.  
[6] M. Arjovsky et al., "Invariant risk minimization," in *arXiv preprint*, 2019.

# TabNet-X: Enhancing Gated Feature Selection through Contrastive Self-Supervised Pretraining and Adaptive Masking

**Authors:**
Elena V. Draganov, Institute for Intelligent Systems, Zurich
Marcus J. Thorne, Department of Computer Science, Stanford University
Sarah L. Chen, AI Research Lab, Google DeepMind

### Abstract
Tabular data remains the cornerstone of enterprise machine learning, yet deep learning models often struggle to outperform gradient-boosted decision trees (GBDTs) due to difficulties in capturing feature interactions and handling heterogeneous column types. While TabNet introduced sequential attention for sparse feature selection, it suffers from significant performance degradation in low-data regimes and sub-optimal convergence during training. In this paper, we propose TabNet-X, an evolved architecture that integrates a novel contrastive self-supervised pretraining objective with an adaptive masking mechanism. By leveraging a dual-stream masking policy, TabNet-X effectively decouples feature importance learning from task-specific prediction, leading to more robust representation learning. We demonstrate that TabNet-X consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on benchmark datasets, including the Adult, Covertype, and Higgs datasets. Our experimental results indicate a 4.2% relative improvement in AUC-ROC on average, while maintaining computational efficiency comparable to standard TabNet. We provide a rigorous ablation study quantifying the contributions of the contrastive loss component and the adaptive masking strategy.

### Keywords
Tabular Machine Learning, Self-Supervised Learning, Feature Selection, Deep Learning, Representation Learning.

---

### Introduction
The ubiquity of tabular datasets in industrial applications, ranging from credit scoring to predictive maintenance, has long sustained the dominance of GBDT models like XGBoost [1] and LightGBM [2]. These models are highly effective at capturing non-linear interactions within structured data while providing inherent interpretability. However, the paradigm shift toward deep learning has introduced architectures like TabNet [3], which utilizes sequential attention to emulate the decision-making process of tree-based models by performing sparse feature selection. Despite these advancements, TabNet’s susceptibility to overfitting in limited data environments and its reliance on supervised signals during the initial learning phase remain significant barriers to adoption.

Recent advancements in self-supervised learning (SSL), such as masked autoencoding [4] and contrastive learning [5], have revolutionized representation learning in computer vision and natural language processing. These paradigms allow models to learn rich, structural priors without the need for extensive labeled datasets. In this work, we argue that the primary bottleneck in TabNet is the rigid coupling of feature selection and predictive heads, which forces the model to learn task-specific importance weights from scratch. 

To address this, we introduce **TabNet-X**, an architecture that decouples feature selection through a pretraining phase driven by a novel contrastive masking loss. Unlike standard SSL methods that rely on simple reconstruction, TabNet-X employs an adaptive masking mechanism that prioritizes the selection of salient feature subsets across diverse tabular structures. Our approach provides three primary contributions:
1. An adaptive masking framework that balances global feature relevance with local instance-specific nuances.
2. A contrastive objective that aligns representations of masked and unmasked feature subspaces, improving the model's robustness to noisy features.
3. Extensive empirical validation demonstrating state-of-the-art performance on high-dimensional tabular datasets.

The remainder of this paper is structured as follows: Section 2 reviews related work in tabular deep learning. Section 3 formalizes the preliminaries. Section 4 describes the proposed TabNet-X architecture. Section 5 presents theoretical insights, followed by experimental results in Section 6. Finally, we discuss limitations and conclude.

---

### Related Work
The landscape of tabular deep learning has evolved from simple Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) [6] to specialized architectures. Neural networks typically struggle with tabular data due to the lack of spatial or temporal locality [7]. To bridge this gap, researchers have proposed models that incorporate attention mechanisms to facilitate feature selection [8, 9]. 

TabNet [3] stands as a landmark in this domain, utilizing an encoder-decoder architecture with sequential attention modules. However, subsequent studies have identified performance plateaus in TabNet when dealing with high-cardinality categorical variables [10, 11]. Other approaches have explored the use of Transformers for tabular data, such as TabTransformer [12] and FT-Transformer [13], which treat tabular data as sequences. While powerful, these models are often computationally expensive and struggle with sparse, heterogeneous datasets.

Self-supervised learning has recently gained traction in the tabular domain. VIME [14] introduced a self-supervised approach using mask prediction, while SCARF [15] leveraged contrastive learning by corrupting input features. Our work builds upon the conceptual foundation of SCARF but introduces an adaptive masking mechanism derived from the gating logic of TabNet, effectively bridging the gap between feature importance learning and representation learning.

---

### Background / Preliminaries
Let $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^N$ denote a tabular dataset, where $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^D$ is a feature vector and $y_i$ is the target label. TabNet employs an encoder consisting of $K$ sequential steps. At each step $k$, the encoder produces a mask $\mathbf{M}[k]$ that selects a subset of features:
$$\mathbf{f}[k] = \text{mask}(\mathbf{x}) \odot \text{att}(\mathbf{x})$$
where $\text{att}$ is the attention mechanism and $\odot$ denotes the element-wise product. The selection mechanism is constrained by a sparsity penalty $\lambda$ to ensure that only the most relevant features are active at any given step. 

The TabNet encoder outputs a transformed representation $\mathbf{z} = \sum_{k=1}^K \mathbf{f}[k]$. The goal of TabNet-X is to optimize this representation space before the classification head $g(\cdot)$ is applied, such that $\hat{y} = g(\mathbf{z})$.

---

### Methodology / Proposed Approach
TabNet-X introduces an auxiliary pretraining objective based on adaptive masking. Given an input $\mathbf{x}$, we create a corrupted version $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ by applying a mask $\mathbf{M}_a$ generated via an adaptive gating layer.

**1. Adaptive Masking Mechanism:**
The adaptive mask is derived from the internal state of the sequential steps. Let $\mathbf{a}_k$ be the attention weight vector at step $k$. We define the adaptive mask $\mathbf{M}_a$ as:
$$\mathbf{M}_a = \sigma(\tau \cdot \sum_{k=1}^K \mathbf{a}_k)$$
where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid function and $\tau$ is a temperature parameter controlling mask sparsity.

**2. Contrastive Objective:**
We map $\mathbf{x}$ and $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ into an embedding space $\mathcal{Z}$. We define the loss function $L_{ssl}$ using a InfoNCE-like objective:
$$L_{ssl} = -\log \frac{\exp(\text{sim}(\phi(\mathbf{x}), \phi(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})) / \gamma)}{\sum_{\mathbf{x}' \in \mathcal{B}} \exp(\text{sim}(\phi(\mathbf{x}), \phi(\mathbf{x}')) / \gamma)}$$
where $\phi(\cdot)$ is the encoder, $\text{sim}(\cdot)$ is cosine similarity, and $\mathcal{B}$ is the batch.

**3. Algorithm Pseudocode:**
```python
Algorithm 1: TabNet-X Pretraining
Input: Dataset D, Batch Size B, Temperature tau, Iterations T
Output: Optimized weights theta
For iter = 1 to T:
    Batch = sample(D, B)
    Mask = generate_adaptive_mask(Batch, tau)
    Augmented_Batch = apply_mask(Batch, Mask)
    Z_orig = Encoder(Batch)
    Z_aug = Encoder(Augmented_Batch)
    Loss = Contrastive_Loss(Z_orig, Z_aug)
    Backprop(Loss)
End For
```

---

### Theoretical Analysis
TabNet-X relies on the hypothesis that minimizing the contrastive loss between an unmasked and adaptively masked sample forces the encoder to capture "structural invariants" within the tabular features. 

**Theorem 1 (Representation Stability):** Under the assumption that features are drawn from a mixture of sub-Gaussian distributions, the contrastive objective $L_{ssl}$ minimizes the variance of the encoder output $\text{Var}(\phi(\mathbf{x}))$ while maximizing the mutual information between the subset of features selected by $\mathbf{M}_a$ and the target manifold.

The computational complexity of the masking step is $O(D \cdot K)$, where $D$ is the number of features and $K$ is the number of sequential steps. Given that $K \ll D$, the overhead of TabNet-X relative to standard TabNet is marginal, approximately $O(1.1 \times \text{training time})$.

---

### Experiments
We evaluate TabNet-X against five baselines: XGBoost [1], LightGBM [2], TabNet [3], TabTransformer [12], and FT-Transformer [13].

**Datasets:**
- Adult: Predicting income levels (Binary).
- Covertype: Multi-class classification of forest cover.
- Higgs: Particle physics classification (Binary).

**Experimental Setup:**
All models were trained using a 5-fold cross-validation scheme. Hyperparameters for TabNet-X were tuned using Bayesian optimization (Optuna). Learning rate was set to 0.02, batch size to 1024, and $K=8$.

**Results:**

| Dataset | XGBoost | LightGBM | TabNet | FT-Transformer | TabNet-X (Ours) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Adult (AUC) | 0.921 | 0.923 | 0.918 | 0.925 | **0.934** |
| Covertype (Acc) | 0.884 | 0.887 | 0.879 | 0.891 | **0.902** |
| Higgs (AUC) | 0.812 | 0.815 | 0.805 | 0.819 | **0.828** |

*(Table 1: Performance comparison across selected benchmarks.)*

---

### Ablation Study
To isolate the impact of our contributions, we perform an ablation on the Adult dataset.

| Component | AUC | Delta |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Full TabNet-X | 0.934 | - |
| w/o Contrastive Loss | 0.919 | -0.015 |
| w/o Adaptive Masking | 0.922 | -0.012 |
| Standard TabNet | 0.918 | -0.016 |

*(Table 2: Ablation study showing the impact of pretraining components.)*

---

### Discussion / Insights
The experimental results validate our hypothesis that self-supervised pretraining significantly enhances feature selection stability in tabular architectures. The performance gap between TabNet and TabNet-X is most pronounced in datasets with higher noise ratios (e.g., Higgs), where the adaptive masking mechanism effectively filters irrelevant features prior to the supervised phase. 

The adaptive masking policy serves a dual purpose: it acts as a regularizer during pretraining and as a feature-importance guide during inference. We observe that the learned masks in TabNet-X are notably more sparse than those learned in standard TabNet, suggesting that the contrastive loss forces the model to identify a "minimal sufficient set" of features for downstream tasks.

One trade-off observed is the increased training time during the pretraining phase. While the inference latency remains identical to standard TabNet, the pretraining stage requires approximately 30% more wall-clock time. However, for large-scale enterprise deployments, this is a negligible price to pay for the observed gains in predictive accuracy and model robustness.

---

### Limitations & Future Work
Despite the improvements, TabNet-X faces limitations. The current adaptive masking mechanism is global, meaning it does not account for feature dependencies that change across different strata of the data. Future work will investigate dynamic, instance-conditional masking using reinforcement learning.

Furthermore, while TabNet-X demonstrates improved performance on categorical data, its handling of extreme high-cardinality categorical features (e.g., user IDs) remains suboptimal. We plan to integrate entity embeddings directly into the masking logic to allow for better representational control.

Finally, we recognize that the contrastive objective is sensitive to the choice of the negative sampling strategy in the InfoNCE loss. Exploring hard-negative mining techniques could potentially further improve the robustness of the learned representations in highly imbalanced datasets.

---

### Conclusion
We have presented TabNet-X, a novel architecture that enhances tabular deep learning through adaptive masking and contrastive self-supervised pretraining. Our approach effectively bridges the gap between GBDT-like feature selection and deep neural representations. Through comprehensive experiments, we demonstrated that TabNet-X achieves superior performance on standard tabular benchmarks while maintaining computational efficiency. We believe this framework provides a robust foundation for future research in tabular representation learning.

# Sparse Mixture-of-Experts Attention (S-MoEA): Efficient Long-Document Summarization via Dynamic Expert Routing

**Authors:**
Dr. Elena R. Vance (Department of Computer Science, Stanford University), 
Dr. Marcus T. Thorne (Institute for Advanced AI, MIT), 
Dr. Sarah J. Jenkins (Google Research, Mountain View)

---

### Abstract
Long-document summarization remains a bottleneck in natural language processing due to the quadratic complexity of standard self-attention mechanisms. While sparse attention and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures have independently addressed efficiency and parameter scaling, their integration for long-context tasks remains under-explored. We propose Sparse Mixture-of-Experts Attention (S-MoEA), a novel architectural framework that replaces dense attention heads with a routing-based sparse expert layer. S-MoEA dynamically selects top-$k$ expert heads to process token interactions, effectively decoupling model capacity from computational cost. We formalize the routing objective as a constrained optimization problem and introduce a load-balancing auxiliary loss to ensure expert specialization. Empirical evaluations on the GovReport and Multi-News datasets demonstrate that S-MoEA achieves competitive ROUGE scores while reducing floating-point operations (FLOPs) by 42% compared to state-of-the-art long-document baselines. We provide a theoretical analysis of the routing stability and demonstrate that our approach maintains performance parity with dense Transformers while scaling to sequence lengths of 32k tokens.

**Keywords:** Sparse Attention, Mixture-of-Experts, Long-Document Summarization, Transformer Efficiency, Dynamic Routing.

---

### 1. Introduction
The emergence of large-scale Transformer architectures has catalyzed significant advancements in natural language processing [1]. However, the canonical self-attention mechanism, characterized by $O(n^2)$ computational and memory complexity with respect to sequence length $n$, remains the primary impediment to processing ultra-long documents [2], [3]. While recent sparse attention variants—such as BigBird and Longformer—mitigate this through localized windows and global tokens, they often suffer from a static trade-off between representational depth and computational overhead [4], [5].

Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models have successfully addressed the scalability of parameter counts by activating only a subset of network parameters per forward pass [6], [7]. Despite these successes, existing MoE implementations primarily target feed-forward network (FFN) layers, leaving the attention mechanism—the core engine of context modeling—largely dense and computationally expensive [8], [9]. Consequently, long-document summarization, which requires dense semantic modeling over thousands of tokens, remains suboptimal in current architectures [10].

In this paper, we bridge this gap by proposing Sparse Mixture-of-Experts Attention (S-MoEA). We posit that not all attention heads are equally critical for all document segments; thus, we introduce a routing mechanism that assigns specific attention experts to token clusters based on syntactic and semantic entropy. Our contributions are threefold: (i) we define a novel sparse attention routing protocol; (ii) we derive a theoretical bound on the routing efficiency of the expert head selection; and (iii) we conduct a comprehensive experimental evaluation, demonstrating superior efficiency-accuracy trade-offs on long-document benchmarks.

---

### 2. Related Work
The literature on long-sequence processing is primarily categorized into three paradigms: global sparse attention, hierarchical modeling, and recurrent state compression. Sparse attention models, such as Reformer [11] and Sparse Transformer [12], reduce the attention matrix via hashing or fixed patterns, though they often exhibit degradation in performance on complex summarization tasks. Hierarchical models, exemplified by HAT [13] and HIBERT [14], segment documents into hierarchical structures, which can lose global coherence.

MoE architectures represent the state-of-the-art in parameter-efficient scaling [15], [16]. Switch Transformers [17] pioneered the use of sparse routing within FFN blocks, and subsequent works like ST-MoE [18] improved training stability through expert capacity balancing. However, applying MoE to attention heads has been limited by the non-differentiable nature of discrete routing. Differentiable routing techniques, such as Soft-TopK [19] and Sparse-Attention-MoE [20], represent recent attempts to solve this, yet they often fail to account for the document-level structure required in summarization tasks.

Our proposed S-MoEA differs from previous approaches by introducing a context-aware gating mechanism that modulates the sparse attention pattern based on the document's latent representation. Unlike standard sparse models that use static patterns [21], [22], S-MoEA adapts the sparsity degree based on the input sequence entropy, a strategy previously explored in limited capacities within non-attention contexts [23], [24], [25].

---

### 3. Background and Preliminaries
Let $\mathbf{X} = \{x_1, \dots, x_n\}$ denote a sequence of $n$ tokens. The canonical self-attention is defined as $\text{Attn}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{softmax}(\frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}})\mathbf{V}$. In long-document scenarios where $n > 8192$, the $O(n^2)$ complexity leads to prohibitive memory consumption. 

We assume the existence of a set of $E$ specialized attention experts $\{\mathcal{A}_1, \dots, \mathcal{A}_E\}$. In our framework, we decompose the attention operation into a sparse selection process. Let $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{X}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times E}$ be a gating function that computes the assignment of tokens to experts. The sparse attention output is defined as:
$$\text{S-MoEA}(\mathbf{X}) = \sum_{e=1}^E \text{G}_e(\mathbf{X}) \cdot \text{Attn}_e(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V})$$
where $\text{G}_e(\mathbf{X})$ serves as the routing probability for expert $e$. To maintain computational efficiency, we enforce $\sum_{e=1}^E \mathbb{I}(\text{G}_e(\mathbf{X}) > 0) \leq k$, where $k \ll E$.

---

### 4. Methodology: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts Attention (S-MoEA)
The core of S-MoEA lies in the **Entropy-Guided Router (EGR)**. The EGR computes a token-wise importance score $\mathbf{s}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and passes it through a linear projection $W_g$ to produce logit scores for $E$ experts. To ensure efficient gradient flow, we employ the Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization trick during training:
$$p_{i,e} = \frac{\exp((\text{log}(\pi_{i,e}) + g_{i,e})/\tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^E \exp((\text{log}(\pi_{i,j}) + g_{i,j})/\tau)}$$
where $\pi$ is the learned router parameter, $g$ is Gumbel noise, and $\tau$ is the temperature.

#### 4.1 Routing Objective
To prevent expert collapse, where the router assigns all tokens to a single expert, we introduce an auxiliary load-balancing loss $\mathcal{L}_{aux}$:
$$\mathcal{L}_{aux} = \alpha \sum_{e=1}^E f_e \cdot P_e$$
where $f_e$ is the fraction of tokens routed to expert $e$ and $P_e$ is the average routing probability. This penalty ensures that the load is distributed across the expert space, maintaining parameter diversity.

#### 4.2 Algorithm Pseudocode
The S-MoEA layer integrates into a standard Transformer block.

```python
Algorithm 1: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts Attention
Input: Hidden states X, Expert set {A_1, ..., A_E}, Top-k
1: Compute routing logits: L = Linear_router(X)
2: Select top-k experts: indices = TopK(L, k)
3: For e in indices:
4:    Attn_out_e = Attention_head_e(X)
5:    Assign weight w_e = L[e] / sum(L[indices])
6: Return sum(w_e * Attn_out_e)
```

---

### 5. Theoretical Analysis
**Theorem 1 (Complexity).** *The computational complexity of S-MoEA is $O(n \cdot (k \cdot d_h + n_c))$, where $n_c$ is the constant cost of routing and $k$ is the number of active experts. Given $k \ll E$, the complexity approaches linear scaling relative to the sequence length.*

*Proof:* Each token interacts only with $k$ experts. For each token, the routing operation is an $O(E)$ matrix multiplication, and the sparse attention is $O(n \cdot d_h)$. Thus, the total complexity is $O(n \cdot (E + k \cdot d_h))$. As $E$ is a fixed architectural hyperparameter, the scaling behavior is dominated by the linear term in $n$.

---

### 6. Experiments
We evaluate S-MoEA on two long-document summarization benchmarks: GovReport ($n \approx 10k$) and Multi-News ($n \approx 5k$).

#### 6.1 Experimental Setup
Models were implemented in PyTorch and trained on 8x A100 GPUs. We utilized a base Transformer-Large architecture, replacing the attention heads with S-MoEA modules ($E=16, k=2$). Hyperparameters: learning rate $5 \times 10^{-5}$, batch size 32, $\tau=0.5$.

#### 6.2 Results
Table 1 summarizes the ROUGE performance. S-MoEA-Large achieves comparable results to dense baselines while utilizing significantly fewer FLOPs.

**Table 1: ROUGE-L and Efficiency Comparison**
| Model | ROUGE-L | FLOPs (T) | Params (B) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Longformer [5] | 34.2 | 4.8 | 0.5 |
| BigBird [4] | 35.1 | 4.6 | 0.5 |
| HAT [13] | 36.8 | 5.2 | 0.7 |
| S-MoEA (Proposed) | 37.5 | 2.8 | 0.8 |

**Table 2: Scaling Analysis on GovReport**
| Experts ($k$) | Speedup (vs Dense) | ROUGE-L |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 2.4x | 35.8 |
| 2 | 1.8x | 37.5 |
| 4 | 1.2x | 37.9 |

---

### 7. Ablation Study
We analyze the contribution of the routing mechanism. Removing the auxiliary loss $\mathcal{L}_{aux}$ resulted in an expert collapse where 80% of tokens were routed to two experts, causing a 2.1 point drop in ROUGE-L.

**Table 3: Component Ablation**
| Component | ROUGE-L |
| :--- | :--- |
| Full S-MoEA | 37.5 |
| w/o Aux Loss | 35.4 |
| w/o Routing (Static) | 33.9 |

---

### 8. Discussion
The experimental results confirm that S-MoEA effectively manages the computational burden of long-document summarization. The primary insight is that attention heads specialize; our analysis of expert gate activations reveals that certain experts prioritize local syntactic structure, while others focus on document-level semantic cohesion. This functional specialization allows the model to compress the attention space without losing vital summarization performance.

Furthermore, the trade-off between $k$ (number of active experts) and accuracy suggests that for most summarization tasks, $k=2$ is sufficient, providing a sweet spot in the Pareto frontier between efficiency and precision. The observed performance degradation in static routing (Table 3) underscores the necessity of dynamic, input-dependent assignment.

Finally, we observe that S-MoEA scales gracefully as document length increases. While standard Transformers exhaust GPU memory at 16k tokens, S-MoEA maintains a stable memory profile up to 32k tokens, enabling the processing of high-density technical reports that were previously inaccessible to baseline models.

---

### 9. Limitations and Future Work
Despite its efficiency, S-MoEA introduces a non-trivial training overhead due to the necessity of balancing expert loads. The Gumbel-Softmax temperature $\tau$ requires careful scheduling; if $\tau$ is too low, the router becomes deterministic, leading to sub-optimal gradient propagation. Future work will investigate reinforcement learning-based routing policies to replace the differentiable approximation.

Additionally, while S-MoEA improves inference speed, the memory footprint remains tied to the total number of experts loaded in VRAM. Future architectures may consider offloading experts to CPU or storage, though this introduces latency concerns. Investigating the quantization of expert attention weights could further minimize this footprint.

Finally, we acknowledge that S-MoEA has not yet been tested on multi-modal long-document tasks. Extending the routing mechanism to handle vision-language inputs remains an open research direction that could unlock potential in broader document understanding domains.

---

### 10. Conclusion
We have presented S-MoEA, a sparse Mixture-of-Experts approach to attention mechanisms for long-document summarization. By dynamically routing token interactions through specialized attention heads, S-MoEA achieves a superior efficiency-accuracy trade-off. Our theoretical and empirical results demonstrate that sparse routing is a viable pathway for scaling Transformers to extreme sequence lengths, providing a robust architecture for future NLP research.

---

### References
[1] A. Vaswani, N. Shazeer, et al., "Attention is all you need," in Proc. NeurIPS, Long Beach, 2017, pp. 5998-6008.
[2] J. Devlin, M. Chang, et al., "BERT: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers," in Proc. NAACL-HLT, Minneapolis, 2019, pp. 4171-4186.
[3] Y. Liu, M. Ott, et al., "RoBERTa: A robustly optimized BERT pretraining approach," arXiv preprint arXiv:1907.11692, 2019.
[4] M. Zaheer, G. Guruganesh, et al., "Big Bird: Transformers for longer sequences," in Proc. NeurIPS, Virtual, 2020, pp. 17283-17297.
[5] I. Beltagy, M. Peters, et al., "Longformer: The long-document transformer," arXiv preprint arXiv:2004.05150, 2020.
[6] N. Shazeer, A. Mirhoseini, et al., "Outrageously large neural networks: The sparsely-gated mixture-of-experts layer," in Proc. ICLR, Toulon, 2017.
[7] D. Lepikhin, H. Lee, et al., "GShard: Scaling giant models with conditional computation and automatic sharding," in Proc. ICLR, Virtual, 2021.

# Neuro-Symbolic Logic-Guided Decoding: Enhancing Commonsense Inference in Open-Domain Question Answering

**Authors:**  
Dr. Elena R. Vance¹, Dr. Marcus T. Thorne², Dr. Sarah J. Chen³  
¹Department of Computer Science, Stanford University  
²Institute for Artificial Intelligence, MIT  
³Google Research, Mountain View  

---

### I. Introduction
The pursuit of human-level reasoning in open-domain question answering (QA) has long been hampered by the "commonsense gap." While large language models (LLMs) demonstrate remarkable statistical fluency, they frequently falter when faced with tasks requiring multi-hop deductive reasoning or the application of latent physical or social world knowledge [1], [2]. Current neural architectures rely on implicit representations derived from massive-scale pre-training, which, while effective for pattern matching, lack the structural rigor required for consistent logical inference [3], [4].

In this paper, we propose the **Neuro-Symbolic Logic-Guided Decoder (NS-LGD)**, a novel framework that integrates formal symbolic knowledge graphs (KGs) with neural generative models via a constrained decoding objective. Unlike previous neuro-symbolic hybrids that utilize post-hoc retrieval or simple prompt engineering [5], [6], our method incorporates a differentiable logic layer directly into the beam search process. By enforcing consistency between candidate tokens and the axioms derived from structured knowledge bases, we constrain the model's output space to logically plausible trajectories.

Our approach addresses three primary challenges: (1) the grounding of implicit neural knowledge into explicit symbolic facts, (2) the mitigation of hallucinated reasoning paths, and (3) the computational efficiency of symbolic verification during autoregressive generation. We demonstrate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks requiring non-trivial commonsense reasoning, while maintaining high generation quality.

---

### II. Related Work
The landscape of commonsense reasoning in NLP has evolved from static knowledge-base queries to dynamic neural reasoning [7], [8]. Early attempts at incorporating symbolic knowledge involved augmenting neural inputs with retrieved triples from databases like ConceptNet or ATOMIC [9], [10]. However, these approaches often suffer from "knowledge-noise," where irrelevant triples degrade model performance [11], [12].

Recent advancements in neuro-symbolic integration have shifted toward architectural modifications. Approaches such as Neural-Symbolic VQA and Logic-Tensor Networks have laid the groundwork for differentiable reasoning [13], [14]. Nevertheless, these methods often lack the scalability required for open-domain QA, as they struggle with the sparsity of symbolic representations [15], [16].

Furthermore, the emergence of chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting has shown that LLMs can perform multi-step reasoning if prompted correctly [17], [18]. Yet, these models remain prone to "logical drift," where the reasoning path deviates from factual constraints [19], [20]. Our proposed NS-LGD directly addresses this by introducing a symbolic regularizer during the decoding phase, effectively bridging the gap between probabilistic generation and formal logical verification [21], [22].

---

### III. Background / Preliminaries
We define the open-domain QA task as finding an answer $a$ given a question $q$. An LLM $P_\theta(y|q)$ predicts a reasoning sequence $y = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n\}$. In a neuro-symbolic setting, we assume access to a symbolic knowledge graph $\mathcal{G} = (V, E)$, where $V$ denotes entities and $E$ denotes relations.

The objective is to maximize the likelihood of a sequence that is consistent with $\mathcal{G}$. We define a logic function $L(y, \mathcal{G}) \in \{0, 1\}$ that evaluates whether the sequence $y$ is supported by the facts in $\mathcal{G}$. The standard autoregressive objective is:
$$ \mathcal{J}(\theta) = \sum_{(q, a)} \log P_\theta(a|q) $$
To incorporate symbolic knowledge, we introduce a constraint term based on the logical density of the sequence:
$$ \mathcal{J}_{ns}(\theta) = \sum_{(q, a)} \left[ \log P_\theta(a|q) + \lambda \cdot \mathcal{F}(y, \mathcal{G}) \right] $$
where $\mathcal{F}$ is a differentiable proxy for logical consistency and $\lambda$ is a temperature hyperparameter.

---

### IV. Methodology / Proposed Approach
The NS-LGD architecture consists of three core components: the **Neural Encoder-Decoder**, the **Symbolic Fact Retriever**, and the **Logic-Guided Decoder**.

1. **Symbolic Fact Retriever:** Given an input $q$, the retriever extracts a subgraph $g_q \subset \mathcal{G}$ using a dense retrieval mechanism [23]. This ensures that the symbolic context is relevant to the query.
2. **Logic-Guided Decoder:** During generation, the model maintains a belief state $B_t$ representing the current logical path. At each step $t$, the decoder computes a distribution $P(t|y_{<t}, g_q)$.
3. **Consistency Constraint:** We apply a masking function $M(t)$ that penalizes tokens that violate the connectivity in $g_q$.

**Algorithm 1: NS-LGD Decoding Process**
```python
Input: Question q, KG G, Model P, Threshold tau
Output: Sequence y
1. Subgraph g = RetrieveSubgraph(q, G)
2. y = []
3. for t in range(max_len):
4.   logits = P.forward(y)
5.   scores = compute_logic_scores(logits, g) # Differentiable layer
6.   p_t = softmax(logits + alpha * scores)
7.   token = sample(p_t)
8.   y.append(token)
9. return y
```
The complexity of the decoding step is $O(K \cdot |V_{sub}|)$, where $K$ is the beam width and $|V_{sub}|$ is the size of the retrieved subgraph, making it computationally feasible for real-time inference [24], [25].

---

### V. Theoretical Analysis
We claim that NS-LGD reduces the variance of reasoning paths by constraining the search space to the manifold of the knowledge graph. 

**Theorem 1.** *Let $\mathcal{H}$ be the entropy of the output distribution. With the logic-guided decoder, the constrained entropy $\mathcal{H}_{NS} \leq \mathcal{H}_{orig}$ for any query $q$ with non-empty $g_q$.*

*Proof Sketch:* Since the masking function $M(t)$ zero-out probability mass for tokens that lack a logical path to the previous entity $e_{t-1}$ in $\mathcal{G}$, the support of the distribution $P$ is restricted. By Jensen’s inequality, the reduction in support monotonically decreases the expected information entropy of the sequence, thereby reducing the probability of logical hallucination.

This framework assumes that the symbolic graph $\mathcal{G}$ is a complete representation of the domain. In practice, we utilize a soft-logic approach where $\mathcal{F}$ weights edges by their confidence score, mitigating the impact of KG incompleteness.

---

### VI. Experiments

#### Datasets
We evaluate on three benchmarks: **CommonsenseQA (CSQA)**, **StrategyQA**, and **OpenBookQA**. These datasets require multi-hop reasoning over implicit knowledge.

#### Baselines
We compare against: (1) GPT-3.5, (2) LLaMA-2-7B, (3) CoT-Prompting, (4) ReAct, and (5) Graph-Augmented LLMs.

#### Experimental Setup
Models were fine-tuned using a learning rate of $2 \times 10^{-5}$ with a batch size of 32. The logic weight $\lambda$ was tuned via grid search in the range $[0.1, 1.0]$.

**Table 1: Accuracy Comparison on Commonsense Benchmarks**
| Model | CSQA (Acc) | StrategyQA (Acc) | OpenBookQA (Acc) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| GPT-3.5 | 68.2 | 62.4 | 71.5 |
| LLaMA-2 | 65.4 | 59.8 | 68.9 |
| CoT-Prompting | 71.1 | 67.2 | 74.3 |
| ReAct | 72.8 | 69.5 | 76.1 |
| **NS-LGD (Ours)** | **76.4** | **73.1** | **79.8** |

---

### VII. Ablation Study
We isolate the impact of the symbolic constraint and the retrieval mechanism.

**Table 2: Ablation Results (CSQA Performance)**
| Configuration | Accuracy | $\Delta$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Full NS-LGD | 76.4 | - |
| No Logic Constraint | 71.2 | -5.2 |
| Random Subgraph | 69.4 | -7.0 |
| W/O Knowledge Graph | 66.8 | -9.6 |

---

### VIII. Discussion / Insights
The experimental results underscore that the integration of symbolic constraints significantly stabilizes the reasoning process. While base models like LLaMA-2 demonstrate strong language modeling capabilities, they frequently prioritize linguistic probability over logical consistency. The observed improvements in Table 1 suggest that the NS-LGD framework forces the model to adhere to the underlying facts, effectively pruning the search space.

One notable insight is the model's resilience to noisy inputs. In cases where the retrieved subgraph contains redundant nodes, the differentiable logic layer effectively assigns lower weights to these paths. This demonstrates that neuro-symbolic integration acts as a form of "logical regularization," preventing the model from over-relying on spurious correlations in the pre-training data.

Furthermore, the computational overhead is minimal. Because the constraint layer operates on the logits during the autoregressive step, the latency increase is less than 15% compared to standard decoding, rendering the approach suitable for practical applications.

---

### IX. Limitations & Future Work
Despite the performance gains, NS-LGD is inherently dependent on the quality and coverage of the underlying knowledge graph. If the KG lacks the necessary relations to support a specific reasoning step, the decoder may become over-constrained, leading to a "forced" incorrect prediction. Future work will focus on dynamic KG expansion, where the model can autonomously learn to add new relations to $\mathcal{G}$ based on high-confidence neural inferences.

Another limitation is the reliance on the retrieval module. While dense retrieval methods have advanced significantly, they are still prone to missing relevant contexts in highly specialized domains. We intend to explore hierarchical retrieval architectures that can navigate larger graphs more efficiently.

Finally, we acknowledge that the formal logic implemented is currently restricted to first-order connectivity. Extending this to higher-order logical predicates and temporal reasoning remains an open challenge that we plan to address in subsequent iterations of this framework.

---

### X. Conclusion
This paper introduced the Neuro-Symbolic Logic-Guided Decoder, a framework designed to bridge the chasm between neural fluency and symbolic rigor. By integrating a differentiable logic layer into the decoding process, we have demonstrated a robust method for improving commonsense inference in open-domain QA. Our empirical results validate the efficacy of this approach, showing consistent improvements across multiple benchmarks. As we continue to refine the interplay between neural and symbolic systems, we anticipate that NS-LGD will serve as a foundational architecture for more reliable and interpretable AI agents.

---

# Debiasing Large Language Models via Orthogonal Subspace Contrastive Fine-Tuning (OSC-FT)

**Authors:**  
Elena V. Sterling, Department of Computer Science, Stanford University  
Marcus J. Thorne, AI Research Institute, ETH Zürich  
Li Wei, School of Data Science, Tsinghua University  

---

### Abstract
Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across various cognitive tasks, yet they frequently perpetuate demographic biases present in their pre-training corpora. Existing mitigation strategies, such as post-hoc debiasing or supervised fine-tuning, often suffer from a catastrophic trade-off between fairness and downstream task utility. In this work, we propose Orthogonal Subspace Contrastive Fine-Tuning (OSC-FT), a novel framework that encourages models to learn demographic-invariant representations by projecting hidden states onto a manifold orthogonal to a learned bias-subspace. We introduce a contrastive loss objective that penalizes the alignment between sensitive demographic attributes and latent token projections. Our experiments on the CrowS-Pairs and StereoSet benchmarks demonstrate that OSC-FT achieves a 14.2% reduction in stereotypical association scores while maintaining 98.5% of the performance on standard GLUE benchmarks compared to vanilla fine-tuned baselines. We provide a theoretical analysis of the gradient dynamics of OSC-FT, demonstrating that the objective effectively prevents gradient interference between utility-maximizing features and bias-sensitive dimensions.

**Keywords:** Fairness in Machine Learning, Large Language Models, Contrastive Learning, Representation Learning, Algorithmic Bias.

---

### Introduction
The deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) in sensitive domains—such as legal reasoning, healthcare diagnosis, and recruitment—has rendered the mitigation of demographic bias a critical imperative. Despite the efficacy of Transformer-based architectures, these models inherently capture spurious correlations between demographic identifiers (e.g., gender, race, religion) and semantic contexts [1]. Recent studies indicate that these associations are not merely artifacts of data distribution but are deeply embedded within the high-dimensional latent space of the models [2].

Existing fairness interventions, particularly those relying on adversarial debiasing, often struggle with the "fairness-utility trade-off," where the removal of biased information results in a degradation of semantic reasoning capabilities [3]. This phenomenon is frequently attributed to the inextricable coupling of linguistic features and demographic signals, where standard regularization techniques fail to disentangle orthogonal components of the feature space [4].

In this paper, we introduce Orthogonal Subspace Contrastive Fine-Tuning (OSC-FT). Unlike traditional approaches that attempt to mask or remove sensitive attributes, OSC-FT identifies the subspace span of bias indicators through a lightweight projection layer and employs a contrastive objective to rotate the hidden representations away from this subspace. Our contribution is threefold:
1. We define a geometric framework for identifying demographic bias subspaces in LLMs.
2. We propose the OSC-FT loss, which facilitates representation invariance without sacrificing model capacity.
3. We empirically validate the method across multiple bias benchmarks, showing superior retention of task-specific knowledge compared to existing state-of-the-art approaches.

---

### Related Work
The landscape of fairness in NLP has evolved from simple dictionary-based debiasing to complex representation learning techniques. Bolukbasi et al. [5] initiated this field by demonstrating that word embeddings contain gender-biased vectors that can be neutralized via linear projection. However, these methods are insufficient for contextualized models like BERT or GPT-based architectures [6].

Adversarial training represents the current paradigm in fairness research [7]. In this setting, a discriminator attempts to predict demographic attributes from the model’s internal states, while the primary model minimizes the discriminator’s accuracy [8]. While effective, these methods are notoriously unstable and require careful balancing of loss functions to prevent the collapse of linguistic features [9].

Contrastive learning has recently gained traction as a method for enhancing representation quality [10]. Recent extensions have applied contrastive objectives to fairness, aiming to minimize the distance between representations of synonymous sentences that vary only by sensitive attribute [11]. Our work builds upon this by explicitly incorporating orthogonal subspace constraints, ensuring that the contrastive signal does not disrupt the primary task manifold.

---

### Background and Preliminaries
Let $\mathcal{X}$ be the input space and $\mathcal{Y}$ the label space. Consider a Transformer-based model $f_\theta: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{H}$ with parameters $\theta$, where $\mathcal{H} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the hidden representation space. Let $S$ denote a demographic attribute variable. 

The goal of bias mitigation is to learn a representation $z = f_\theta(x)$ such that $z \perp S$. In our formulation, we define the bias subspace $B \subset \mathcal{H}$ as the set of directions in the hidden space that exhibit high mutual information with $S$. Formally, we seek an orthogonal projection matrix $P_\perp$ such that for any hidden state $h \in \mathcal{H}$, the projected state $h' = P_\perp h$ minimizes $I(h'; S)$, where $I$ denotes mutual information.

---

### Proposed Approach: OSC-FT
We propose the Orthogonal Subspace Contrastive Fine-Tuning (OSC-FT) framework. The architecture consists of a frozen or partially fine-tuned transformer and a learned projection matrix $W_p \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$ that identifies the bias subspace.

#### The Contrastive Objective
Given a pair of sentences $(x_i, x_j)$ which are semantically equivalent but differ in demographic markers, we define the OSC-FT loss as:
$$\mathcal{L}_{OSC} = \mathcal{L}_{CE} + \lambda \sum_{i,j} \max(0, \text{sim}(P_\perp z_i, P_\perp z_j) - \tau)$$
where $\mathcal{L}_{CE}$ is the standard cross-entropy task loss, $\text{sim}(\cdot, \cdot)$ is the cosine similarity, and $\tau$ is a margin hyperparameter. 

#### Algorithm 1: OSC-FT Training
```python
# Pseudocode for OSC-FT training loop
for batch in dataloader:
    x_pos, x_neg = sample_demographic_pairs(batch)
    z_pos = model(x_pos)
    z_neg = model(x_neg)
    
    # Project to orthogonal space
    proj_pos = project_orthogonal(z_pos, bias_subspace)
    proj_neg = project_orthogonal(z_neg, bias_subspace)
    
    loss = CrossEntropy(output, labels) + lambda * ContrastiveLoss(proj_pos, proj_neg)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
```

The computational complexity of OSC-FT is $O(N \cdot d^2)$, where $N$ is the sequence length and $d$ is the embedding dimension, making it highly efficient for integration into standard fine-tuning pipelines.

---

### Theoretical Analysis
**Theorem 1.** *Given a representation $z = z_{task} + z_{bias}$, where $z_{task}$ and $z_{bias}$ are orthogonal, the gradient of the contrastive loss $\mathcal{L}_{OSC}$ with respect to the bias component vanishes as the projection $P_\perp$ aligns with the true orthogonal complement of the bias manifold.*

*Proof Sketch.* Consider the gradient of the loss $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{OSC}$. By the chain rule, $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z'} \frac{\partial z'}{\partial \theta}$. Since $P_\perp$ acts as a linear filter, the components in the bias subspace are nulled. Thus, the update rule ignores the demographic signal, forcing the model to minimize the task loss using only information residing in $z_{task}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### Experiments

#### Experimental Setup
We evaluate OSC-FT using the RoBERTa-base architecture. We utilize the CrowS-Pairs and StereoSet datasets to measure bias. The GLUE benchmark is used to evaluate task utility. Hyperparameters include a learning rate of $2 \times 10^{-5}$, a batch size of 32, and $\lambda = 0.1$.

#### Results
Table 1 shows the comparison of OSC-FT against baseline models (Vanilla, Adversarial, Logit-Pairing, Dropout-Noise, and Debias-BERT).

| Method | CrowS-Pairs (Bias $\downarrow$) | GLUE (Avg $\uparrow$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Vanilla | 68.2% | 84.5 |
| Adversarial [12] | 62.1% | 81.2 |
| Logit-Pairing [13] | 61.5% | 80.8 |
| Dropout-Noise [14] | 63.8% | 82.1 |
| Debias-BERT [15] | 60.2% | 83.2 |
| **OSC-FT (Ours)** | **54.0%** | **83.9** |

---

### Ablation Study
We analyze the sensitivity of OSC-FT to the projection dimension $k$ and the margin $\tau$.

| Component | CrowS-Pairs | GLUE |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Full OSC-FT | 54.0% | 83.9 |
| w/o Contrastive | 64.2% | 84.1 |
| w/o Orthogonal Projection | 61.3% | 80.5 |

The results indicate that the orthogonal constraint is the primary driver of performance, preventing task degradation.

---

### Discussion
The empirical results confirm that OSC-FT successfully disentangles demographic markers from latent semantic features. Unlike adversarial methods, which often oscillate during training, our orthogonal projection approach provides a stable geometric guarantee for fairness. The slight drop in GLUE performance compared to the vanilla model suggests that some demographic information may be entangled with task-relevant syntax, though this is a necessary trade-off for reduced bias.

Furthermore, we observe that the model exhibits improved robustness to adversarial input perturbations. This suggests that the subspace projection acts as a regularizer, forcing the model to rely on more stable, invariant features rather than superficial correlations present in the training data.

Future work should investigate the scalability of this method to larger models, such as GPT-4 or Llama-3, where the memory overhead of the projection matrix might become significant. Additionally, exploring dynamic subspace learning—where the bias subspace is updated periodically during training—could offer further improvements in adaptivity.

---

### Limitations and Future Work
While OSC-FT demonstrates significant bias reduction, it is limited by the availability of high-quality, balanced contrastive pairs. If the dataset lacks sufficient coverage for intersectional identities, the model may fail to mitigate bias for underrepresented groups. Furthermore, the reliance on a linear projection assumption may be insufficient for highly non-linear bias manifolds, suggesting that future extensions should consider non-linear projection operators using kernel methods or MLP-based projectors.

The computational overhead of maintaining the projection matrix, although minor, may pose challenges for ultra-large-scale deployments. Future research will focus on low-rank approximations of the projection matrix and the potential for multi-head projections to capture multifaceted biases simultaneously.

Finally, we acknowledge that "fairness" is a socially constructed concept, and technical interventions cannot replace comprehensive ethical frameworks for AI development. We hope this work provides a robust foundation for future technical implementations of fairness in language modeling.

---

### Conclusion
In this paper, we introduced OSC-FT, a robust framework for mitigating demographic bias in LLMs. By combining contrastive objectives with orthogonal subspace projection, we effectively eliminate stereotypical associations without compromising downstream utility. Our experiments demonstrate a significant improvement over existing state-of-the-art baselines. We believe this geometric approach provides a scalable and theoretically grounded path toward more equitable AI systems.

---

### References
[1] T. Bolukbasi, K. W. Chang, J. Zou, "Man is to computer programmer as woman is to homemaker?," in Proc. NIPS, Barcelona, 2016, pp. 4349-4357.  
[2] N. K. Kushal, S. R. Garg, "Bias in latent spaces," in Proc. ICML, Vienna, 2020, pp. 543-552.  
[3] A. Wang, Y. Zhang, "The fairness-utility trade-off in NLP," in Proc. ACL, Bangkok, 2021, pp. 120-135.  
[4] J. Devlin, M. W. Chang, "BERT: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers," in Proc. NAACL, Minneapolis, 2019, pp. 4171-4186.  
[5] B. P. A. Lastname, "Geometric debiasing," in Proc. NeurIPS, Vancouver, 2018, pp. 112-125.  
[6] C. D. Smith, E. R. Jones, "Contextual bias detection," in Proc. EMNLP, Hong Kong, 2020, pp. 889-901.  
[7] M. H. Zhang, L. K. Wang, "Adversarial debiasing," in Proc. ICML, Long Beach, 2019, pp. 723-734.  
[8] K. P. Miller, T. R. Green, "Discriminator instability in NLP," in Proc. ACL, Florence, 2022, pp. 334-345.  
[9] S. V. Gupta, "Gradient interference in multi-task learning," in Proc. NeurIPS, Montreal, 2021, pp. 2023-2035.  
[10] T. Chen, S. Kornblith, "Simple framework for contrastive learning," in Proc. ICML, Virtual, 2020, pp. 1597-1607.  
[11] F. J. Rossi, "Contrastive fairness," in Proc. NeurIPS, New Orleans, 2022, pp. 990-1005.  
[12] H. Z. Liu, "Adversarial training for fairness," in Proc. AAAI, New York, 2020, pp. 112-118.  
[13] R. G. Patel, "Logit pairing methods," in Proc. ICLR, Addis Ababa, 2021, pp. 445-456.

[18] C. K. Davis, "Representation theory," in Proc. EMNLP, Singapore, 2023, pp. 789-801.

[20] G. H. Foster, "Orthogonal projections," in Proc. ICLR, Kigali, 2023, pp. 889-902.
