# Temporal-Contrastive Manifold Alignment (TCMA) for Self-Supervised Change Detection in Multitemporal Satellite Imagery

**Authors:**  
Dr. Elena R. Vance, Institute for Geospatial Intelligence, Zurich  
Dr. Marcus T. Thorne, Department of Computer Science, University of Munich  
Dr. Sarah J. Jenkins, Laboratory for Remote Sensing and AI, MIT  

---

### Abstract
Change detection (CD) in multitemporal satellite imagery remains a cornerstone of environmental monitoring and urban planning, yet it is significantly hindered by the reliance on large-scale, manually annotated datasets. Current self-supervised learning (SSL) paradigms often fail to capture the long-range spatio-temporal dependencies inherent in high-resolution multi-spectral data, leading to sensitivity towards phenological variations and sensor noise. In this work, we propose Temporal-Contrastive Manifold Alignment (TCMA), a novel self-supervised framework that learns robust representations by projecting bitemporal image pairs into a shared latent manifold via a learnable topological constraint. By integrating a multi-head temporal attention mechanism with a dual-stream encoder, TCMA effectively disentangles seasonal changes from structural land-cover alterations. We validate our approach on the Onera Satellite Change Detection (OSCD) and LEVIR-CD datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that TCMA outperforms state-of-the-art SSL baselines, achieving a 4.2% improvement in F1-score while maintaining computational efficiency during inference. Our analysis suggests that forcing latent alignment through topological regularization significantly mitigates the "false positive" problem prevalent in unsupervised change detection.

**Keywords:** Self-supervised learning, Change detection, Satellite imagery, Manifold alignment, Temporal attention, Computer vision.

---

### 1. Introduction
The advent of high-resolution remote sensing has provided an unprecedented volume of multitemporal Earth observation data, creating a critical demand for automated change detection (CD) systems [1]. While deep learning-based CD models have achieved remarkable performance in supervised settings, their practical deployment is often constrained by the prohibitive cost of expert-labeled annotations [2]. Consequently, research has increasingly pivoted toward self-supervised learning (SSL) as a means to harness the vast, unlabelled data archives available via platforms such as Sentinel-2 or Landsat-8 [3].

However, existing SSL approaches for CD, typically based on standard contrastive learning objectives, frequently collapse when confronted with the complex radiometric variations induced by atmospheric conditions and lighting cycles [4]. These models often fail to distinguish between semantic land-cover changes (e.g., new infrastructure) and transient variations (e.g., seasonal vegetation changes), resulting in suboptimal performance in real-world deployment scenarios [5]. The challenge, therefore, lies in developing representations that are invariant to environmental noise while remaining sensitive to structural change.

To address this, we introduce Temporal-Contrastive Manifold Alignment (TCMA). TCMA posits that bitemporal satellite imagery, despite pixel-level variance, shares an underlying geometric manifold in the latent space that remains invariant to transient spectral shifts. By incorporating a learnable topological constraint, our model forces the feature encoders to project temporal snapshots onto a shared space, effectively isolating persistent structural features. This framework not only bridges the gap between supervised and unsupervised performance but also provides interpretability through latent spatial mapping. We contribute a novel architecture, a theoretical justification for manifold alignment in high-dimensional spectral spaces, and an extensive experimental validation against established baselines.

---

### 2. Related Work
The landscape of change detection has been extensively surveyed in recent literature, moving from pixel-wise algebraic methods to deep semantic architectures [6]. Early approaches relied heavily on change vector analysis (CVA), which, while computationally lightweight, lacked the ability to model non-linear semantic relationships [7]. With the rise of convolutional neural networks (CNNs), architectures like U-Net and Siamese networks became the standard for supervised CD, leveraging local spatial context to identify localized changes [8].

Self-supervised learning has recently gained traction as a remedy for label scarcity. Methods such as SimCLR and MoCo have been adapted for remote sensing, typically by treating temporal snapshots as positive pairs in a contrastive loss framework [9]. However, as noted in [10], these methods struggle with the "temporal drift" problem, where the model interprets seasonal change as structural change. Addressing this, recent efforts have integrated attention mechanisms to weight temporal significance, though they often rely on heavy computational overhead [11].

Manifold alignment, while well-studied in dimensionality reduction, has seen limited application in multi-temporal remote sensing. Techniques such as Procrustes analysis have been used for cross-domain mapping, but they typically assume linear transformations, failing to capture the complexity of satellite image features [12]. Our approach, TCMA, extends these concepts into the deep learning domain, utilizing non-linear projections to align manifolds in a way that is robust to non-linear radiometric noise [13].

---

### 3. Background and Preliminaries
Let $X_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ and $X_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ be two satellite images captured at times $t_1$ and $t_2$. The objective of change detection is to identify a binary map $Y \in \{0, 1\}^{H \times W}$, where $Y_{i,j}=1$ denotes a change at pixel $(i,j)$. 

We define our encoders $f_\theta$ and $g_\phi$ as deep neural networks mapping $X_1$ and $X_2$ to latent representations $Z_1 = f_\theta(X_1)$ and $Z_2 = g_\phi(X_2)$, where $Z \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times w \times d}$. The goal of TCMA is to learn parameters $\{\theta, \phi\}$ such that the divergence between the manifolds $\mathcal{M}_1 = \{Z_1\}$ and $\mathcal{M}_2 = \{Z_2\}$ is minimized for static regions, while maximized for changed regions [14].

We operate under the assumption that the latent space possesses an intrinsic structure governed by the underlying topology of land-cover patches [15]. By utilizing a contrastive loss with a topological penalty, we ensure that the alignment is not purely pixel-based but semantic, which is vital for remote sensing applications where registration errors are common [16].

---

### 4. Methodology
The TCMA framework consists of three primary modules: a Dual-Stream Feature Extractor (DSFE), a Temporal Attention Block (TAB), and the Manifold Alignment Head (MAH).

#### 4.1 Dual-Stream Feature Extractor
The DSFE utilizes a ResNet-50 backbone with shared weights (optionally) to extract hierarchical features. Unlike standard encoders, we apply a spectral normalization layer to preserve the range of multi-spectral values:
$$ \hat{Z} = \sigma \left( \frac{Z - \mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}} \right) $$
where $\epsilon$ is a small constant for numerical stability.

#### 4.2 Temporal Attention Block
To focus on relevant change regions, the TAB processes the concatenated features of $Z_1$ and $Z_2$. It computes a spatial mask $A$ via:
$$ A = \text{softmax}\left( \frac{(W_q Z_1)(W_k Z_2)^T}{\sqrt{d_k}} \right) $$
This attention map prioritizes pixels that deviate from the expected temporal distribution.

#### 4.3 Manifold Alignment Loss
The core of our approach is the alignment loss $\mathcal{L}_{TCMA}$, defined as:
$$ \mathcal{L}_{TCMA} = \mathcal{L}_{NCE} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{topo} $$
where $\mathcal{L}_{NCE}$ is the Normalized Contrastive Estimation loss and $\mathcal{L}_{topo}$ is the topological constraint enforcing the preservation of local neighborhoods in the latent space [17]. We define the topological loss based on the persistent homology of the latent features:
$$ \mathcal{L}_{topo} = \sum_{i,j} | dist(z_{i,1}, z_{j,1}) - dist(z_{i,2}, z_{j,2}) | \cdot \mathbb{I}(Y_{ij}=0) $$
This forces static pixels to maintain their relative distances in the latent space across time.

---

### 5. Theoretical Analysis
**Theorem 1.** *Given a sufficiently deep encoder $f_\theta$, the topological constraint $\mathcal{L}_{topo}$ acts as a regularizer that bounds the spectral radius of the latent embedding space, effectively preventing the collapse of disparate land-cover classes during training.*

*Proof Sketch:* By enforcing the preservation of local neighbors, we constrain the Lipschitz constant of the mapping. As the Lipschitz constant is bounded, the mapping $f_\theta$ must preserve the topology of the underlying manifold. This implies that small perturbations in the input space (radiometric noise) result in bounded deviations in the latent space, thus enhancing stability [18].

**Computational Complexity:** The model operates at $O(N \cdot d^2)$ per forward pass, where $N$ is the number of pixels and $d$ the embedding dimension. The topological computation adds a marginal $O(N \log N)$ overhead due to the distance matrix calculation, which is mitigated by sparse sampling of the latent grid.

---

### 6. Experiments

#### 6.1 Datasets
We evaluate TCMA on:
1. **OSCD:** A dataset containing 24 pairs of multispectral images covering diverse urban areas [19].
2. **LEVIR-CD:** A large-scale building change detection dataset with 637 pairs of high-resolution images [20].

#### 6.2 Baselines
We compare against:
1. **CDNet [21]** (Supervised)
2. **SimCLR-CD [9]** (SSL)
3. **ChangeFormer [22]** (Transformer-based)
4. **Siam-NestedUNet [23]**
5. **BYOL-RS [24]** (SSL for Remote Sensing)

#### 6.3 Results
Tables 1 and 2 summarize the performance on the Onera and LEVIR-CD datasets respectively.

**Table 1: F1-score comparison on OSCD.**
| Model | F1-Score | Precision | Recall |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CDNet | 78.4 | 80.2 | 76.7 |
| SimCLR-CD | 72.1 | 71.5 | 72.8 |
| ChangeFormer | 81.3 | 82.1 | 80.5 |
| Siam-NestedUNet | 79.5 | 78.9 | 80.1 |
| BYOL-RS | 75.8 | 76.2 | 75.4 |
| **TCMA (Ours)** | **82.5** | **83.4** | **81.6** |

**Table 2: F1-score comparison on LEVIR-CD.**
| Model | F1-Score | Precision | Recall |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CDNet | 85.2 | 84.9 | 85.5 |
| SimCLR-CD | 78.9 | 77.5 | 80.3 |
| ChangeFormer | 87.8 | 88.2 | 87.4 |
| Siam-NestedUNet | 86.4 | 86.1 | 86.7 |
| BYOL-RS | 81.2 | 80.5 | 81.9 |
| **TCMA (Ours)** | **88.9** | **89.1** | **88.7** |

---

### 7. Ablation Study
We analyze the contribution of the temporal attention block (TAB) and the topological loss ($\mathcal{L}_{topo}$).

| Configuration | F1-Score | Improvement |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Baseline (No components) | 74.2 | - |
| Baseline + TAB | 79.8 | +5.6 |
| Baseline + $\mathcal{L}_{topo}$ | 78.5 | +4.3 |
| **TCMA (Full)** | **82.5** | **+8.3** |

The results indicate that while temporal attention is essential for capturing structural changes, the topological loss is critical for reducing false positives caused by illumination variations.

---

### 8. Discussion
The experimental findings suggest that the integration of manifold alignment significantly stabilizes the training process in self-supervised remote sensing. It is worth noting that traditional contrastive objectives often suffer from the "shortcut learning" problem, where the encoder identifies changes based purely on brightness gradients [25]. By contrast, TCMA’s topological constraint ensures that the latent space maintains a semantic consistency, even when the input signals are degraded by atmospheric haze or seasonal cycles.

Arguably, the most significant advantage of our approach is its robustness in urban settings. In the LEVIR-CD dataset, which features complex building geometries, TCMA effectively isolates construction changes from shadow variations. This confirms the hypothesis that forcing latent alignment through topological regularization mitigates the sensitivity to non-structural changes.

Furthermore, we observed that the performance of TCMA is relatively insensitive to the choice of hyper-parameters compared to standard contrastive methods. This stability is likely due to the inherent geometric constraints imposed by the topological loss, which restrict the search space during optimization. We posit that this framework could be extended to multi-modal imagery, such as fusing SAR and optical data, provided the manifolds are aligned via a shared latent space.

---

### 9. Limitations & Future Work
Despite its effectiveness, TCMA faces several limitations. First, the distance matrix calculation for $\mathcal{L}_{topo}$ is currently memory-intensive, limiting the batch size for very high-resolution imagery. Future work will investigate sparse manifold approximations, such as random projection forests, to reduce the computational complexity.

Second, the current implementation assumes a relatively clean registration between temporal pairs. While our attention mechanism provides some robustness, significant georegistration errors still result in high false-positive rates at the edges of buildings. Future iterations will incorporate a differentiable alignment module to handle sub-pixel displacement.

Finally, we intend to explore the generalizability of TCMA to global-scale monitoring tasks, where cross-continental data variability poses a significant challenge for existing SSL models. By incorporating domain-adversarial training into our manifold alignment framework, we hope to achieve a more universal representation for Earth observation.

---

### 10. Conclusion
This paper presented TCMA, a self-supervised framework for change detection in multitemporal satellite imagery. By combining temporal attention with topological manifold alignment, we demonstrated that it is possible to learn robust feature representations without explicit labels. Our experimental results on OSCD and LEVIR-CD confirm that TCMA offers a significant performance boost over existing SSL baselines, effectively balancing accuracy with stability. We believe this work provides a foundational step towards scalable, automated environmental monitoring using the vast quantities of unlabeled satellite data currently being generated.

---

# Adaptive Deformable Feature Alignment for Anchor-Free Object Detection

**Authors:**
Elena V. Sterling$^1$, Marcus Thorne$^2$, and Julian A. Vance$^3$
$^1$Department of Computer Science, Stanford University; $^2$Institute for Robotics and AI, ETH Zurich; $^3$Vision Systems Lab, MIT CSAIL

---

### Abstract
Anchor-free object detection paradigms have gained significant traction due to their design simplicity and reduced hyperparameter sensitivity. However, current architectures often struggle with the misalignment between static convolutional kernels and the varying geometric topologies of objects, particularly in high-aspect-ratio scenarios. In this paper, we introduce the Deformable Feature Pyramid Network (DFPN), a novel framework that integrates learnable geometric offsets into the Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) hierarchy. By substituting standard convolutional layers with Deformable Convolutional v3 (DCNv3) blocks, we allow the feature extraction backbone to dynamically adapt its receptive field to the spatial distribution of object instances. We define a modulation mechanism that optimizes the alignment of high-level semantic features with spatial localizations. Extensive experiments on the COCO detection benchmark demonstrate that DFPN achieves a competitive mAP of 48.2 while maintaining computational efficiency comparable to standard FPN-based detectors. We analyze the trade-offs between deformation overhead and detection precision, providing insights into the optimal placement of deformable layers within the hierarchy.

### Keywords
Object Detection, Anchor-Free, Deformable Convolutions, Feature Pyramids, Computer Vision.

---

### Introduction
Object detection remains a foundational challenge in computer vision, serving as a prerequisite for autonomous driving, robotics, and surveillance systems [1], [2]. Historically, detection pipelines relied on predefined anchor boxes to establish spatial priors, a methodology that introduces non-trivial hyperparameter complexity and sensitivity to scale distributions [3], [4]. The advent of anchor-free detection methods, such as FCOS [5] and CenterNet [6], has demonstrated that defining objects as keypoints or center-regions can simplify the architecture and improve generalization across diverse object geometries.

Despite this progress, a persistent bottleneck in anchor-free detectors is the rigid nature of the convolutional kernels used in the feature extraction backbone [7]. Standard convolutional layers operate on a fixed square grid, which fails to capture the intricate spatial variance of irregular, elongated, or multi-part objects [8]. While Feature Pyramid Networks (FPN) successfully mitigate scale variations by constructing a multi-scale feature hierarchy [9], the semantic features at each level remain constrained by the spatially invariant nature of the learned weights.

To address this, we propose the Deformable Feature Pyramid Network (DFPN). Unlike previous approaches that treat feature pyramids as static representations, DFPN incorporates deformable convolutional primitives at each pyramid stage. This allows the network to learn spatially adaptive offsets that align the receptive field to the object's foreground pixels. This paper makes three primary contributions: (1) we formulate a spatially-adaptive feature aggregation method for anchor-free pipelines; (2) we introduce a cross-scale modulation unit that computes deformable offsets based on both local features and global context; and (3) we provide a rigorous analysis of the computational overhead introduced by deformable primitives, establishing a Pareto-optimal configuration for real-time deployment.

### Related Work
The literature on object detection is vast, spanning two-stage frameworks [10], [11] and single-stage anchor-based detectors [12], [13]. Anchor-free detectors emerged as a response to the heavy computation and complex matching heuristics required by RPN-based models [14], [15]. Models such as CornerNet [16] pioneered the keypoint-based localization approach, while FCOS [5] introduced a per-pixel prediction strategy that is now considered standard.

Deformable convolutions were originally proposed by Dai et al. [17] to address the rigid limitations of standard kernels. Subsequent refinements, such as DCNv2 [18] and the recent DCNv3 [19], have improved the stability and efficiency of these modules by incorporating modulation scalars and improved normalization techniques. However, the integration of these modules into hierarchical feature pyramids remains underexplored. Existing research has largely focused on using DCNs within the backbone or the head, rarely considering the structural modification of the FPN itself [20], [21].

Furthermore, attention-based mechanisms have been proposed to enhance feature alignment [22], [23], but these are often limited by memory constraints on high-resolution feature maps. Our work differentiates itself by focusing on the geometry of the convolution kernel as a primary driver of alignment, sidestepping the dense matrix multiplication overhead of traditional self-attention modules [24]. This approach aligns with recent trends in efficient vision transformers and backbone optimization [25].

### Background / Preliminaries
Let $\mathcal{F} = \{F_3, F_4, F_5, F_6, F_7\}$ denote the set of feature maps produced by the FPN. In a standard FPN, each level $F_l$ is computed via a concatenation of upsampled high-level features and lateral features from the backbone. A standard 2D convolution at position $p_0$ is defined as:
$$y(p_0) = \sum_{p_n \in \mathcal{R}} w(p_n) \cdot x(p_0 + p_n)$$
where $\mathcal{R}$ is the fixed grid. In our proposed DFPN, we introduce learnable offsets $\Delta p_n$, transforming the operation into:
$$y(p_0) = \sum_{p_n \in \mathcal{R}} w(p_n) \cdot x(p_0 + p_n + \Delta p_n)$$
where $\Delta p_n$ is learned through a sibling branch. This geometric flexibility allows the kernel to focus on the semantic contours of an object rather than strictly local spatial neighbors.

### Methodology / Proposed Approach
The DFPN architecture replaces the static $3 \times 3$ convolution layers in the FPN lateral connection modules with Deformable Convolutional v3 (DCNv3) blocks. The primary innovation lies in the *Adaptive Offset Modulation* (AOM) module.

**Algorithm 1: DFPN Feature Aggregation**
```python
Input: Feature map F_{in}, Upsampled map F_{up}
Output: Refined feature map F_{out}
1: Initialize DCNv3 layer with offset projection W_o
2: F_cat = concat(F_in, Upsample(F_up))
3: Offset_map = Conv(F_cat) # Learns geometric shifts
4: Mask_map = Sigmoid(Conv(F_cat)) # Learns importance
5: F_out = DCNv3(F_cat, Offset_map, Mask_map)
6: return F_out
```

The complexity of the DCNv3 operation is $O(K \cdot H \cdot W \cdot C^2)$, where $K$ is the number of sampling points. By restricting the offsets to a local neighborhood of $3 \times 3$, we maintain a complexity profile nearly identical to standard convolutions, while significantly improving the spatial receptive field's responsiveness to irregular object boundaries.

### Theoretical Analysis
Consider the variance of a feature response $V(y)$. In a static kernel, the variance is dependent solely on the input statistics. In DFPN, the variance is conditioned on the offset distribution:
$$V(y | \Delta p) = \mathbb{E}[|| \nabla_x y(p_0 + \Delta p) ||^2]$$
We hypothesize that by minimizing the misalignment error $\epsilon = || y_{fixed} - y_{deform} ||$, the model achieves a lower bias in localization. Since $\Delta p$ is learned to maximize IoU, the gradient flow from the localization head directly shapes the receptive field to align with the object's spatial extent.

### Experiments
We evaluate our model on the COCO 2017 detection dataset, utilizing ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 as backbones. Training follows the 1x schedule (12 epochs) with SGD, a base learning rate of 0.02, and weight decay of $10^{-4}$.

**Table 1: Comparison with State-of-the-Art**
| Method | Backbone | mAP | AP$_{50}$ | AP$_{75}$ | FPS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| FCOS [5] | R-50 | 41.2 | 60.5 | 44.1 | 18 |
| RetinaNet [12] | R-50 | 38.9 | 58.2 | 41.8 | 16 |
| ATSS [14] | R-50 | 43.6 | 62.1 | 47.4 | 15 |
| CenterNet [6] | DLA-34 | 41.9 | 61.0 | 45.1 | 22 |
| **DFPN (Ours)** | R-50 | **44.8** | **63.4** | **48.6** | 14 |

**Table 2: Scaling with Backbone Depth**
| Method | Backbone | mAP | AP$_{s}$ | AP$_{m}$ | AP$_{l}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| DFPN | R-50 | 44.8 | 26.1 | 48.9 | 58.2 |
| DFPN | R-101 | 48.2 | 29.3 | 52.4 | 62.1 |

### Ablation Study
We analyze the contribution of different stages of the FPN.

| Component | Offset | Modulation | mAP | $\Delta$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Baseline FPN | No | No | 41.2 | - |
| FPN + DCNv3 (P5) | Yes | Yes | 42.8 | +1.6 |
| FPN + DCNv3 (P3-P5) | Yes | Yes | 44.8 | +3.6 |

### Discussion / Insights
The experimental results underscore that geometric adaptation is most beneficial at lower pyramid levels (P3 and P4), where spatial resolution is high and objects occupy a smaller footprint. Our results show that while global context is vital, local feature alignment via deformation handles the occlusion cases often found in complex scenes.

Computational overhead is the primary limitation. While DCNv3 is optimized, the additional parameters for offset generation increase the memory footprint by approximately 12%. However, the precision gains—particularly in $AP_l$ (large objects)—justify this cost in high-accuracy scenarios.

Future iterations of DFPN could explore sparse offset learning to reduce the computational cost of the deformable branches, potentially through teacher-student distillation where the offset branch is pruned after training.

### Limitations & Future Work
The primary limitation is the increased training time, which stems from the non-optimized CUDA kernels for DCNv3 in standard frameworks. Furthermore, the reliance on dense offsets may introduce artifacts in extremely cluttered environments where local gradients are noisy. Future research will explore multi-head attention-based offset prediction to improve robustness in high-occlusion datasets.

### Conclusion
We presented the Deformable Feature Pyramid Network, a framework that enhances anchor-free detection through learnable geometric transformations. By integrating DCNv3 into the FPN, we bridge the gap between static feature representations and the dynamic shapes of objects in the wild. Our results on COCO confirm that this approach consistently improves detection precision, validating the importance of spatial adaptivity in modern vision architectures.

---

### References
[1] T. Lin, P. Dollar, R. Girshick, et al., "Feature pyramid networks for object detection," in Proc. CVPR, Honolulu, 2017, pp. 2117-2125.
[2] K. He, G. Gkioxari, P. Dollar, R. Girshick, "Mask R-CNN," in Proc. ICCV, Venice, 2017, pp. 2961-2969.
[3] S. Ren, K. He, R. Girshick, J. Sun, "Faster R-CNN: Towards real-time object detection," in Proc. NeurIPS, Montreal, 2015, pp. 91-99.
[4] J. Redmon, A. Farhadi, "YOLOv3: An incremental improvement," in Proc. CVPR, Salt Lake City, 2018, pp. 1-6.
[5] Z. Tian, C. Shen, H. Chen, T. He, "FCOS: Fully convolutional one-stage object detection," in Proc. ICCV, Seoul, 2019, pp. 9627-9636.
[6] K. Duan, S. Bai, L. Xie, H. Qi, Q. Huang, Q. Tian, "CenterNet: Keypoint triplets for object detection," in Proc. ICCV, Seoul, 2019, pp. 6569-6578.
[7] N. Wang, Y. Gao, H. Chen, "Revisiting feature alignment in anchor-free detectors," in Proc. WACV, Snowmass, 2021, pp. 102-110.

[9] A. Shrivastava, R. Sukthankar, "Scaling feature pyramids," in Proc. BMVC, London, 2018, pp. 45-56.

# Latent Topology-Aware Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Long-Horizon Robotic Manipulation

**Authors:**
Elena V. Sterling (Institute for Advanced Robotics, ETH Zurich), Marcus T. Thorne (Department of Computer Science, MIT), Aris Thorne (Robotics Research Lab, Stanford University)

---

### Abstract
Reinforcement Learning (RL) in high-dimensional robotics domains often falters under the burden of sparse rewards and long-horizon temporal dependencies. While Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (HRL) mitigates these challenges by decomposing complex tasks into sequences of sub-goals, existing frameworks frequently struggle with the semantic alignment of latent sub-goals and the underlying geometry of the task space. We propose *Latent Topology-Aware Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (LTA-HRL)*, a novel framework that integrates a topological graph-based representation of the state space into a manager-worker architecture. By leveraging a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to learn a manifold-constrained latent space and a Graph Neural Network (GNN) to predict transitions between sub-goal states, LTA-HRL enables the agent to navigate complex manipulation sequences with enhanced sample efficiency. We demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on multi-step object rearrangement tasks, achieving an improvement of approximately 14% in success rates over non-topological hierarchical baselines. Our theoretical analysis establishes that the topological constraint reduces the effective search space dimension, providing a formal justification for the observed acceleration in policy convergence.

**Keywords:** Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning, Robotics, Graph Neural Networks, Latent Manifolds, Long-Horizon Planning.

---

### Introduction
The challenge of autonomous robotic manipulation in unstructured environments remains a cornerstone problem in artificial intelligence. Traditional Reinforcement Learning (RL) paradigms, while theoretically elegant, often struggle with the "curse of horizon," where the probability of encountering a terminal reward state decays exponentially with the length of the required action sequence [1], [2]. Recent advancements in Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) have demonstrated success in localized motor control, yet complex multi-step tasks—such as sequential object assembly or dynamic tool manipulation—require a level of temporal abstraction that standard monolithic policies fail to provide [3], [4].

Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (HRL) has emerged as a promising solution, typically employing a two-tier structure consisting of a "manager" that dictates sub-goals and a "worker" that executes low-level primitives [5], [6]. However, the fundamental bottleneck in HRL remains the definition of the sub-goal space. If the sub-goal space is excessively granular, the manager faces an intractable search problem; conversely, if the space is too coarse, the worker cannot execute the required precise transitions [7], [8]. Existing approaches, such as those relying on random goal sampling or state-action clustering, frequently ignore the latent topological structure inherent in physical task spaces [9], [10].

In this paper, we propose *Latent Topology-Aware Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (LTA-HRL)*. Our core insight is that robotic manipulation tasks can be modeled as a graph, where nodes represent topologically distinct configurations and edges represent reachable transitions. By learning a VAE-based latent representation constrained by graph-theoretic properties, we decouple the high-level navigation of the task manifold from low-level execution [11], [12]. We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the "topology" of the task—rather than treating the state space as a flat Euclidean manifold—we can regularize the manager’s policy, ensuring it only selects feasible sub-goals.

The contributions of this work are three-fold: First, we introduce the LTA-HRL framework, which utilizes a Graph Neural Network (GNN) to guide the selection of latent sub-goals. Second, we provide a theoretical derivation showing that the topological bottleneck restricts the manager’s policy space, effectively reducing the variance of the gradient updates [13], [14]. Finally, we conduct extensive experiments on high-fidelity simulation benchmarks, demonstrating significant performance gains over state-of-the-art HRL and model-free baselines [15].

---

### Related Work
The literature on long-horizon manipulation is vast, spanning from classical motion planning to end-to-end deep learning. Classical approaches, such as Probabilistic Roadmaps (PRM) and Rapidly-exploring Random Trees (RRT), provide formal guarantees for path finding but often require explicit knowledge of the environment geometry [16], [17]. In contrast, data-driven HRL approaches aim to learn these structures directly from experience. The seminal work by Kulkarni et al. [18] introduced the concept of goal-conditioned hierarchies, which inspired subsequent developments in Feudal Networks [19] and Option-Critic architectures [20].

However, these frameworks often face issues with mode collapse and non-stationary worker objectives. To address this, researchers have explored unsupervised skill discovery, where agents learn a repertoire of primitives through mutual information maximization [21], [22]. While effective for navigation, these skill-based approaches frequently struggle with the contact-rich dynamics of manipulation. Our work is distinct in its utilization of latent topological constraints, which serves as a prior for sub-goal feasibility, an area previously explored in limited capacities by graph-based RL researchers [23], [24]. We extend these notions by proposing an integrated end-to-end training objective that co-optimizes the latent graph and the policy.

---

### Background and Preliminaries
We formalize the environment as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) defined by the tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma)$, where $\mathcal{S}$ is the continuous state space, $\mathcal{A}$ the action space, $\mathcal{P}$ the transition dynamics, $\mathcal{R}$ the reward function, and $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ the discount factor. In a hierarchical setting, the manager $\pi_M$ operates at a coarser timescale $\Delta t_M > 1$, outputting a sub-goal $g_t \in \mathcal{G} \subset \mathcal{S}$ every $\Delta t_M$ steps. The worker $\pi_W$ receives the sub-goal $g_t$ and executes primitive actions $a_t \sim \pi_W(a_t | s_t, g_t)$.

We assume the existence of a latent mapping $\phi: \mathcal{S} \to \mathcal{Z}$, where $\mathcal{Z}$ is a lower-dimensional manifold. Let $G = (V, E)$ be a directed graph where nodes $v \in V$ correspond to discretized latent states $z \in \mathcal{Z}$. The topological constraint requires that for any sub-goal $g_t$ proposed by the manager, there must exist a feasible path in $G$ from the current latent state $z_t$ to $\phi(g_t)$.

---

### Methodology
The LTA-HRL architecture consists of three core modules: (1) A VAE that maps states to a topological latent space, (2) A GNN-based transition model that predicts the feasibility of moving between nodes, and (3) A hierarchical policy architecture.

#### Latent Graph Construction
We utilize a VAE to learn the latent representation $z = \phi(s)$, optimized via the evidence lower bound (ELBO):
$$\mathcal{L}_{VAE} = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z|s)}[\log p_\theta(s|z)] - \beta D_{KL}(q_\phi(z|s) || p(z)) \quad (1)$$
where $\beta$ is a hyperparameter controlling the bottleneck. To ensure the latent space is topologically informative, we augment the training objective with a graph reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L}_{graph}$, ensuring that the distance between latent embeddings reflects the reachability of states in the original environment.

#### Manager and Worker Objectives
The manager is trained to maximize long-term rewards by selecting sub-goals $g_t$ that are "topologically reachable." The policy $\pi_M$ is conditioned on the graph state, outputting a distribution over nodes in $G$. The worker policy is trained to minimize the distance between the current state embedding $\phi(s_t)$ and the sub-goal embedding $\phi(g_t)$:
$$\mathcal{L}_W = \mathbb{E}_{s, g} [ || \phi(s_{t+\Delta t}) - \phi(g_t) ||^2_2 ] \quad (2)$$
The complete policy update for the manager incorporates a penalty for choosing sub-goals that lack a connecting path in $G$, defined by:
$$J_M = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_M} \left[ \sum_{k=0}^T \gamma^k r_k + \lambda \cdot \text{feasibility}(g_k, G) \right] \quad (3)$$
where $\lambda$ is a weight constant regulating the topological exploration.

#### Algorithm 1: LTA-HRL Training Procedure
1. Initialize VAE, GNN, and Hierarchical Policies.
2. **Repeat:**
3.   Collect trajectories using current $\pi_M, \pi_W$.
4.   Update VAE parameters using (1).
5.   Update GNN transition weights based on observed state-transitions.
6.   Update $\pi_W$ to reach sub-goals $g_t$.
7.   Update $\pi_M$ via policy gradient using (3).
8. **Until** convergence.

The computational complexity of the GNN update is $O(|V| + |E|)$, where $|V|$ is the number of latent nodes. Since $|V| \ll |\mathcal{S}|$, this approach is significantly more efficient than planning in raw state space.

---

### Theoretical Analysis
We claim that the topological constraint reduces the effective dimension of the policy search space. Let $\Sigma_M$ be the set of all possible policy updates for the manager. Without topological priors, $\Sigma_M = \mathcal{A}^H$. With the topological constraint, the manager is restricted to paths $P \in \text{Paths}(G)$, where $|\text{Paths}(G)| \ll |\mathcal{A}|^H$.

**Theorem 1:** The variance of the policy gradient estimate $\hat{\nabla} J_M$ is bounded by the sparsity of the graph $G$.
*Proof Sketch:* Given that the topological constraint masks non-feasible sub-goals, the probability mass assigned to high-cost actions in the policy distribution $\pi_M$ is reduced. As $|\text{Paths}(G)|$ decreases, the effective support of the gradient update shrinks, directly lowering the variance $\text{Var}(\hat{\nabla} J_M)$. This reduction in variance is proportional to the spectral gap of the transition matrix defined by $G$ [25].

---

### Experiments

#### Datasets and Setup
We evaluate LTA-HRL on three manipulation tasks: *Block Stacking*, *Nut-and-Bolt Assembly*, and *Drawer Manipulation*. We utilize the MuJoCo physics engine for high-fidelity simulation.

#### Baselines
We compare against:
1. **PPO:** Proximal Policy Optimization (standard model-free).
2. **SAC:** Soft Actor-Critic.
3. **HIRO:** Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning with Off-Policy Correction [6].
4. **HAC:** Hierarchical Actor-Critic.
5. **Skill-RL:** An unsupervised skill-discovery baseline.

#### Results
Table 1 shows the success rates across the three tasks after $10^6$ environment steps.

| Method | Block Stacking | Nut-Bolt Assembly | Drawer Manipulation |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| PPO | 0.22 | 0.15 | 0.31 |
| SAC | 0.28 | 0.19 | 0.38 |
| HIRO | 0.54 | 0.42 | 0.62 |
| HAC | 0.51 | 0.38 | 0.59 |
| LTA-HRL (Ours) | **0.69** | **0.58** | **0.75** |

Table 2: Comparison of sample efficiency (steps to reach 50% success).

| Method | Block Stacking | Nut-Bolt Assembly | Drawer Manipulation |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| HIRO | 850k | 920k | 780k |
| LTA-HRL | **410k** | **520k** | **390k** |

---

### Ablation Study
We analyze the contribution of the topological graph and the latent manifold constraint.

| Component | Success Rate (Avg) |
| :--- | :---: |
| Full LTA-HRL | 0.67 |
| Without Topological Graph | 0.48 |
| Without Latent VAE | 0.39 |
| Without Feasibility Penalty ($\lambda=0$) | 0.55 |

The results indicate that the topological graph is the primary driver of performance, contributing an 19% improvement, while the VAE mapping is essential for dealing with the high-dimensional input space.

---

### Discussion
The empirical results corroborate our theoretical claim that topological awareness is critical for long-horizon manipulation. By restricting the manager to valid sub-goal trajectories, LTA-HRL avoids the common pitfall of selecting "impossible" states that lead to catastrophic failures in the worker policy. It is worth noting that the performance gap between LTA-HRL and HIRO widens as the task complexity increases, suggesting that our approach scales more gracefully to multi-stage problems.

One observation worth exploring is the sensitivity of the hyperparameter $\beta$ in the VAE. We found that excessively small values of $\beta$ lead to latent spaces that are too entangled, causing the GNN to struggle with transition prediction. Conversely, high values of $\beta$ lead to overly compressed representations that lose the fine-grained details necessary for contact-rich assembly tasks. This suggests a delicate trade-off between semantic abstraction and task-specific fidelity.

Furthermore, we acknowledge that the construction of the latent graph is currently a static offline-to-online process. An interesting future direction would be to develop an adaptive graph-learning mechanism that refines the topology in real-time as the agent encounters novel state-action pairs. Such a framework could theoretically generalize to completely unseen environments, provided the state-space manifold retains similar topological properties.

---

### Limitations and Future Work
Despite the advantages of LTA-HRL, several limitations remain. First, the dependency on a pre-trained latent space necessitates a large buffer of exploratory data, which may be costly in real-world robotics. Second, the current implementation assumes a discrete graph structure for sub-goals; extending this to continuous topological structures (e.g., via topological data analysis or persistent homology) could provide a more robust basis for planning in highly non-linear environments.

Future work will focus on deploying LTA-HRL on physical hardware, specifically testing the robustness of our topological priors to sensor noise and external disturbances. Additionally, we aim to integrate large-language models (LLMs) to provide semantic guidance to the manager, potentially allowing the agent to perform tasks defined by natural language instructions within the established topological constraints.

---

### Conclusion
We have presented LTA-HRL, a framework for hierarchical reinforcement learning that leverages latent topological constraints to guide robotic manipulation. By integrating graph-based transition modeling with a hierarchical architecture, our method significantly improves sample efficiency and task success rates compared to existing baselines. Theoretical and empirical evidence suggests that our approach successfully addresses the curse of horizon by constraining the search space of the manager. We believe this work provides a solid foundation for more scalable and reliable autonomous agents capable of navigating the complexities of real-world physical tasks.

---

# Hierarchical Spatio-Temporal Graph Contrastive Learning for Supply Chain Anomaly Detection

**Authors:**
Elena V. Sterling, Department of Computer Science, MIT; Marcus J. Thorne, Institute for Data Systems, ETH Zurich; Sarah L. Jenkins, Department of Operations Research, Stanford University

---

### Abstract
Supply chain networks are increasingly susceptible to complex, cascading anomalies that evade traditional detection paradigms. We propose **ST-GCL (Spatio-Temporal Graph Contrastive Learning)**, a novel framework designed to capture intricate dependencies in temporal supply chain graphs. Unlike existing graph neural networks (GNNs) that rely on static structural priors, ST-GCL utilizes a hierarchical contrastive objective to learn invariant representations of nodal behaviors under both normal and disrupted states. By integrating a temporal attention mechanism with a dual-stream graph convolution architecture, the model effectively disentangles latent seasonal fluctuations from genuine anomalies. Experimental results on two large-scale supply chain datasets demonstrate that ST-GCL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving an average F1-score improvement of 6.4% in high-noise environments. We provide theoretical analysis regarding the information-theoretic bounds of our contrastive loss, demonstrating its efficacy in mitigating the over-smoothing problem inherent in deep GNNs.

**Keywords:** Graph Neural Networks, Supply Chain Analytics, Anomaly Detection, Contrastive Learning, Spatio-Temporal Dependencies.

---

### I. Introduction
Modern global supply chains operate as high-dimensional, stochastic networks characterized by volatile lead times, fluctuating inventory levels, and complex interdependencies [1], [2]. The detection of anomalies—ranging from logistics bottlenecks to supply disruptions—is critical for organizational resilience [3], [4]. Traditional time-series forecasting models often fail to account for the graph-structured nature of these networks, where an anomaly at one node propagates through the graph topology [5], [6].

Deep learning approaches, particularly GNNs, have emerged as powerful tools for relational data [7], [8]. However, current GNN architectures for anomaly detection frequently succumb to two primary limitations: (i) the inability to distinguish between exogenous structural shifts and endogenous anomalous events [9], [10]; and (ii) the over-smoothing of node embeddings in deep architectures, which obscures local, transient signals [11], [12]. 

To address these challenges, we introduce ST-GCL, a hierarchical spatio-temporal framework. ST-GCL employs a dual-stream architecture that processes structural and temporal features independently before fusing them via a cross-attention bottleneck. Furthermore, we implement a contrastive learning objective that maximizes the mutual information between local subgraph representations across shifted time windows [13], [14]. This approach ensures that the model remains robust to noise while maintaining sensitivity to localized structural perturbations.

---

### II. Related Work
The literature on anomaly detection in supply chains is extensive, spanning statistical process control to deep generative models [15], [16]. Early work focused on univariate time-series methods, such as ARIMA and Exponential Smoothing, which proved insufficient for complex network dynamics [17], [18]. Consequently, researchers pivoted toward multivariate approaches, including Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks and Gated Recurrent Units (GRU), to capture temporal correlations [19], [20].

More recently, GNNs have been applied to model non-Euclidean dependencies [21], [22]. Frameworks like GCNs and Graph Attention Networks (GATs) have been adapted for anomaly detection, often combined with autoencoders to identify deviations from reconstructed normal states [23], [24]. However, these approaches often overlook the hierarchical nature of supply chain tiers. Recent advancements in Contrastive Learning (CL) for graphs have demonstrated success in self-supervised learning, yet their application to dynamic anomaly detection remains under-explored [25], [26]. Our work bridges this gap by proposing a spatio-temporal contrastive objective tailored for hierarchical node-level anomaly scoring.

---

### III. Background / Preliminaries
Let the supply chain be represented as a dynamic graph $\mathcal{G} = \{G_1, G_2, \dots, G_T\}$, where each snapshot $G_t = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E}_t, X_t)$ consists of a set of nodes $\mathcal{V}$ (suppliers, warehouses, retailers), edges $\mathcal{E}_t$ (logistics links), and nodal feature matrices $X_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$.

An anomaly at time $t$ is defined as a significant deviation in the feature vector $x_{i,t}$ or the edge connectivity $e_{ij,t}$ that cannot be explained by historical trends or structural dependencies [27]. We aim to learn a mapping $f_\theta: \mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H}$, where $\mathcal{H}$ denotes the representation space, such that an anomaly score function $\psi: \mathcal{H} \to [0, 1]$ discriminates between normal and anomalous states [28].

---

### IV. Methodology / Proposed Approach
The architecture of ST-GCL consists of three primary modules: the Spatio-Temporal Encoder, the Hierarchical Contrastive Objective, and the Anomaly Scorer.

#### A. Spatio-Temporal Encoder
Given a graph snapshot $G_t$, we employ a dual-stream encoding. The spatial stream applies a Graph Attention Layer:
$$h_{i,t}^{(l+1)} = \sigma \left( \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} \alpha_{ij,t} W^{(l)} h_{j,t}^{(l)} \right)$$
where $\alpha_{ij,t}$ is the attention coefficient derived from the features of nodes $i$ and $j$, and $W^{(l)}$ is the layer-specific weight matrix. The temporal stream utilizes a Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) to capture local temporal dependencies:
$$z_{i,t} = \text{TCN}(\{h_{i,t-k}, \dots, h_{i,t}\})$$

#### B. Hierarchical Contrastive Objective
To ensure robust representation, we maximize the mutual information between representations of the same node across different temporal contexts. The contrastive loss $\mathcal{L}_{CL}$ is defined as:
$$\mathcal{L}_{CL} = -\sum_{i \in \mathcal{V}} \log \frac{\exp(sim(z_{i,t}, z_{i,t+\tau})/\tau)}{\sum_{j \in \mathcal{V}} \exp(sim(z_{i,t}, z_{j,t+\tau})/\tau)}$$
where $\tau$ is the temperature parameter and $sim(\cdot)$ denotes the cosine similarity [29].

#### C. Algorithm 1: ST-GCL Training Procedure
1. **Input:** Dynamic graph sequence $\{G_t\}_{t=1}^T$.
2. **Initialize:** Parameters $\theta$.
3. **For** epoch = 1 to E:
    a. Generate structural embeddings using GAT.
    b. Generate temporal embeddings using TCN.
    c. Fuse via cross-attention.
    d. Compute $\mathcal{L}_{total} = \mathcal{L}_{recon} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{CL}$.
    e. Update $\theta$ via backpropagation.
4. **Return:** Optimized model parameters $\theta^*$.

---

### V. Theoretical Analysis
**Theorem 1:** *The contrastive loss $\mathcal{L}_{CL}$ acts as a regularizer against feature drift in high-dimensional node embeddings.*
*Proof Sketch:* By minimizing the divergence between $z_{i,t}$ and $z_{i,t+\tau}$, the model forces the embedding space to be invariant to transient noise. The Information Bottleneck (IB) principle dictates that maximizing the mutual information between temporal projections limits the capacity of the encoder to capture stochastic volatility [30].

**Complexity Analysis:** The computational complexity of the GAT layer is $O(|\mathcal{V}|d^2 + |\mathcal{E}|d)$, where $d$ is the feature dimension. The TCN component adds a linear factor $O(T \cdot k)$, where $k$ is the kernel size, rendering the framework scalable to large-scale supply chains with $10^5$ nodes.

---

### VI. Experiments

#### A. Datasets
We evaluate our model on two datasets:
1. **GlobalLogistics-100:** A simulated network of 100 nodes capturing international shipping routes.
2. **SupplyChain-Real:** A proprietary dataset containing 5,000 nodes representing regional retail distribution.

#### B. Baselines
We compare ST-GCL against: (1) Isolation Forest [31], (2) LSTM-AE [32], (3) GCN-VAE [33], (4) TGCN [34], and (5) EvolveGCN [35].

#### C. Results
Table 1 demonstrates the performance in terms of F1-score across both datasets.

| Model | GlobalLogistics-100 | SupplyChain-Real |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Isolation Forest | 0.62 | 0.58 |
| LSTM-AE | 0.71 | 0.69 |
| GCN-VAE | 0.74 | 0.72 |
| TGCN | 0.78 | 0.75 |
| EvolveGCN | 0.81 | 0.78 |
| **ST-GCL (Ours)** | **0.87** | **0.84** |

---

### VII. Ablation Study
To isolate the contribution of components, we trained variants of the model:
- **w/o Temporal Stream:** Removes TCN.
- **w/o Contrastive Loss:** Standard reconstruction loss only.

| Variant | Precision | Recall | F1-Score |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Full Model | 0.88 | 0.86 | 0.87 |
| w/o Temporal | 0.76 | 0.74 | 0.75 |
| w/o Contrastive | 0.79 | 0.77 | 0.78 |

---

### VIII. Discussion / Insights
The performance gains observed in ST-GCL suggest that spatial connectivity is highly informative for detecting propagation patterns in supply chains. The superiority of the contrastive objective over pure reconstruction loss underscores the importance of learned invariance in noisy environments. 

However, we observe that the model exhibits performance degradation when the graph topology undergoes rapid, non-continuous changes (e.g., node entry/exit). This suggests that the current assumption of a stable node set $\mathcal{V}$ is a potential bottleneck for highly dynamic networks.

Finally, the computational efficiency allows for near real-time anomaly inference. The trade-off between the depth of the GAT layers and the latency of the system suggests that 3-layer architectures represent the optimal balance for current hardware constraints.

---

### IX. Limitations & Future Work
The primary limitation of ST-GCL is the reliance on historical data for training, which may not capture "black swan" events—anomalies without historical precedent. Future work will investigate few-shot anomaly detection techniques to improve performance on rare, high-impact disruptions.

Furthermore, the integration of causal inference modules could allow the model to provide actionable insights into the *cause* of an anomaly, rather than merely flagging the event. We intend to explore hierarchical Causal GNNs as the next evolution of this framework.

Lastly, the scalability to ultra-large graphs exceeding $10^6$ nodes remains an open question, requiring more efficient graph sampling strategies or distributed training protocols beyond current implementations.

---

### X. Conclusion
In this paper, we proposed ST-GCL, a spatio-temporal GNN framework for supply chain anomaly detection. By leveraging a novel hierarchical contrastive objective, the model effectively balances structural and temporal information, leading to superior performance in detecting complex anomalies. Our experimental results confirm that the proposed method is both robust to environmental noise and scalable to realistic network sizes.

---

### XI. References
[1] A. Smith and B. Jones, "Resilience in global supply chains," *Journal of Operations Management*, vol. 45, pp. 112-125, 2020.
[2] C. Wang et al., "Network dynamics in logistics," *Proc. IEEE Int. Conf. Data Mining*, Singapore, 2021, pp. 200-210.
[3] D. Lee, "Anomalies in complex networks," *Nature Communications*, vol. 12, pp. 45-58, 2022.
[4] F. Garcia, "Supply chain risks," *Supply Chain Management Review*, vol. 18, pp. 33-40, 2019.
[5] H. Zhang, "Graph neural networks for anomaly detection," in *Proc. NeurIPS*, Vancouver, 2022, pp. 1021-1035.
[6] I. Petrov, "Temporal graph analysis," *Journal of AI Research*, vol. 66, pp. 789-805, 2023.
[7] J. Kim and L. Park, "Deep learning on supply networks," *IEEE Trans. Neural Networks*, vol. 32, pp. 550-562, 2021.
[8] K. Miller, "Anomaly detection survey," *ACM Computing Surveys*, vol. 54, pp. 1-35, 2022.
[9] L. Brown, "The impact of noise on GNNs," *Proc. ICML*, Baltimore, 2023, pp. 445-458.

# Graph-Guided Cross-Modal Reasoning: Integrating Scene Graph Supervision for Robust Visual Question Answering

**Authors:**
*Julian V. Thorne, Eleanor S. Vance, and Marcus H. Chen*
*Institute for Advanced Machine Learning (IAML), Zürich, Switzerland*
*{j.thorne, e.vance, m.chen}@iaml.ethz.ch*

---

### Abstract
Visual Question Answering (VQA) remains a fundamental challenge in bridging computer vision and natural language processing, particularly when models must move beyond statistical correlation toward compositional reasoning. Current state-of-the-art vision-language models frequently succumb to dataset biases, relying on linguistic priors rather than explicit scene understanding. We introduce the **Graph-Guided Cross-Modal Reasoning (G2CMR)** framework, a novel architecture that explicitly incorporates structured scene graph supervision to ground latent representation learning. By enforcing a consistency objective between implicit latent features and explicit graph-based relational manifolds, G2CMR improves performance on complex compositional reasoning tasks. Our theoretical analysis confirms that this dual-stream integration effectively reduces the hypothesis space for downstream question answering. Experimental results on VQA v2.0 and GQA datasets demonstrate that G2CMR achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining robustness against common shortcut learning patterns.

### Keywords
Multimodal Learning, Scene Graph Supervision, Vision-Language Transformers, Compositional Reasoning, Relational Inductive Bias.

---

### Introduction
The intersection of vision and language has seen transformative shifts with the advent of large-scale pre-trained multimodal transformers [1, 2, 3]. Despite impressive gains on benchmark datasets, these models often demonstrate significant weaknesses in relational reasoning and object-attribute grounding [4, 5, 6]. Existing architectures predominantly operate on unstructured visual features, such as regional proposals or patch embeddings, which often fail to explicitly capture the hierarchical and relational structure of visual scenes [7, 8, 9]. This lack of explicit structure frequently leads to "shortcut learning," where models rely on spurious correlations between language queries and frequent visual answers, rather than grounding their reasoning in the physical scene [10, 11, 12].

To address these limitations, we propose the **Graph-Guided Cross-Modal Reasoning (G2CMR)** framework. The core innovation of G2CMR is the introduction of a scene-graph-informed alignment module that acts as a structural regularizer during the cross-modal fusion phase. By mapping visual features into a structured relational manifold governed by scene graphs, we force the transformer to acknowledge atomic relations (e.g., *holding*, *under*, *next-to*) during the reasoning process [13, 14, 15]. Unlike previous approaches that treat graphs as auxiliary inputs, G2CMR employs an iterative cross-attention mechanism that dynamically re-weights latent representations based on predicted semantic triples [16, 17, 18].

Our contributions are three-fold:
1. We introduce the **Relational Alignment Loss (RAL)**, a novel objective function that constrains cross-modal latent spaces to adhere to the connectivity patterns of ground-truth scene graphs.
2. We derive a theoretical bound showing that scene graph supervision reduces the VC-dimension of the reasoning head, thereby mitigating overfitting to linguistic priors [19, 20, 21].
3. We conduct extensive experiments across VQA v2.0 and GQA, demonstrating superior compositional reasoning capabilities and robustness to distribution shifts [22, 23, 24, 25].

---

### Related Work
Visual Question Answering has evolved from early bag-of-words approaches to sophisticated multimodal transformers [1, 26, 27]. Early methods often struggled with grounding; however, the shift toward object-centric representations in Faster-RCNN-based models marked a significant improvement [28, 29, 30]. More recently, vision-language pre-training models, such as CLIP and ALBEF, have leveraged large-scale contrastive learning to align image and text modalities [31, 32, 33]. While successful in retrieval and classification, these models often lack deep understanding of spatial arrangements [34, 35, 36].

Scene graph generation (SGG) has emerged as a parallel field, focusing on extracting object-relationship-object triples from imagery [37, 38, 39]. Integrating these graphs into VQA has been explored through Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) and Graph Attention Networks (GATs) [40, 41, 42]. For example, the work of [43] and [44] demonstrated that GCNs could improve spatial reasoning. However, these methods often suffer from high computational complexity and difficulty in end-to-end training [45, 46]. Our approach, G2CMR, departs from rigid GCN architectures, instead opting for a dynamic attention-based mechanism that treats scene graphs as differentiable soft-constraints, ensuring better integration with transformer-based backbones [47, 48, 49].

---

### Background / Preliminaries
Let $\mathcal{I}$ be an input image and $\mathcal{Q}$ be a natural language question. The objective of VQA is to learn a mapping $f: (\mathcal{I}, \mathcal{Q}) \to \mathcal{A}$, where $\mathcal{A}$ is the answer space.
Representing the visual scene as a scene graph $\mathcal{G} = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$, where $\mathcal{V} = \{v_1, \dots, v_n\}$ are objects and $\mathcal{E} \subseteq \mathcal{V} \times \mathcal{R} \times \mathcal{V}$ are relationships.

**Definition 1 (Cross-Modal Fusion).** We define the cross-modal fusion as a concatenation of latent projections $\phi(\mathcal{I})$ and $\psi(\mathcal{Q})$, followed by a series of transformer blocks $\mathcal{T}$.
**Definition 2 (Graph Supervision).** We represent the scene graph adjacency matrix as $A \in \{0, 1\}^{n \times n}$. The goal is to ensure the cross-modal representation $H$ respects $A$.

---

### Methodology / Proposed Approach
The G2CMR framework utilizes a dual-path architecture consisting of a **Visual-Linguistic Transformer (VLT)** and a **Graph-Guided Reasoner (GGR)**.

#### 1. Architecture Overview
The VLT provides contextualized multimodal tokens $\{z_1, \dots, z_k\}$. The GGR module receives these tokens alongside the scene graph extracted from a pre-trained SGG model. We define the GGR block as:
$$H_{l+1} = \text{Softmax}\left(\frac{Q_l K_l^T}{\sqrt{d_k}} + \lambda \cdot A\right)V_l \quad (1)$$
where $\lambda$ is a learnable scaling parameter that modulates the influence of the scene graph connectivity $A$.

#### 2. Relational Alignment Loss
To ensure that the latent reasoning captures relational dependencies, we minimize the Kullback-Leibler divergence between the attention maps $M$ produced by the VLT and the graph adjacency matrix $A$:
$$\mathcal{L}_{RAL} = \sum_{i,j} KL(M_{i,j} || \hat{A}_{i,j}) \quad (2)$$
where $\hat{A}$ is a normalized version of $A$ including self-loops.

#### 3. Algorithm: G2CMR Training Step
```python
def G2CMR_step(image, question, scene_graph):
    # 1. Feature Extraction
    v_feats = Encoder_V(image)
    q_feats = Encoder_Q(question)
    
    # 2. Multimodal Fusion
    z_tokens = Transformer_Fusion(v_feats, q_feats)
    
    # 3. Graph-Guided Attention
    A = Construct_Adjacency(scene_graph)
    h_reasoning = GGR_Module(z_tokens, A)
    
    # 4. Losses
    L_vqa = CrossEntropy(h_reasoning, target_answer)
    L_ral = KL_Divergence(Attention_Map(z_tokens), A)
    total_loss = L_vqa + alpha * L_ral
    return total_loss
```

---

### Theoretical Analysis
**Theorem 1.** *Given a hypothesis class $\mathcal{H}$ of VQA models, the inclusion of the RAL constraint reduces the capacity of the model, effectively bounding the Rademacher complexity $\mathcal{R}_n(\mathcal{H})$ by confining the learning to paths consistent with $\mathcal{G}$.*

*Proof Sketch.* Let the set of valid reasoning paths be $\mathcal{P} \subseteq \mathcal{G}$. By enforcing $\lambda > 0$, the attention matrix $M$ is restricted such that $\text{supp}(M) \subseteq \text{supp}(A)$. The reduction in the support set of the attention operator effectively reduces the search space of the model, lowering the generalization error bound:
$$\epsilon \leq \sqrt{\frac{2\log(2/\delta)}{n}} + \frac{C}{\sqrt{|\mathcal{P}|}} \quad (3)$$
As the density of the scene graph $|\mathcal{P}|$ increases, the second term vanishes, assuming the graph is a faithful representation of the scene.

---

### Experiments

#### Datasets
We evaluate our model on the **VQA v2.0** dataset and the **GQA** dataset. GQA is particularly relevant as it requires multi-hop relational reasoning.

#### Experimental Setup
We use a ViT-B/16 backbone for visual features and BERT-base for text. Training is performed on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a batch size of 256.

#### Results
Table 1 demonstrates the efficacy of G2CMR against state-of-the-art baselines.

| Model | VQA v2.0 (Acc) | GQA (Acc) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| LXMERT [1] | 72.4 | 54.1 |
| VisualBERT [26] | 71.0 | 53.8 |
| ALBEF [33] | 75.8 | 58.2 |
| VinVL [30] | 76.2 | 59.5 |
| **G2CMR (Ours)** | **78.9** | **63.4** |

Table 2 highlights the performance on compositional questions.

| Model | Binary | Num | Other |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| LXMERT | 78.2 | 45.3 | 61.2 |
| G2CMR | **82.5** | **52.8** | **67.4** |

---

### Ablation Study
We analyze the necessity of the RAL term and the GGR module.

| Config | VQA Acc | GQA Acc |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Baseline (No Graph) | 74.1 | 55.2 |
| GGR Module only | 77.2 | 60.1 |
| G2CMR (Full) | 78.9 | 63.4 |

The ablation confirms that both the GGR module and the alignment loss are critical to performance.

---

### Discussion / Insights
The experimental results indicate that scene graph supervision provides a crucial inductive bias for VQA. By grounding the reasoning process in symbolic relationships, the model avoids relying on linguistic correlations that often plague standard transformers. 

Furthermore, our analysis of the attention maps reveals that G2CMR consistently attends to objects identified as linked in the scene graph during the inference process. This confirms that the model has internalized the relational manifold imposed by the graph structure.

Finally, the trade-off between computational cost and accuracy is noted. While the GGR module adds approximately 15% to training time, the inference overhead remains negligible, making G2CMR suitable for deployment in scenarios where relational accuracy is paramount.

---

### Limitations & Future Work
Despite its performance, G2CMR relies on the accuracy of the underlying SGG module. In scenarios where the scene graph generation is noisy, the RAL constraint may introduce false priors, potentially degrading performance.

Future work will investigate "Graph-Free" relational distillation, where the model learns to internally simulate a scene graph structure without requiring explicit ground-truth graphs during test time. 

Additionally, exploring non-hierarchical graph structures and hyper-graphs may further enhance the ability of the model to capture complex spatial dynamics in highly cluttered scenes.

---

### Conclusion
We have presented G2CMR, a novel vision-language framework that leverages scene graph supervision to enhance reasoning. Through formal analysis and empirical validation, we have demonstrated that forcing cross-modal alignment with symbolic relational graphs significantly improves robustness and compositional accuracy in VQA tasks.

---
