# Adaptive Manifold-Constrained Latent Diffusion Models for High-Fidelity Medical Image Synthesis

**Authors:**  
Elena V. Sterling, Department of Computer Science, ETH Zurich  
Marcus J. Thorne, Institute for Medical Imaging, University of Cambridge  
Siddharth R. Gupta, Department of Artificial Intelligence, Stanford University  

---

### Abstract
The synthesis of high-fidelity medical imaging data remains a significant bottleneck in deep learning applications due to privacy regulations and data scarcity. While latent diffusion models (LDMs) have demonstrated remarkable efficacy in natural image synthesis, they often struggle to preserve the rigorous anatomical consistency and high-frequency textural details required for clinical-grade diagnostics. In this paper, we propose the Adaptive Manifold-Constrained Latent Diffusion Model (AMC-LDM). Our approach introduces a novel manifold-alignment objective that penalizes latent trajectories diverging from the underlying anatomical distribution, coupled with a cross-attention mechanism conditioned on modality-specific physiological priors. We demonstrate that AMC-LDM achieves superior structural similarity index (SSIM) and Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) scores compared to state-of-the-art generative models across multiple modalities, including MRI and CT datasets. Furthermore, we provide a theoretical analysis of the convergence bounds of our manifold-constrained denoising process. Our findings suggest that incorporating anatomical inductive biases directly into the diffusion process significantly mitigates the hallucination artifacts common in standard generative models.

**Keywords:** Latent Diffusion Models, Medical Image Synthesis, Manifold Learning, Generative AI, Anatomical Consistency.

---

### I. Introduction
The advent of deep generative models has fundamentally transformed the landscape of computer vision. However, the application of these models to medical imaging presents distinct challenges that remain largely unaddressed by generic architectures [1], [2], [3]. Medical images are characterized by high-dimensional, structured anatomical dependencies where pixel-perfect precision is not merely an aesthetic goal but a diagnostic requirement [4], [5], [6]. Standard diffusion models, while proficient at learning the global distribution of images, often fail to enforce the strict geometric constraints necessitated by medical physics [7], [8].

Current generative frameworks, such as Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs) and their latent-space variants, operate under the assumption that the data distribution lies on a continuous manifold [9], [10], [11]. In practice, medical data is highly non-uniform and constrained by physical processes (e.g., K-space sampling in MRI). When these models are trained on limited cohorts, they frequently exhibit "anatomical drift," where generated structures violate physiological logic [12], [13], [14]. This necessitates a paradigm shift from purely data-driven generation to hybrid approaches that integrate structural priors into the diffusion trajectory [15], [16].

In this work, we introduce the Adaptive Manifold-Constrained Latent Diffusion Model (AMC-LDM). By embedding a manifold-alignment loss directly into the denoising objective, we ensure that the latent diffusion path remains within the subspace of valid anatomical configurations [17], [18], [19]. We hypothesize that by constraining the latent space to adhere to modality-specific physical priors, we can reduce the variance of the generative process and improve the fidelity of high-frequency details [20], [21]. Our contributions are three-fold: (i) the introduction of the AMC-LDM architecture, (ii) a novel manifold-alignment objective function, and (iii) an extensive empirical evaluation showing significant improvement in clinical relevance metrics over baseline generative architectures [22], [23], [24], [25].

---

### II. Related Work
The field of generative medical imaging has evolved from early Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) to sophisticated diffusion-based models. Early efforts focused on CycleGAN architectures for modality translation, yet these models often suffered from mode collapse and lacked the stability required for diverse clinical scenarios [1], [4], [26]. The emergence of Score-Based Generative Models (SGMs) allowed for more stable training by reformulating the problem as learning the gradient of the log-density of the data distribution [2], [9], [27].

Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) further optimized these processes by operating in a compressed latent space, reducing the computational overhead of high-resolution image synthesis [3], [10], [28]. Despite these advancements, standard LDM implementations often prioritize perceptual realism over anatomical accuracy [5], [11], [29]. Recent works have attempted to address this through conditional generation, utilizing segmentations or clinical labels as inputs to steer the diffusion process [6], [12], [30]. However, these approaches remain highly dependent on the quality and availability of annotated training data [7], [13], [31].

Manifold learning techniques have been explored in the context of dimensionality reduction and representation learning, yet their integration into the iterative denoising process of diffusion models is relatively nascent [14], [19], [32]. Our proposed AMC-LDM builds upon the theoretical framework of Riemannian Langevin dynamics, extending the concept of manifold-constrained sampling to the latent space of generative models [8], [15], [33]. By constraining the latent manifold, we bridge the gap between generative flexibility and domain-specific structural rigidity [16], [20], [34].

---

### III. Background / Preliminaries
We define the latent diffusion process as a Markov chain that transforms a data distribution $p(x)$ into a latent representation $z = E(x)$, where $E$ is an encoder. The forward process adds noise to the latent vector $z_0$ over $T$ timesteps, governed by a variance schedule $\beta_t$:
$$q(z_t | z_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(z_t; \sqrt{1 - \beta_t} z_{t-1}, \beta_t \mathbf{I})$$
The reverse process, which generates data from noise, is parameterized by a neural network $\epsilon_\theta(z_t, t, c)$, where $c$ is the conditioning signal. The objective is to minimize the mean squared error between the added noise $\epsilon$ and the predicted noise:
$$\mathcal{L}_{diff} = \mathbb{E}_{z, \epsilon, t} [||\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(z_t, t, c)||^2]$$
In our context, we consider the latent manifold $\mathcal{M}$ as a d-dimensional submanifold of the latent space $\mathbb{R}^D$. We assume that valid anatomical structures reside on this manifold, and deviations represent generative hallucinations [17], [21], [35].

---

### IV. Methodology / Proposed Approach
The AMC-LDM architecture incorporates a dual-pathway mechanism. First, the latent diffusion process is constrained by an "Anatomical Manifold Projection" (AMP) layer. Second, we introduce a cross-attention module that fuses physiological priors directly into the denoising blocks.

#### A. The Manifold-Alignment Objective
To ensure that $z_t$ remains on the manifold $\mathcal{M}$, we introduce an auxiliary loss term $\mathcal{L}_{man}$. Given a set of reference anatomical features $f_{anat}$, the loss is defined as:
$$\mathcal{L}_{total} = \mathcal{L}_{diff} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{man} = \mathcal{L}_{diff} + \lambda || \nabla_{\mathcal{M}} \Phi(z_t) ||^2$$
where $\Phi$ represents a manifold-embedding function and $\lambda$ is a dynamic weighting hyperparameter. This objective penalizes trajectories that move orthogonal to the tangent space of $\mathcal{M}$.

#### B. Algorithm: AMC-LDM Sampling
The sampling algorithm follows a modified Langevin dynamic process:

**Algorithm 1:** AMC-LDM Sampling Process
1. Initialize $z_T \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{I})$
2. For $t = T, T-1, \dots, 1$ do:
3.   Predict noise $\epsilon_\theta(z_t, t, c)$
4.   Compute gradient of manifold constraint $\nabla \mathcal{L}_{man}$
5.   Update $z_{t-1} = z_t - \eta \epsilon_\theta - \gamma \nabla \mathcal{L}_{man}$
6. End For
7. Return $x = D(z_0)$

The computational complexity of the denoising step is $O(L \cdot H \cdot W)$, where $L$ is the number of latent layers, and $H, W$ represent the spatial dimensions of the latent map. The additional manifold constraint adds an overhead of $O(K^2)$ where $K$ is the dimension of the embedding space, making it efficient for large-scale medical datasets [22], [36], [37].

---

### V. Theoretical Analysis
We provide a brief argument regarding the stability of the proposed approach. Let $\mathcal{T}_t$ be the transition operator of the unconstrained diffusion model. The AMC-LDM operator $\mathcal{T}_t^*$ satisfies the constraint $\mathcal{T}_t^* \subseteq \mathcal{M}$. 
*Claim 1:* The inclusion of the manifold gradient term ensures that the error between the sampled distribution $p_\theta(z)$ and the true distribution $p(z)$ is bounded by $\epsilon = \mathbb{E}[||\Delta_{\mathcal{M}}||^2]$. By choosing $\lambda$ such that $\lambda \propto 1/t$, we guarantee convergence to the manifold surface as $t \to 0$. This confirms that our method preserves structural integrity in the limit of the reverse diffusion process [23], [38], [39].

---

### VI. Experiments

#### A. Datasets and Experimental Setup
We evaluate AMC-LDM on the BraTS-2023 (MRI) and LIDC-IDRI (CT) datasets. We utilize a latent space dimension of 64x64. Hyperparameters were set to $\lambda=0.01$ and $T=1000$. Training was performed on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs for 500,000 iterations.

#### B. Baselines
We compare against: (1) Standard DDPM [9], (2) Latent Diffusion Model [10], (3) MedGAN [26], (4) CycleGAN [4], and (5) Score-based SDE [27].

#### C. Results
Table 1 displays the performance metrics. AMC-LDM consistently outperforms baselines in FID and SSIM.

| Model | FID $\downarrow$ | SSIM $\uparrow$ | PSNR $\uparrow$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| DDPM | 34.2 | 0.78 | 22.1 |
| LDM | 28.5 | 0.81 | 24.5 |
| MedGAN | 42.1 | 0.65 | 19.8 |
| CycleGAN | 39.4 | 0.72 | 21.3 |
| SDE | 29.8 | 0.80 | 23.9 |
| **AMC-LDM** | **22.4** | **0.89** | **26.8** |

---

### VII. Ablation Study
We analyze the contribution of the manifold-alignment loss ($\mathcal{L}_{man}$) and the cross-attention mechanism.

| Components | FID | SSIM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Base LDM | 28.5 | 0.81 |
| + Cross-Attention | 26.2 | 0.84 |
| + $\mathcal{L}_{man}$ | 24.1 | 0.87 |
| **Full AMC-LDM** | **22.4** | **0.89** |

Observations indicate that $\mathcal{L}_{man}$ provides the most significant boost to structural fidelity, while cross-attention aids in modality-specific detail retention.

---

### VIII. Discussion / Insights
The experimental results demonstrate that embedding anatomical priors into the latent manifold significantly reduces the hallucination of non-physiological structures. While standard LDMs capture global statistics, they struggle with the "small-scale" details (e.g., vascular bifurcations) that are critical for clinical utility. Our approach addresses this by enforcing a constraint that mirrors the physical properties of the scanned tissue [40], [41], [42].

An interesting observation from our ablation study is that the manifold-alignment objective effectively acts as a regularizer. By restricting the search space to valid anatomical manifolds, we reduce the training variance, leading to faster convergence in the later stages of the diffusion process [43], [44], [45]. This suggests that "anatomical-awareness" is a powerful inductive bias that should be more widely adopted in medical generative tasks.

Furthermore, we observed that AMC-LDM shows superior generalization capability when trained on limited data subsets compared to the baseline LDM. This is likely because the manifold constraint provides a strong prior that prevents the model from overfitting to noise or artifacts present in the training set [46], [47], [48]. These findings suggest that incorporating domain-specific constraints is a viable pathway for reducing dependency on large-scale datasets.

---

### IX. Limitations & Future Work
Despite the performance gains, AMC-LDM has limitations. First, the selection of the manifold-embedding function $\Phi$ is currently dependent on pre-trained anatomical feature extractors. If the feature extractor is biased, these biases propagate into the generated images. Future work should investigate self-supervised manifold discovery methods that do not rely on external pre-training [49], [50], [51].

Second, the computational overhead of calculating $\nabla \mathcal{L}_{man}$ during each sampling step, while manageable, still prevents real-time interactive clinical applications. Optimization of the gradient calculation through distillation or caching strategies remains an open research question [52], [53], [54].

Finally, while we focused on MRI and CT, the robustness of AMC-LDM in multi-modal scenarios (e.g., PET-CT) requires further exploration. Scaling the manifold-alignment objective to handle heterogeneous data sources will be a key focus of our future research endeavors [55], [56], [57].

---

### X. Conclusion
We have presented the Adaptive Manifold-Constrained Latent Diffusion Model (AMC-LDM), a novel framework for high-fidelity medical image synthesis. By integrating a manifold-alignment loss into the latent diffusion process, we have demonstrated that it is possible to achieve superior anatomical accuracy without sacrificing the generative capacity of standard diffusion models. Our approach provides a scalable, robust solution for augmenting medical datasets, which we believe will be instrumental in the future of data-driven clinical diagnostics [58], [59], [60].

---

# Dynamic Persona-Anchored Memory Networks: Enhancing Consistency in Neural Dialogue Generation

**Authors:**  
Dr. Elena R. Vance, Department of Computer Science, Stanford University  
Dr. Marcus T. Thorne, AI Research Lab, MIT CSAIL  
Dr. Sarah J. Jenkins, Institute for Language and Speech Processing  

---

### 1. Abstract
The generation of coherent, persona-consistent dialogue remains a significant challenge for Transformer-based large language models (LLMs). While current architectures excel at linguistic fluency, they frequently suffer from "persona drift"—the tendency to lose track of assigned identity attributes over long-context multi-turn interactions. We propose the **Dynamic Persona-Anchored Memory Network (DPAM-Net)**, a novel architecture that decouples persona storage from the latent generative space using a gated cross-attention mechanism. By implementing a sparse, retrieval-augmented persona buffer, DPAM-Net selectively modulates the attention heads to prioritize persona-relevant semantic tokens. We evaluate our method on the PersonaChat and DailyDialog benchmarks, demonstrating a 14.2% improvement in consistency metrics over standard LLMs while maintaining competitive perplexity scores. Our theoretical analysis confirms that this memory-gating mechanism reduces the variance of persona-related token distributions, effectively bounding the probability of identity contradictions.

**Keywords:** Dialogue Systems, Persona-Consistency, Memory-Augmented Transformers, Natural Language Generation, Retrieval-Augmented Generation.

---

### 2. Introduction
The advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) has revolutionized the field of conversational AI, enabling human-like interaction across a variety of domains [1], [2]. Despite these advancements, sustained persona consistency—the ability of an agent to maintain a coherent identity over extended dialogue—remains a elusive objective [3], [4]. Standard autoregressive architectures, such as the GPT-family, rely on self-attention mechanisms that distribute context over time, often resulting in the "dilution" of static persona traits as the conversation history grows [5], [6].

The problem of persona inconsistency is fundamentally a problem of long-term memory management. In most existing systems, persona information is treated as a prefix prompt [7], [8]. As the dialogue context expands, the relative signal-to-noise ratio of this persona prompt diminishes, leading to "amnesia" or the adoption of contradictory traits [9], [10]. Researchers have attempted to mitigate this via external memory modules, but these often suffer from high latency and rigid retrieval patterns that fail to adapt to the nuance of conversational intent [11], [12].

To address these limitations, we introduce the **Dynamic Persona-Anchored Memory Network (DPAM-Net)**. Unlike existing approaches, our model utilizes a decoupled memory bank that acts as a dynamic filter for the attention heads. By employing a differentiable gating mechanism, the model learns when to prioritize persona-derived information versus contextual history. This paper makes three primary contributions: (1) we propose a novel memory-gated attention architecture; (2) we provide a theoretical proof that this mechanism reduces the entropy of persona-related token generation; and (3) we demonstrate through extensive experiments that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in consistency across multiple datasets.

---

### 3. Related Work
The literature on dialogue generation is vast, primarily categorized into persona-conditioned models and memory-augmented architectures. Early work focused on static embeddings, where persona traits were encoded as fixed vectors added to the input sequence [13], [14]. However, these methods failed to account for the dynamic evolution of conversational context, leading to repetitive or generic responses [15], [16].

More recent approaches have utilized retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to inject external knowledge into the dialogue process [17], [18]. By retrieving relevant persona facts from a structured database, models such as the Persona-RAG variants have shown improvements in factual accuracy [19], [20]. Nevertheless, these models often suffer from "retrieval noise," where irrelevant facts are injected into the context, degrading the quality of the response [21], [22].

Architectural innovations in Transformer-based models have also emerged, such as the Longformer and Transformer-XL, which address the limitations of the fixed context window [23], [24]. While these models are effective for processing long sequences, they do not inherently prioritize persona information over general dialogue content [25]. Our work bridges this gap by integrating a dedicated, memory-gated mechanism that specifically addresses the prioritization of persona-consistent features within the attention layers.

---

### 4. Background / Preliminaries
Let $\mathcal{C} = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_N\}$ denote the dialogue context, and $\mathcal{P} = \{p_1, p_2, \dots, p_M\}$ denote the set of persona traits. The objective of a dialogue model is to generate a response $\mathcal{R} = \{r_1, r_2, \dots, r_T\}$ such that $P(\mathcal{R} | \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{P})$ is maximized.

Standard Transformers compute hidden states $H$ via the self-attention mechanism:
$$ \text{Attn}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V $$
where $Q, K, V$ are the query, key, and value matrices respectively. In this setup, persona information $\mathcal{P}$ is often concatenated to $\mathcal{C}$, meaning the model must learn to attend to $\mathcal{P}$ through the same parameters used for $\mathcal{C}$ [1], [5]. This leads to the "attention bottleneck" where persona signal is lost in the noise of the dialogue history.

---

### 5. Methodology / Proposed Approach
The DPAM-Net architecture introduces a dedicated **Persona Memory Buffer (PMB)**. The PMB consists of a set of trainable key-value pairs $\{\mathbf{K}_p, \mathbf{V}_p\}$ that are kept separate from the dialogue context memory. We define a Gated Cross-Attention (GCA) layer that modulates the influence of the PMB:

$$ \mathbf{g} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_g [Q; \text{context}]) $$
$$ \text{Output} = (1 - \mathbf{g}) \cdot \text{Attn}(\mathcal{C}) + \mathbf{g} \cdot \text{Attn}_{PMB}(Q, \mathbf{K}_p, \mathbf{V}_p) $$
where $\mathbf{g}$ is the gating vector learned via a linear transformation of the query and context, and $\sigma$ is the sigmoid activation function.

#### 5.1 Algorithm: DPAM-Net Forward Pass
The procedure for generating a response token is detailed in Algorithm 1.

```python
Algorithm 1: DPAM-Net Token Generation
Input: Context C, Persona P, Current tokens R_{<t}
1: Initialize PMB with Persona Embeddings K_p, V_p
2: Encode C into hidden state H_c
3: For each layer in Transformer:
4:    Compute standard self-attention: A_c = SelfAttn(H_c)
5:    Compute gated cross-attention: 
6:    g = Sigmoid(W_g * [Query; A_c])
7:    A_p = CrossAttn(Query, K_p, V_p)
8:    Output_layer = (1-g)*A_c + g*A_p
9: Compute probability distribution over vocabulary
10: Sample token r_t
```

---

### 6. Theoretical Analysis
We claim that the GCA mechanism bounds the variance of persona-related token probabilities. Let $X_p$ be the random variable representing the probability of a persona-consistent token. 

**Theorem 1:** *Given the gating mechanism, the variance $\text{Var}(X_p)$ is minimized relative to standard concatenation methods because the gating factor $\mathbf{g}$ acts as a low-pass filter on non-persona-related noise.*

*Proof Sketch:* By construction, $\mathbf{g}$ is trained to minimize the cross-entropy loss specifically on persona-consistent validation sets. As the number of layers increases, the composition of $\mathbf{g}$ creates a contraction mapping towards the manifold of persona-consistent latent states, effectively dampening the influence of irrelevant context tokens.

---

### 7. Experiments
#### 7.1 Datasets
We evaluate on:
1. **PersonaChat:** A crowdsourced dataset of 160k sentences with defined personas [7].
2. **DailyDialog:** A multi-turn dialogue dataset focused on daily conversation [12].

#### 7.2 Experimental Setup
We fine-tune a pre-trained Llama-2-7B model using our DPAM-Net architecture. We set the learning rate to $2 \times 10^{-5}$ and utilize a batch size of 32. All experiments were conducted on a cluster of 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs.

#### 7.3 Results
Tables 1 and 2 summarize the performance comparisons against the baselines.

**Table 1: Persona-Consistency Accuracy (Higher is better)**
| Model | PersonaChat (%) | DailyDialog (%) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| GPT-3.5-Turbo | 62.4 | 58.1 |
| BlenderBot 3.0 | 68.9 | 61.3 |
| RAG-Transformer | 71.2 | 64.2 |
| DialoGPT-Large | 65.5 | 59.8 |
| **DPAM-Net (Ours)** | **78.4** | **70.5** |

**Table 2: Perplexity (Lower is better)**
| Model | PersonaChat | DailyDialog |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| GPT-3.5-Turbo | 14.2 | 16.5 |
| BlenderBot 3.0 | 12.8 | 15.2 |
| **DPAM-Net (Ours)** | **11.5** | **13.8** |

---

### 8. Ablation Study
We analyze the contribution of the components:

**Table 3: Ablation Results**
| Component | Consistency | PPL |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Full DPAM-Net | 78.4 | 11.5 |
| w/o Gated Mechanism | 70.1 | 12.4 |
| w/o PMB Buffer | 64.3 | 14.1 |

The results indicate that the gated mechanism is crucial for consistency, while the PMB buffer provides the necessary grounding for identity.

---

### 9. Discussion / Insights
The experimental results strongly support our hypothesis that decoupling persona storage from general linguistic memory enhances consistency. The DPAM-Net architecture excels by providing a "hard" constraint on identity-related information, preventing the drift observed in standard autoregressive models [3], [10]. 

Furthermore, our observations suggest that the gating mechanism adapts dynamically. In turns where the user asks direct questions about the persona, the gate $\mathbf{g}$ increases significantly. Conversely, in phatic segments of the conversation, the model relies more on the standard attention mechanism, maintaining linguistic fluency without forcing persona facts where they do not belong.

This dual-path processing mimics, in a rudimentary sense, the distinction between working memory and long-term semantic memory in cognitive psychology. By modularizing these functions, we allow the model to scale its persona knowledge without affecting the general-purpose generative capability of the Transformer backbone.

---

### 10. Limitations & Future Work
Despite these improvements, DPAM-Net faces limitations. First, the fixed size of the PMB buffer may become a bottleneck for extremely large persona profiles. Future work will investigate dynamic hashing or hierarchical memory structures to store more complex persona biographies.

Second, our current implementation assumes that the persona is static. However, human personas evolve during conversation. We intend to extend the DPAM-Net to include a "Memory Update" mechanism, allowing the model to write new persona information based on the interaction history.

Finally, the computational overhead of the GCA layer, while minimal, does introduce a 5% latency increase in inference time. Investigating hardware-aware sparse attention kernels could mitigate this, making the system viable for real-time applications in edge devices.

---

### 11. Conclusion
We have presented DPAM-Net, a novel architecture for persona-consistent dialogue generation. By utilizing a gated cross-attention mechanism and a dedicated memory buffer, we have demonstrated a robust improvement in consistency across diverse benchmarks. Our theoretical framework provides a foundation for understanding how memory-gated attention bounds identity drift. We believe this work represents a significant step toward developing more reliable, consistent conversational agents in the era of large-scale language modeling.

---

### 12. References
[1] A. Vaswani et al., "Attention is all you need," in Proc. NeurIPS, Long Beach, 2017, pp. 5998-6008.  
[2] J. Devlin et al., "BERT: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers," in Proc. NAACL, Minneapolis, 2019, pp. 4171-4186.  
[3] S. Roller et al., "Recipes for building an open-domain chatbot," in Proc. EACL, Online, 2021, pp. 300-325.  
[4] Y. Zhang et al., "Personalizing dialogue agents: I have a dog, do you have pets too?" in Proc. ACL, Melbourne, 2018, pp. 2204-2213.  
[5] T. Brown et al., "Language models are few-shot learners," in Proc. NeurIPS, Online, 2020, pp. 1877-1901.  
[6] M. Shuster et al., "Retrieval augmentation reduces hallucination in conversation," in Proc. EMNLP, Punta Cana, 2021, pp. 3784-3803.  
[7] E. Dinan et al., "The second conversational intelligence challenge (convai2)," arXiv preprint arXiv:1902.00098, 2019.  
[8] K. Lee et al., "Persona-aware dialogue generation with memory networks," in Proc. AAAI, New York, 2020, pp. 8450-8457.  
[9] L. Zheng et al., "Handling persona drift in long-term conversation," in Proc. ACL, Dublin, 2022, pp. 556-568.

# Dynamic Spatial-Channel Routing: Adaptive Sparse Mixture of Experts for Vision Transformers

**Authors:**
Elena V. Sterling, Institute for Advanced Machine Learning, Zurich; 
Marcus J. Thorne, Department of Computer Science, MIT; 
Aria K. Varma, Google DeepMind, London.

---

### Abstract
Vision Transformers (ViTs) have achieved state-of-the-art performance across computer vision benchmarks; however, their computational cost scales quadratically with input resolution, posing significant challenges for deployment on resource-constrained devices. Sparse Mixture of Experts (MoE) offers a promising avenue for conditional computation, yet traditional token-level routing often suffers from load imbalance and semantic fragmentation. In this paper, we propose **Dynamic Spatial-Channel Routing (DSCR)**, a novel MoE architecture for ViTs that decouples routing into spatial importance estimation and channel-wise expert selection. By employing a dual-gate mechanism, DSCR maintains high representational capacity while enforcing load-balanced sparsity. We provide theoretical bounds on the routing variance and demonstrate that our approach achieves competitive accuracy on ImageNet-1K with a 40% reduction in FLOPs compared to dense baselines. Empirical results across multiple scales confirm that DSCR mitigates the "expert-collapse" phenomenon, ensuring robust performance in diverse vision tasks.

---

### Keywords
Mixture of Experts, Vision Transformers, Conditional Computation, Sparse Routing, Model Compression.

---

### 1. Introduction
The emergence of Vision Transformers (ViTs) has fundamentally shifted the paradigm of image representation learning [1]. Despite their superiority, the computational density required for high-resolution inference remains a bottleneck for real-time applications [2]. Conditional computation, specifically through Mixture of Experts (MoE), has been introduced to decouple parameter count from inference latency [3]. However, current MoE implementations in ViTs typically rely on token-level gating, which fails to capture the hierarchical nature of visual features [4].

In this work, we argue that standard routing mechanisms in ViTs suffer from two primary limitations: spatial redundancy and channel-wise semantic entanglement. When all experts operate on the same feature vector, the specialization of individual experts is hindered by the high-dimensional noise inherent in raw token embeddings [5]. Furthermore, static load balancing often leads to under-utilization of experts, reducing the effective model capacity [6].

We introduce Dynamic Spatial-Channel Routing (DSCR), a framework that bifurcates the routing process. First, a spatial gate identifies relevant patches, filtering out background noise. Second, a channel-wise router assigns these patches to specific experts based on feature-space projections. This approach ensures that experts specialize in distinct spatial textures and channel-wise patterns. We hypothesize that by enforcing sparsity at both spatial and channel levels, we can achieve a more stable training dynamic and superior inference efficiency.

---

### 2. Related Work
Vision Transformers have been extensively studied since the seminal work of Dosovitskiy et al. [1]. To improve efficiency, architectures such as Swin Transformer [7] and PVT [8] introduced hierarchical structures. However, these models remain dense. 

Sparse MoE methods were pioneered in NLP, notably by Shazeer et al. [9] and later refined in the Switch Transformer [10]. Applying these to vision, the V-MoE [11] demonstrated that token-based sparsity could scale effectively. Recent variants, such as Soft-MoE [12] and MoE-ViT [13], have attempted to address the instability of top-k routing. 

Beyond routing, model distillation and pruning have been used to compress ViTs [14], [15]. Knowledge distillation [16] has shown promise in transferring information from teacher models to sparse student networks. Nevertheless, these methods often require significant pre-training time. Our work distinguishes itself by integrating routing directly into the transformer block, allowing for end-to-end optimization of both the gating mechanism and the expert parameters.

---

### 3. Background and Preliminaries
A standard Transformer block consists of Multi-Head Self-Attention (MSA) and a Feed-Forward Network (FFN). For a sequence of tokens $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times D}$, the FFN is defined as:
$FFN(X) = \sigma(XW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$ (1)
where $W_1, W_2$ are weight matrices. In an MoE-FFN, the layer is replaced by a set of $E$ experts $\{F_e\}_{e=1}^E$:
$MoE(X) = \sum_{e=1}^E G(X)_e F_e(X)$ (2)
where $G(X)$ is a gating function, typically a softmax over a linear projection $G(X) = \text{softmax}(X W_g)$. The challenge lies in the objective function to minimize the routing loss $L_{load}$, ensuring $G(X)$ does not collapse to a single expert.

---

### 4. Methodology: Dynamic Spatial-Channel Routing (DSCR)
The DSCR architecture modifies the standard MoE layer by introducing two distinct routing phases:

**4.1 Spatial Gating ($S_g$):**
We apply a lightweight depth-wise convolutional gate to the input tokens to generate a spatial importance map $M \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 1}$. Tokens with $M_i < \tau$ are assigned to a shared "background expert," reducing redundant computation on non-salient regions.

**4.2 Channel-wise Routing ($C_g$):**
For salient tokens, we compute a channel-wise routing decision. Given token $x_i$, the router produces $E$ probability scores. We utilize a Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization to ensure gradient flow during training:
$P_{i,e} = \frac{\exp((\log(\pi_{i,e}) + g_e)/\tau)}{\sum \exp((\log(\pi_{i,j}) + g_j)/\tau)}$ (3)
where $g$ is Gumbel noise. This forces the network to assign specific channel sub-spaces to experts, encouraging feature-wise specialization.

**4.3 Computational Complexity:**
The complexity of DSCR is $O(N \cdot D + E \cdot D_{exp})$, where $D_{exp}$ is the dimension of the expert. Since only a fraction of tokens are processed by multiple experts, the effective FLOPs are significantly lower than standard ViT-MoEs.

---

### 5. Theoretical Analysis
We define the routing entropy $H(G)$ as the measure of expert utilization. 
**Claim 1:** *Minimizing the routing variance $\text{Var}(G)$ leads to a more uniform load distribution.*
Proof sketch: Given the load balancing constraint $L_B = \sum \text{Var}(G_e)$, the optimal distribution occurs when $\nabla L_B = 0$, which requires equal expected value for all expert activations. By introducing the spatial filter, we reduce the total variance by limiting the input space, effectively stabilizing the gradient of the gating network.

---

### 6. Experiments
**Datasets:** We evaluate on ImageNet-1K (1.2M images) and CIFAR-100.
**Baselines:** ViT-Base [1], V-MoE [11], Swin-B [7], Soft-MoE [12], and Dense-FFN.
**Metrics:** Top-1 Accuracy, FLOPs, and Inference Latency (ms).

| Model | Params (M) | FLOPs (G) | Top-1 Acc |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ViT-Base | 86 | 17.5 | 81.2 |
| V-MoE | 150 | 12.0 | 82.5 |
| Soft-MoE | 145 | 11.5 | 82.8 |
| **DSCR (Ours)** | 148 | 10.2 | 83.1 |

---

### 7. Ablation Study
| Component | Top-1 Acc | Latency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Baseline (No gating) | 81.2 | 18.5ms |
| Spatial Gate Only | 82.0 | 14.2ms |
| Channel Gate Only | 82.4 | 13.8ms |
| **Full DSCR** | 83.1 | 12.5ms |

---

### 8. Discussion
The experimental results demonstrate that DSCR consistently outperforms token-level MoE baselines. The spatial gate effectively discards background tokens, allowing the model to focus capacity on informative foreground features. We observed that the channel-wise routing prevents expert collapse, as experts are forced to specialize in different feature subspaces.

However, the overhead of the dual-gate mechanism is non-trivial. While FLOPs are reduced, memory access patterns in modern GPUs may not perfectly align with the sparse nature of DSCR. Future work should focus on kernel-level optimizations to better exploit the sparsity.

Despite the gains, we note that on very small datasets, the increased parameter count of the MoE experts can lead to overfitting. We recommend aggressive regularization for smaller-scale applications.

---

### 9. Limitations and Future Work
DSCR assumes a fixed number of experts, which may not be optimal for all input distributions. Future iterations will explore dynamic expert allocation where the number of active experts varies based on image complexity. 

Additionally, we currently ignore the temporal dimension. Extending DSCR to Video Transformers would be a logical next step to handle frame-level redundancy. Finally, quantifying the energy savings on edge hardware remains a critical task for future deployment studies.

---

### 10. Conclusion
We have presented DSCR, a novel sparse routing mechanism for ViTs. By decoupling spatial and channel routing, we achieve a superior trade-off between model capacity and computational efficiency. Our theoretical and empirical analyses confirm that DSCR mitigates standard MoE instabilities, providing a robust pathway for scaling vision models.

---

### 11. References
[1] A. Dosovitskiy et al., "An image is worth 16x16 words," in Proc. ICLR, 2021.
[2] H. Touvron et al., "Training data-efficient image transformers," in Proc. ICML, 2021.
[3] R. Jacobs et al., "Adaptive mixtures of local experts," Neural Comp., 1991.
[4] J. Devlin et al., "BERT: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers," in Proc. NAACL, 2019.
[5] K. He et al., "Deep residual learning for image recognition," in Proc. CVPR, 2016.
[6] B. Zoph et al., "Learning transferable architectures for scalable image recognition," in Proc. CVPR, 2018.
[7] Z. Liu et al., "Swin transformer: Hierarchical vision transformer," in Proc. ICCV, 2021.
[8] W. Wang et al., "Pyramid vision transformer," in Proc. ICCV, 2021.
[9] N. Shazeer et al., "Outrageously large neural networks," in Proc. ICLR, 2017.
[10] W. Fedus et al., "Switch transformer: Scaling to trillion parameter models," JMLR, 2022.
[11] A. Riquelme et al., "Vision transformer with mixture-of-experts," in Proc. NeurIPS, 2021.
[12] R. M. H. G. et al., "Soft-MoE: Differentiable routing," in Proc. ICCV, 2023.
[13] S. J. Lee et al., "MoE-ViT: Sparsity in vision," arXiv preprint, 2023.
[14] P. Molchanov et al., "Importance estimation for neural network pruning," in Proc. ICLR, 2019.
[15] T. Chen et al., "Lottery ticket hypothesis," in Proc. ICLR, 2019.
[16] G. Hinton et al., "Distilling the knowledge in a neural network," in Proc. NIPS, 2015.
[17] Y. LeCun et al., "Gradient-based learning applied to document recognition," Proc. IEEE, 1998.
[18] S. Xie et al., "Aggregated residual transformations," in Proc. CVPR, 2017.
[19] M. Tan et al., "EfficientNet: Rethinking model scaling," in Proc. ICML, 2019.
[20] I. Loshchilov et al., "Decoupled weight decay regularization," in Proc. ICLR, 2019.
[21] D. Kingma et al., "Adam: A method for stochastic optimization," in Proc. ICLR, 2015.
[22] A. Vaswani et al., "Attention is all you need," in Proc. NIPS, 2017.
[23] T. T. Lin et al., "Feature pyramid networks," in Proc. CVPR, 2017.
[24] J. Redmon et al., "You only look once," in Proc. CVPR, 2016.
[25] K. Simonyan et al., "Very deep convolutional networks," in Proc. ICLR, 2015.

# Geometric-Semantic Alignment: A Layout-Aware Multimodal Transformer for Hierarchical Document Information Extraction

**Authors:**
*   **Elena V. Thorne**, Department of Computer Science, Stanford University
*   **Marcus J. Sterling**, AI Research Lab, Google DeepMind
*   **Aiko Tanaka**, Institute for Intelligent Systems, University of Tokyo

### Abstract
Document Information Extraction (DIE) remains a significant challenge due to the complex interplay between textual content and visual layout geometry. Existing multimodal approaches often treat spatial information as an auxiliary feature, failing to explicitly model the relational structure of heterogeneous document elements. We propose **Geo-SemAlign**, a novel transformer architecture that integrates layout-aware self-attention with a global geometric consistency loss. By employing a dynamic graph-based coordinate embedding, our model facilitates explicit cross-modal reasoning across text tokens, spatial bounding boxes, and tabular structures. We theoretically analyze the convergence properties of the spatial-attention mechanism and demonstrate that our geometric alignment strategy reduces error propagation in complex layouts. Extensive experiments on the FUNSD and CORD datasets reveal that Geo-SemAlign achieves competitive performance, outperforming current state-of-the-art models by an average of 1.8% F1-score while maintaining superior inference efficiency. 

**Keywords:** Multimodal Transformers, Document Understanding, Geometric Alignment, Information Extraction, Spatial Representation.

---

### Introduction
The digitization of physical documents—ranging from invoices and receipts to academic papers and technical manuals—necessitates sophisticated machine learning models capable of cross-modal reasoning. Document Information Extraction (DIE) involves identifying entities and relations in unstructured or semi-structured visual formats [1]. While Transformer-based architectures have revolutionized NLP, their application to DIE is hindered by the disconnect between textual semantics and spatial configurations [2].

Traditional approaches rely on either sequential text processing or visual-only detection paradigms [3]. Recent multimodal frameworks have attempted to bridge this gap by injecting spatial embeddings into attention blocks [4]. However, these methods often suffer from the "coordinate-drift" problem, where positional embeddings fail to capture the hierarchical nested structures common in complex tables and forms. Furthermore, the lack of geometric invariance prevents these models from generalizing across varying image resolutions and scanning distortions.

In this work, we introduce **Geo-SemAlign**, a novel framework designed to enforce semantic-geometric alignment during the pre-training phase. By treating a document as a multimodal graph where nodes represent textual clusters and edges represent spatial dependencies, we leverage a dual-stream attention mechanism that simultaneously optimizes for semantic relevance and topological validity. We provide a theoretical analysis demonstrating that our proposed objective function enforces a tighter lower bound on the entropy of spatial representation. Our results demonstrate that Geo-SemAlign consistently outperforms existing baselines, particularly in long-context documents requiring high spatial precision.

---

### Related Work
The literature on document understanding is primarily categorized into two paradigms: pixel-level visual extraction and token-level semantic extraction. Early methods such as CNN-based OCR engines relied on template-matching heuristics, which were fragile to document variance [5]. The advent of LayoutLM shifted the focus toward language-model pre-training with auxiliary spatial supervision [6].

Subsequent iterations, such as LayoutLMv2 and v3, introduced multimodal fusion modules that concatenate visual features with text embeddings [7]. While effective, these models treat spatial information as a static attribute, ignoring the relational semantics inherent in document flow [8]. Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) were later proposed to explicitly model page layouts as adjacency matrices, though they often struggle with scaling to large documents due to cubic computational complexity [9]. Recent advancements in cross-attention mechanisms have attempted to unify these streams, yet they frequently overlook the importance of rotational and scale-invariant geometric descriptors [10]. 

Our approach distinguishes itself by implementing a dynamic geometric loss, which allows the model to learn representations that are robust to variations in document orientation, a constraint that remains poorly addressed in standard DIE benchmarks [11].

---

### Background and Preliminaries
Let a document be represented as a tuple $D = (\mathcal{T}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{I})$, where $\mathcal{T} = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n\}$ is the set of tokens, $\mathcal{B} = \{b_1, b_2, \dots, b_n\}$ are the associated normalized bounding boxes $(x_0, y_0, x_1, y_1)$, and $\mathcal{I}$ is the image representation. 

The standard Transformer encoder maps these inputs into hidden representations $H \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$. We define our layout-aware attention weight $\alpha_{ij}$ between tokens $i$ and $j$ as:
\begin{equation}
\alpha_{ij} = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{Q_i K_j^T}{\sqrt{d_k}} + \psi(b_i, b_j)\right)
\end{equation}
where $\psi(b_i, b_j)$ is a bias term derived from the Euclidean distance and the relative overlap coefficient of the bounding boxes.

We assume the existence of a latent space $\mathcal{Z}$ such that the geometric configuration $B$ and semantic content $T$ are conditionally independent given a latent layout variable $L$. The objective is to minimize the mutual information between the error in predicted spatial coordinates and the semantic classification cross-entropy:
\begin{equation}
\min_{\theta} \mathcal{L}_{Total} = \mathcal{L}_{CE}(T, \hat{T}) + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{Geo}(B, \hat{B})
\end{equation}
where $\mathcal{L}_{Geo}$ penalizes structural inconsistencies in the predicted graph.

---

### Proposed Approach
Geo-SemAlign utilizes a tripartite architecture: (1) a Vision Encoder, (2) a Layout-Aware Text Transformer, and (3) a Geometric Fusion Head. Unlike prior models, we utilize a **Relative Coordinate Encoding (RCE)** mechanism that normalizes distances based on page density rather than absolute pixel coordinates. 

The RCE function is defined as:
\begin{equation}
\rho(b_i, b_j) = \log\left(\frac{\|c_i - c_j\|_2 + \epsilon}{\sigma_{local}}\right)
\end{equation}
where $c_i$ is the centroid of $b_i$, and $\sigma_{local}$ represents the local average bounding box size, providing scale invariance.

**Algorithm 1: Geo-SemAlign Training Loop**
1.  **Input:** Doc image $I$, text $T$, and boxes $B$.
2.  **Extract:** Features $V = \text{CNN}(I)$.
3.  **Embed:** $X = \text{Embed}(T) + \text{PosEnc}(B)$.
4.  **Forward:** Compute Transformer layers with RCE-bias.
5.  **Alignment:** Compute Geometric Loss $\mathcal{L}_{Geo}$ via structural graph matching.
6.  **Backpropagate:** $\nabla_\theta (\mathcal{L}_{CE} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{Geo})$.

Computational complexity of the attention mechanism is reduced from $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ to $O(n \log n \cdot d)$ by restricting spatial attention to a sliding-window k-nearest-neighbor set based on the RCE distances.

---

### Theoretical Analysis
**Theorem 1:** *Given a document with $N$ tokens, the RCE mechanism provides a bound on the variance of the attention distribution relative to the document topology.*

*Proof:* Let the spatial attention weights satisfy $\sum_{j} \exp(\psi_{ij}) = 1$. Under the Lipschitz continuity of the RCE function $\rho$, the sensitivity of the attention output $\alpha_{ij}$ to perturbations in bounding box coordinates is bounded by $L_\rho \cdot ||\Delta B||$. Since $\rho$ employs a normalized distance metric, the spectral radius of the attention matrix remains bounded as $N \to \infty$, ensuring stability during training. 

This implies that our model is less prone to vanishing gradients when encountering sparse document layouts where tokens are separated by large white space, effectively improving convergence speed by a factor of 1.4x compared to vanilla attention [12].

---

### Experiments

#### Datasets
We evaluate on **FUNSD** (Form Understanding in Noisy Scanned Documents) [13] and **CORD** (Consolidated Receipt Dataset) [14]. FUNSD provides 199 annotated forms with complex tabular structures, while CORD contains 800 receipts with varying receipt formats.

#### Baselines
We compare against:
1.  **LayoutLMv1** [6]: Standard transformer baseline.
2.  **LayoutLMv2** [7]: Adds visual integration.
3.  **DocFormer** [15]: Uses multi-modal coordinate embeddings.
4.  **BROS** [16]: Bidirectionally-rotated spatial attention.
5.  **Pick-Net** [17]: Graph-based extraction.

#### Metrics
We utilize Entity-Level F1-Score and Average Intersection-over-Union (IoU) for bounding box prediction.

**Table 1: Performance Comparison on FUNSD Dataset**
| Model | F1-Score | IoU Accuracy | Latency (ms) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| LayoutLMv1 | 78.2% | 81.5% | 45 |
| LayoutLMv2 | 84.1% | 85.9% | 62 |
| DocFormer | 85.4% | 86.2% | 70 |
| BROS | 83.9% | 85.1% | 55 |
| Pick-Net | 82.5% | 84.3% | 88 |
| **Geo-SemAlign** | **87.2%** | **88.4%** | **52** |

---

### Ablation Study
We evaluate the contribution of the RCE and the geometric consistency loss ($\mathcal{L}_{Geo}$).

**Table 2: Ablation Study (FUNSD)**
| Configuration | F1-Score | $\Delta$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Full Geo-SemAlign | 87.2% | - |
| w/o RCE | 83.1% | -4.1% |
| w/o $\mathcal{L}_{Geo}$ | 84.5% | -2.7% |
| w/o Vision Stream | 79.8% | -7.4% |

The results indicate that the RCE mechanism is the primary driver of performance, contributing significantly to handling long-range spatial dependencies.

---

### Discussion and Insights
The performance gains observed in Table 1 suggest that explicit geometric alignment acts as a regularizer, preventing the model from overfitting to specific textual patterns (e.g., "Total:" or "Date:") that are prone to hallucination in noisy OCR output. By enforcing structural graph matching, Geo-SemAlign learns that spatial relationships—such as the proximity of a key to its corresponding value—are invariant across different template styles.

Furthermore, the latency metrics highlight a critical efficiency benefit. While models like DocFormer require high-dimensional multimodal projection layers that bloat the parameter count, our RCE approach uses a memory-efficient sparse attention mechanism. This allows Geo-SemAlign to be deployed in edge environments with limited GPU memory, a necessity for real-time document processing pipelines [18].

We also observed that Geo-SemAlign performs exceptionally well on "nested tables," a notorious failure mode for Transformer-based architectures [19]. Because the geometric loss forces the attention heads to prioritize local topological neighbors, the model maintains coherence even when textual sequences are interrupted by tabular cell boundaries [20].

---

### Limitations and Future Work
Despite these improvements, Geo-SemAlign exhibits limitations when processing handwritten documents with significant ink bleed or non-linear handwriting trajectories. The current geometric assumption relies on bounding box inputs provided by an external OCR engine; errors in the OCR stage propagate directly into our model's coordinate embeddings [21]. 

Future research will focus on end-to-end integration where the spatial extraction is performed via a differentiable differentiable rendering module, removing the reliance on pre-computed boxes [22]. Additionally, we intend to expand the geometric loss function to incorporate hierarchical tree structures, which would better model multi-level document sections like nested list items in legal contracts [23].

Scaling the model to larger, multi-page document corpuses remains a non-trivial challenge [24]. Current self-attention is limited by window sizes; applying linear-attention techniques or state-space models (SSMs) to the multimodal stream could provide the necessary scalability for books or technical manuals [25].

---

### Conclusion
We have presented Geo-SemAlign, a novel multimodal Transformer architecture for document information extraction that prioritizes geometric-semantic alignment. By introducing the Relative Coordinate Encoding (RCE) and a structural geometric loss, we addressed the fundamental disconnect between document layout and textual meaning. Empirical results on benchmark datasets confirm that our method improves extraction accuracy while remaining computationally efficient. We believe that this framework establishes a new foundation for document understanding, providing a robust mechanism for models to interpret the complex structural relationships inherent in modern digital documents.

---

### References
[1] H. Zhang et al., "Deep document understanding: A survey," IEEE Trans. Pattern Anal. Mach. Intell., vol. 43, no. 5, pp. 1200-1215, 2021.
[2] J. Li and M. Wang, "Attention mechanisms in multimodal learning," in Proc. NeurIPS, Vancouver, 2020, pp. 450-462.
[3] A. Smith, "Visual-only document processing," in Proc. CVPR, Seattle, 2019, pp. 112-125.
[4] B. Doe, "Geometric embeddings for transformers," in Proc. ACL, Bangkok, 2022, pp. 300-315.
[5] C. Chen, "Template matching for OCR," J. Doc. Proc., vol. 12, no. 1, pp. 45-58, 2015.
[6] Y. Xu et al., "LayoutLM: Pre-training of text and layout for document image understanding," in Proc. KDD, 2020, pp. 1192-1200.
[7] Y. Xu et al., "LayoutLMv2: Multi-modal pre-training for visually-rich document understanding," in Proc. ACL, 2021, pp. 257-266.
[8] R. Miller, "Relational semantics in documents," in Proc. ICML, 2021, pp. 780-795.
[9] T. Kim, "Graph neural networks for document layout," J. AI Res., vol. 68, pp. 102-120, 2020.
[10] S. Gupta, "Scale-invariant vision models," in Proc. ICCV, 2022, pp. 44-55.
[11] P. K. Singh, "Robustness to rotation in document analysis," in Proc. WACV, 2023, pp. 200-210.
[12] L. D. V. G. (Fictional Author), "Convergence rates of attention," arXiv preprint arXiv:2301.00001, 2023.
[13] G. Jaume et al., "FUNSD: A dataset for form understanding," in Proc. ICDAR, 2019, pp. 1-6.
[14] J. Park et al., "CORD: A receipt dataset," in Proc. CVPR Workshops, 2019, pp. 91-99.
[15] D. Davis, "DocFormer: End-to-end transformers," in Proc. NeurIPS, 2021, pp. 1123-1135.
[16] M. Lee, "BROS: Bi-directionally rotated spatial attention," in Proc. ACL, 2022, pp. 444-458.
[17] X. Zhao, "Pick-Net: Graph-based IE," in Proc. AAAI, 2020, pp. 889-901.
[18] R. Wilson, "Edge-based document processing," IEEE Embedded Syst. Lett., vol. 14, no. 2, pp. 50-55, 2022.
[19] F. Zhang, "Failures of transformers in tabular data," in Proc. EMNLP, 2021, pp. 333-345.
[20] H. Liu, "Topological constraints in self-attention," in Proc. ICLR, 2022, pp. 560-575.
[21] G. K. P. (Fictional Author), "OCR error propagation in IE," J. Comput. Vis., vol. 30, no. 4, pp. 400-415, 2022.

[23] E. Brown, "Hierarchical structural learning," in Proc. COLING, 2021, pp. 222-235.

# Latent-Relation Contrastive Learning for Robust Adverse Drug Event Extraction in Clinical Narratives

**Authors:**  
Dr. Elena V. Thorne (Department of Biomedical Informatics, Stanford University),  
Dr. Marcus J. Sterling (Institute for Computational Medicine, Johns Hopkins University),  
Dr. Sarah P. Chen (AI Research Lab, Mayo Clinic)

---

### Abstract
The automated extraction of Adverse Drug Events (ADEs) from electronic health records (EHRs) remains a critical challenge due to the high linguistic variability and sparsity of clinical narratives. Existing Transformer-based models often struggle with long-range dependencies and the subtle semantic nuances inherent in causal clinical relations. In this paper, we propose *ClinRel-CL*, a novel architecture that integrates a latent-relation contrastive learning objective with a hierarchical graph-constrained Transformer. By explicitly modeling the interaction between drug entities and their associated physiological manifestations through a latent relational space, our approach significantly reduces false-positive causal assignments. We evaluate our method on the N2C2 and ADE-Corpus-V2 datasets, demonstrating a 4.2% improvement in F1-score over state-of-the-art baselines. We further provide a theoretical analysis of the contrastive loss function’s convergence properties and conduct an extensive ablation study to validate the efficacy of our proposed architectural components.

**Keywords:** Clinical NLP, Adverse Drug Events, Contrastive Learning, Transformer Architectures, Relation Extraction.

---

### I. Introduction
Adverse Drug Events (ADEs) represent a substantial burden on public health systems, contributing significantly to patient morbidity and mortality [1]. Clinical narratives embedded within Electronic Health Records (EHRs) serve as the primary repository for this information; however, extracting these relations is fraught with complexity. Clinical language is characterized by high levels of syntactic ambiguity, ellipsis, and domain-specific terminology that frequently renders traditional Information Extraction (IE) pipelines inadequate [2].

Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) and Transformer-based architectures have shifted the paradigm toward end-to-end extraction models [3]. Despite these gains, current models primarily focus on token-level classification, often neglecting the underlying causal structure of clinical interactions. Specifically, the challenge lies in the "spurious correlation" problem, where a model incorrectly identifies a drug-symptom pair based on proximity rather than true clinical causality [4]. 

To address these limitations, we introduce *ClinRel-CL*, a framework designed to learn distinct representations of causal relations in a latent hypersphere. Unlike traditional supervised learning, our approach leverages a contrastive objective that pulls together positive causal triples while pushing apart negative non-causal associations. This paper contributes: (1) a hierarchical graph-constrained Transformer to capture long-distance clinical context; (2) a novel latent-relation contrastive loss function; and (3) an extensive experimental evaluation demonstrating competitive performance on benchmark clinical datasets.

---

### II. Related Work
The extraction of relations from clinical text has evolved from rule-based systems to deep learning frameworks. Early efforts relied on syntactic patterns and manually curated knowledge bases like UMLS [5]. However, these methods lacked generalization capabilities across diverse clinical settings.

The advent of clinical-specific embeddings, such as BioBERT [6] and ClinicalBERT [7], revolutionized the field by enabling models to internalize the domain’s lexical distribution. Subsequent works introduced multi-task learning frameworks to jointly perform Named Entity Recognition (NER) and Relation Extraction (RE) [8]. Despite these successes, standard Transformer models often fail to capture the long-range dependencies between a drug and a delayed side effect appearing in a separate paragraph [9].

Contrastive learning has recently emerged as a powerful tool for representation learning in NLP [10]. By mapping inputs into a space where semantically similar items are proximal, models achieve higher robustness against noise [11]. In clinical contexts, contrastive frameworks have been applied to patient phenotyping and document retrieval [12], yet their application to specific, causal ADE relation extraction remains under-explored. Our work bridges this gap by proposing a relation-specific contrastive objective.

---

### III. Background
Let $D$ be a clinical document represented as a sequence of tokens $T = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n\}$. We define an ADE relation as a triple $r = (e_d, e_s, \tau)$, where $e_d$ denotes a drug entity, $e_s$ denotes a symptom entity, and $\tau \in \{0, 1\}$ is a binary indicator of a causal relation.

The Transformer backbone computes hidden representations $H = \{h_1, \dots, h_n\}$ through stacked self-attention layers:
$$Attention(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V \quad (1)$$
where $Q, K, V$ are the query, key, and value matrices respectively. In our framework, we augment these representations with a graph-constrained adjacency matrix $A$ to incorporate clinical knowledge, resulting in a modified attention score $\hat{A} = \text{softmax}(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M)$, where $M$ is a relational mask derived from clinical ontologies [13].

---

### IV. Methodology
#### A. The ClinRel-CL Architecture
The proposed architecture comprises three modules: (1) A Clinical-Transformer encoder, (2) A Relation-Aware Graph Layer, and (3) A Latent Contrastive Head. 

The Relation-Aware Graph Layer computes node embeddings by aggregating information from neighboring entities within the document. We define the propagation rule for entity $i$ as:
$$z_i^{(l+1)} = \sigma \left( \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} \alpha_{ij} W^{(l)} z_j^{(l)} \right) \quad (2)$$
where $\alpha_{ij}$ is the attention weight and $\mathcal{N}(i)$ is the set of neighbors identified as potential clinical correlates.

#### B. Latent-Relation Contrastive Learning
To optimize the model, we define a loss function $\mathcal{L}$ that combines cross-entropy $\mathcal{L}_{CE}$ with our novel contrastive loss $\mathcal{L}_{CL}$:
$$\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{CE} + \lambda \sum_{i \in B} \left[ -\log \frac{\exp(sim(r_i, p_i)/\tau)}{\sum_{j \in B} \exp(sim(r_i, n_j)/\tau)} \right] \quad (3)$$
where $sim$ is the cosine similarity, $p_i$ is a positive sample (causal pair), $n_j$ is a negative sample, and $\tau$ is a temperature hyperparameter. 

#### C. Algorithmic Complexity
The computational complexity of the model is dominated by the Transformer self-attention, $O(n^2 \cdot d)$, and the graph-constrained layer, $O(V + E)$ where $V$ and $E$ are nodes and edges in the clinical graph. The overall complexity is manageable, scaling linearly with document length for sparse graphs, providing a significant advantage over dense graph convolutional networks [14].

---

### V. Theoretical Analysis
*Claim:* The inclusion of the contrastive loss $\mathcal{L}_{CL}$ reduces the variance of the gradient estimator during training on sparse ADE occurrences.

*Justification:* By forcing the latent space to cluster causal pairs, the model effectively performs a hard-negative mining process. Given the assumption that clinical causal relations exhibit high semantic density in vector space, minimizing the contrastive loss acts as a regularizer, preventing the model from overfitting to superficial word co-occurrences. We define the convergence bound of the contrastive objective as $O(1/\sqrt{N_{pos}})$, where $N_{pos}$ is the number of causal samples, suggesting that the model remains stable even in data-scarce scenarios [15].

---

### VI. Experiments
#### A. Datasets
We evaluate on the N2C2 2018 ADE extraction task and the ADE-Corpus-V2 [16]. These datasets contain clinical notes labeled with drug, dosage, route, frequency, and adverse reaction entities.

#### B. Setup and Baselines
We compare *ClinRel-CL* against:
1.  **Baseline-1 (CNN):** Standard word-level convolutional network [17].
2.  **Baseline-2 (BiLSTM-CRF):** Traditional sequence labeling architecture [18].
3.  **Baseline-3 (BERT-Base):** Vanilla BERT tuned for clinical tokens [19].
4.  **Baseline-4 (BioBERT):** Domain-adapted BERT [6].
5.  **Baseline-5 (Clinical-Longformer):** Optimized for long documents [20].

#### C. Results
Table 1 shows the performance comparison across datasets.

| Model | N2C2 F1 (Micro) | ADE-Corpus F1 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CNN [17] | 0.62 | 0.58 |
| BiLSTM-CRF [18] | 0.68 | 0.64 |
| BERT-Base [19] | 0.74 | 0.71 |
| BioBERT [6] | 0.78 | 0.75 |
| Longformer [20] | 0.79 | 0.77 |
| **ClinRel-CL (Ours)** | **0.83** | **0.80** |

---

### VII. Ablation Study
To assess individual contribution, we systematically remove components.

| Configuration | F1-Score |
| :--- | :--- |
| Full Model | 0.83 |
| w/o Graph-Constrained Layer | 0.79 |
| w/o Contrastive Objective | 0.76 |
| w/o Latent Projection | 0.75 |

The results indicate that the latent contrastive loss is the primary driver of performance, contributing roughly 7% of the total F1 gain.

---

### VIII. Discussion
The experimental results demonstrate that *ClinRel-CL* consistently outperforms current state-of-the-art models. The success of the latent-relation contrastive objective suggests that clinical entities are better represented when the model is forced to distinguish between causal and incidental co-occurrences. Unlike BERT, which relies solely on local token context, our graph-constrained mechanism ensures that the model respects the hierarchical nature of clinical documents.

A notable observation is the model's resilience to "negative-heavy" clinical notes. In clinical settings, most drug mentions are not followed by ADEs. Standard models typically yield high false-positive rates in these documents. Our model’s ability to map these "non-causal" mentions to a distinct, separate region of the latent space effectively mitigates this error-prone behavior [21].

However, the gains in performance are slightly offset by the increased memory requirements during the contrastive training phase. The necessity of maintaining a large memory bank of negative samples introduces a $O(K)$ space complexity overhead, which must be considered in deployment scenarios with constrained hardware.

---

### IX. Limitations & Future Work
While effective, the model exhibits limitations in handling rare drug-symptom pairings where the semantic signal is insufficient for the contrastive head to converge. Furthermore, the model assumes high-quality entity annotation, which is often expensive to procure in clinical settings. Future work will investigate self-supervised pre-training on unlabeled EHR data to reduce dependency on gold-standard labels.

Additionally, the current graph construction relies on external ontologies. Integrating a learned, end-to-end graph structure that discovers relations without manual pre-processing would make the model more adaptable to emerging clinical terms or off-label drug usage.

Finally, we intend to evaluate the model's performance on cross-lingual clinical data, as clinical practices and terminology vary significantly across global health systems. Extending the contrastive objective to handle multi-lingual alignment may yield significant improvements in global health informatics [22].

---

### X. Conclusion
This paper presented *ClinRel-CL*, a novel framework for ADE extraction that integrates hierarchical graph-based attention with a latent-relation contrastive learning objective. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets while providing a more robust representation of clinical causality. We believe this work provides a scalable path forward for clinical NLP, particularly in environments where high-precision extraction is paramount for patient safety.

---

### References
[1] J. Smith, "Adverse drug events in modern healthcare," *J. Med. Informatics*, vol. 42, pp. 101-115, 2021.  
[2] R. Lee and T. Wang, "Challenges in clinical NLP," in *Proc. ACL*, 2020, pp. 200-210.  
[3] A. Vaswani et al., "Attention is all you need," in *Proc. NeurIPS*, 2017, pp. 5998-6008.  
[4] B. Doe, "Spurious correlations in medical IE," *Nature Machine Intelligence*, vol. 3, pp. 45-52, 2022.  
[5] K. Miller, "UMLS and its role in clinical text mining," *JAMIA*, vol. 25, pp. 88-94, 2018.  
[6] J. Lee et al., "BioBERT: A pre-trained biomedical language representation," *Bioinformatics*, vol. 36, pp. 1234-1240, 2020.  
[7] E. Alsentzer et al., "Publicly available clinical BERT embeddings," in *Proc. BioNLP*, 2019, pp. 72-78.  
[8] S. Zhang et al., "Joint extraction of entities and relations," in *Proc. EMNLP*, 2021, pp. 334-345.  
[9] F. Gupta, "Long-range dependencies in EHRs," *J. Biomed. Data*, vol. 12, pp. 210-225, 2022.  
[10] T. Chen et al., "A simple framework for contrastive learning," in *Proc. ICML*, 2020, pp. 1597-1607.  
[11] H. Wang, "Contrastive learning in NLP," *arXiv preprint arXiv:2104.08812*, 2021.  
[12] M. Patel, "Patient phenotyping with contrastive methods," in *Proc. MLHC*, 2022, pp. 12-25.  
[13] P. Zhou et al., "Graph-constrained attention for clinical text," in *Proc. ACL*, 2022, pp. 445-456.  
[14] T. Kipf and M. Welling, "Semi-supervised classification with GCNs," in *Proc. ICLR*, 2017, pp. 1-14.  
[15] R. Rao, "Convergence bounds for contrastive objectives," *J. Machine Learning Research*, vol. 23, pp. 1-30, 2022.  
[16] H. Gurulingappa et al., "Development of a benchmark for ADEs," *J. Biomed. Informatics*, vol. 45, pp. 546-552, 2012.  
[17] Y. Kim, "CNNs for sentence classification," in *Proc. EMNLP*, 2014, pp. 1746-1751.  
[18] Z. Huang et al., "Bi-LSTM-CRF for sequence labeling," *arXiv preprint arXiv:1508.01991*, 2015.  
[19] J. Devlin et al., "BERT: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers," in *Proc. NAACL*, 2019, pp. 4171-4186.  
[20] I. Beltagy et al., "Longformer: The long-document transformer," *arXiv preprint arXiv:2004.05150*, 2020.  
[21] A. White, "Handling negative instances in clinical IE," *Clinical NLP Review*, vol. 9, pp. 33-40, 2023.  
[22] C. Gomez, "Multi-lingual clinical NLP," in *Proc. COLING*, 2023, pp. 90-105.  
[23] M. Brown, "Advanced Transformers," *AI Press*, 2022.  
[24] S. Liu, "Optimization in deep learning," *Journal of AI Research*, vol. 70, pp. 800-820, 2021.  
[25] K. Zhao, "Clinical data sparsity," *Health Data Science*, vol. 5, pp. 112-128, 2022.
